Design/Fiat-Corde---spoken-script.title: Fiat Corde - Spoken script
Design/Fiat-Corde---spoken-script.content:
Olá.
Chamo-me Filipe,
sou formador no Ensino Profissional
da Escola Secundária de Arrifana,
nas áreas de Design
Comunicação Visual
e Marketing Digital.

Venho falar-vos da minha mobilidade Erasmus+,
do curso de Metodologias Ativas
que frequentei em Madrid
e de como o estou a aplicar
como estratégia de disseminação.

Meio segundo. Corte seco.



Goya invade o ecrã.
Silêncio. Dez segundos.


Isto não é um quadro histórico.
É uma reportagem de guerra.
Madrid, 3 de maio de 1808.
As tropas de Napoleão
executam os madrilenos
que se revoltaram na véspera.
Goya vivia lá, viu e pintou.
Antes de existir fotojornalismo, isto era o fotojornalismo.

Reparem no enquadramento:

O pelotão não tem rosto, é uma máquina.
O fuzilado tem rosto.
Braços abertos,
camisa branca,
iluminado.

Goya escolheu:

escolheu quem vemos,
quem não vemos,
de que lado sentimos.


Agora reparem noutra coisa.
Um ano depois,
na madrugada de 17 de abril de 1809,
as mesmas tropas cercaram Arrifana.
A população refugiou-se na igreja,
o que se revelou uma armadilha.
Os homens foram obrigados a sair
e um em cada cinco foi fuzilado.
Os Quintados.
Este quadro não está só em Madrid.
Também aconteceu aqui.
Só não teve pintor.

Goya fez do 3 de maio
memória universal.
O massacre de Arrifana
teve de esperar pela recriação histórica
que vimos em abril.
É essa a diferença
entre ter um repórter
e não ter.


As perguntas entram no ecrã.
Ler cada uma devagar,
com pausa entre elas.


Quem decide?
Quem paga?
Quem fica fora do frame?


Pausa longa. Avançar.



Cheguei a Madrid no domingo,
3 de maio de 2026.
Fim de semana inteiro de feriado,
que celebrava,
na véspera,
o dia da comunidade,
este quadro,
2 de maio.
a violenta revolta popular
contra as tropas de Napoleão.
Os quadros de Goya
foram um dos elementos aglutinadores
de toda a semana.
Sete dias de formação Erasmus+.

40 horas sobre metodologias ativas,
aprendizagem cooperativa,
CLIL,
Universal Design,
Inteligência artificial na educação.


No primeiro dia,
Images of Spain.
O coordenador contou a história de Espanha
e mostrou uma península
que mudou de mãos e de fés
durante séculos.
Camadas sobre camadas.
E parou aqui,
nestes quadros:
o 2 e o 3 de maio,
sem heróis nem vilões.
Goya foi a grande referência da abertura.


Na tarde seguinte,
num peddypaper pelas ruas
do centro de Madrid,
uma das perguntas levou-nos
a uma placa do Palácio Real.
O que aconteceu aqui
a 2 de maio de 1808?
O feriado que tínhamos acabado de viver
era matéria do jogo.


Na quarta-feira,
visitámos o Museu do Prado,
como parte integrante do curso.
O ponto de encontro
foi aos pés da estátua de Goya.

Das milhares de obras,
a mediadora orientou-nos
por 14 obras
curadas como um argumento,
não como uma visita.
Estes quadros de Goya estavam lá.

Esta abordagem teve um nome:
Context4Content.
O contexto não ilustra o conteúdo.
O contexto é o conteúdo.
A cidade, o museu, a rua:
tudo é sala de aula quando há uma pergunta certa.

Em junho,
quis testar isto com uma turma do 11.º ano,
para o módulo de Neuromarketing:

atenção,
emoção,
enquadramento
como é que as imagens nos fazem decidir?


Dias antes, fui ao Centro de Arte Oliva.
Preparei a visita com o Daniel:

Escolhemos o percurso,
Desenhamos as oficinas,
Estudamos as perguntas.

Dá trabalho,
mas é a diferença
entre um passeio
e uma aula.


Uma das exposições
chamava-se “Sem Terra à Vista”.
À entrada, diante da parede com o título,
a mediadora perguntou:
o que acham que isto quer dizer?
As respostas vieram:

Alto-mar
À deriva
Vazio
No espaço
Piratas
Naufrágio



Lá dentro,
esta fotografia do Augusto Alves da Silva.
Uma paisagem açoriana:

Campos verdes,
oceano azul,
algumas nuvens brancas.
Quase idílica.

A obra chama-se 3.16.


Enquanto tentavam decifrar a imagem,
a mediadora entregou
um recorte de jornal a um aluno,
que leu em voz alta:
“Primeiros-ministros
britânico,
espanhol
e português
aguardam chegada de Bush.
Tony Blair
e José Maria Aznar
juntam-se a Durão
nas Lajes.
Público,
16 de março de 2003,
16:45.”
A paisagem mudou à frente deles.


3.16
O título é uma data:
16 de março de 2003.
O dia da Cimeira das Lajes,
a última encenação
antes da guerra.


O fotojornalismo oficial
mostrou os quatro homens
alinhados com as bandeiras,
num pódio.
Os fazedores da paz.

O jornal desse dia
ainda falava em diplomacia.
Dois dias depois,
começou a invasão do Iraque.
Alves da Silva fotografou
o silêncio antes da guerra.
A mediadora lembrou-nos
que o artista não é neutro.
Quem produz uma obra fá-lo

a partir do seu próprio contexto,
com intenções,
num determinado momento,
com objetivos.

Nada ali estava por acaso.
Nada aqui está por acaso.

O próprio Alves da Silva
escreveu sobre esta série:
“o fotojornalismo não produz
uma imagem imparcial,
nunca,
porque nenhuma fotografia o é”


A obra estava numa zona da exposição
dedicada à desinformação e à desmemória.
Ao que se apaga.

Goya pintou para que não se esquecesse.

O Cerco da Buciqueira,
em Arrifana,
não teve quem o pintasse.

Esta fotografia guarda
o que o frame oficial deixou de fora.
Percorremos o resto:

guerra,
fronteiras,
direitos humanos.
O poder apresentado como pacífico.
A paz distribuída à força.


À saída,
a mediadora voltou à mesma parede.
Fez a mesma pergunta.
Sem terra à vista:
o que quer isto dizer?

Migração
Opressão
Controlo
Sem controlo
Saque
E piratas.

Outra vez piratas,
mas já não eram os mesmos piratas.
A mesma parede.
A mesma pergunta.
Uma hora de diferença.
O contexto tinha mudado tudo.


Pausa longa. Mudar ligeiramente a voz.

“Não estava à espera
que a visita fosse tão política.”

Silêncio.

“Os alunos também
não estavam.”

Deixar a frase respirar.


Tal como a mediadora não estava à espera,
provavelmente esta plateia também não.
Habituámo-nos a pedir à arte que nos entretenha
mas ela insiste em fazer o seu trabalho,
porque esta corrente é antiga.
Goya, Madrid, 1808.


No sábado à tarde,
já depois do encerramento do curso,
visitei o Reina Sofía.
Fui rever Guernica.
Picasso pintou o bombardeamento de uma cidade
a partir das fotografias dos jornais.
Um repórter, à distância.

Na sala em frente,
uma exposição com um título
que era uma tese:
History Doesn’t Repeat Itself,
but It Does Rhyme.


Dumile Feni,
artista da África do Sul,
produziu, em 1967,
“African Guernica”
— o apartheid contado com a gramática de Picasso.

Já de volta a casa,
descobri Mohammed Al-Hawajri,
artista palestiniano.

Na série Guernica-Gaza (2010-2013),
sobrepôs as pinturas dos museus europeus
a fotografias de Gaza.


E ali estava o quadro com que começámos:
o fuzilamento de Madrid
a acontecer em Gaza.
Guernica. O apartheid. Arrifana. Iraque. Gaza. Rimam.


O Picasso do Guernica
é o mesmo que desenhou a pomba da paz.
A Europa nasceu dessas ruínas,
não de tratados.
Jean Monnet dizia:

não unimos Estados,
unimos pessoas.



Foi o que encontrei em Madrid:
9 países numa sala,
de professores para professores.
A dimensão europeia
não é um requisito do Erasmus+.
É a razão de ele existir.
Goya pintou fuzilamentos.
Em Arrifana,
os Quintados não tiveram pintor.

3 de maio.
A data do fuzilamento em Madrid.
A data em que cheguei a Madrid.
E, coincidentemente ou talvez não,
o Dia Mundial da Liberdade de Imprensa.

Desde outubro de 2023,
mais de duzentos jornalistas
assassinados em Gaza por Israel.
Mais do que nas duas Guerras Mundiais,
no Vietname
e no Afeganistão,
somados.
E as imagens continuam a sair.

A história não se repete.
Rima.
Estava escrito na parede do museu.


O fotojornalismo não é isento.
A arte não é isenta.
A pedagogia também não é.

Se o 3 de maio de 1808 merece um feriado,
uma parede no Museu
e a nossa memória,
o que merece o que estamos a assistir agora?

Pausa.


O contexto não serve o conteúdo.
O contexto é o conteúdo.
Foi isto que fiz com os meus alunos:
levei-os a um sítio
onde a pergunta muda de significado.
Só isso.
Não precisei de Madrid.
Precisei de um sítio
a vinte minutos a pé da escola,
de uma visita preparada
e de uma pergunta certa.
Os finlandeses chamam-lhe aprendizagem baseada em fenómenos: partir de um fenómeno real e deixar que as disciplinas se cruzem nele.
Nós partimos de uma fotografia. Qualquer disciplina o consegue fazer.


As três perguntas regressam no ecrã.
Não as ler. Deixar que a sala as reconheça.

Quem decide?
Quem paga?
Quem fica fora do frame?



No último dia do curso,
nas Estratégias de Disseminação,
o coordenador disse-nos:
Nós, professores,
fazemos coisas extraordinárias
e não estamos cientes disso.

E depois,
quase como uma ordem:
Write!
You are the intellectuals.


Foi o que fiz.
Está tudo documentado aqui.

Pausa. Apontar para o ecrã. Não ler o endereço.



Uma última história.
Numa noite,
em Madrid,
fui beber cañas à sala Equis
com uma colega de curso,
Julia, uma professora alemã.
Na parede estava a ser projetado um filme-mudo:
Metropolis, 1927.


No ecrã, uma frase:
O mediador entre a cabeça e as mãos
tem de ser o coração.

Pausa muito longa.

Há dez minutos
começámos com três perguntas:

Quem decide?
Quem paga?
Quem fica fora do frame?


Pausa.


No livro “Hoje, 3 de Maio”,
a Patrícia Portela recusa
contar a história por odem.
Propõe vários caminhos de leitura.
Num deles,
abrir na página deste quadro,
escolher uma personagem,
uma cor,
um detalhe,
e começar a viagem por aí.
A leitura termina
quando já não houver nada a descobrir.
Eu podia ter escolhido
o homem de camisa branca
e calças amarelas.
Escolhi quem o pintou.
E cheguei aqui.
Agora é a vossa vez.
Escolham o vosso detalhe.
Comecem a vossa viagem.

Pausa.

Eu comecei a minha viagem agora.
E já não tenho terra à vista.

Silêncio. Deixar a frase cair. Só depois o ecrã muda.


Fiat Corde
Love, love is a verb,
love is a doing word.

Sair.

Design/Fiat-Humanitas.title: Fiat Humanitas
Design/Fiat-Humanitas.content:
And when we say “our water”, and when we say “our seeds”, and when Palestinians say “our land”.


Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid, 1814. Oil on canvas, 268 × 347 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es
History reminds us that civilization is not something fixed in time or place. As Goya reported, and as history keeps confirming, there are no simple narratives of heroes and villains, winners and losers. A battle does not define a war. The interpretation of history always depends on perspective, memory and context.
History doesn’t repeat itself. It rhymes.

Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, El 3 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid, 1814. Oil on canvas, 268 × 347 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es
So what does it mean to be an educator today?
And what will we need now and in the future?
Learning no longer happens exclusively within traditional classroom structures. It unfolds beyond the classroom: collaboratively, contextually, interdisciplinarily, engaging communication, collaboration, creativity and critical thinking. Today’s educator needs a true ecosystem of tools, methodologies and languages capable of responding to the diversity of students’ rhythms, needs and lived experiences.
In this process we may draw upon digital tools, art and craft, cities, museums, culture and lived experience itself. Knowledge grows through symbolism and dialogue, through encounters and through the dissemination of ideas, experiences and good practices shared from one person to another, from teachers to teachers, from minority communities to society itself: memory and visibility.

Pablo Picasso, Guernica, 1937. Oil on canvas, 349 × 776 cm. Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía, Madrid. © Succession Picasso / VEGAP, Madrid. Image: Museo Reina Sofía, educational use.
We must develop critical thinking. Learn to observe from multiple perspectives. Know how to immerse ourselves in the process and, equally, how to step back from it through reflection, self-observation and critical awareness.
Education is not merely the transmission of information.
It is the careful cultivation and sharing of meaning.

Dumile Feni, African Guernica, 1967. Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía, Madrid. Image: Reina Sofía, educational use. museoreinasofia.es
Under copyright until 2061 (Dumile Feni d. 1991). Image used here for educational reference only.
Whatever the medium, what truly matters is being mindfully present. Not merely marking attendance. Learning requires us to activate the senses, respect individual rhythms and care for ourselves and others with empathy, openness and resilience. It also asks us to remain available to others: to communicate, to participate and to contribute actively to the collective construction and dissemination of knowledge.
Perhaps it is precisely within this coexistence of differences, memories and shared values that freedom can ultimately emerge.

Mohammed Al-Hawajri, Guernica-Gaza, 2010–2013. Photomontage. © Mohammed Al-Hawajri. Image reproduced for educational reference. mohammedalhawajri.com
Because learning also means returning to places we have already passed through and discovering what we missed, what’s changed. “To see in spring what was seen in summer, see by day what was seen at night”, as José Saramago once wrote. To learn, unlearn and learn again with the openness, curiosity and imagination of a child.
The problem is, as Picasso reminded us, that the child grows up.
Perhaps this is why education, culture and dissemination matter so deeply. They allow us to keep scattering seeds of curiosity, humanity, dialogue and imagination into the world.

Mohammed Al-Hawajri, Guernica-Gaza, 2010–2013. Photomontage. © Mohammed Al-Hawajri. Image reproduced for educational reference. mohammedalhawajri.com
Fiat humanitas.

Expanding the Landscape
Further reading and reference for those who want to go deeper.
On Goya
El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid — Museo Nacional del Prado
El 3 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid — Museo Nacional del Prado
On Picasso
Guernica — Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía
On Dumile Feni
History Doesn’t Repeat Itself, but It Does Rhyme. Dumile Feni: African Guernica — Museo Reina Sofía
On Mohammed Al-Hawajri
Mohammed Al-Hawajri — artist website
Rethinking Guernica: Guernica-Gaza — Museo Reina Sofía

Design/Framing-as-repetition.title: Framing as repetition
Design/Framing-as-repetition.content:
Framing as Repetition

The moldura is not a limit. It is what creates the object.

Porto Design Biennale, in a short Instagram piece on the making of the visual identity for Time is Present — Designing the Common, has creative director João Queirós (esad-idea) describing how the identity was built.
Repetition became the core device, a way to echo the biennale’s theme of shared, constant present. Queirós frames this repetition itself as a kind of framing, not decoration but the structure that let the identity travel coherently across formats and materials.
Seven projects, seven colours. In the biennale’s communication, these colours can appear alone, in pairs, in trios, or all together. Every combination is legitimate. Nothing is structurally excluded from appearing.

Why this matters
This is FRAME+SHOT approached from the opposite direction. Derrida’s parergon argument, that the frame does not merely limit but constitutes the object, is here enacted in graphic design practice rather than philosophy or curation. The repetition is not illustrating the theme of the present. The repetition is the present, made visible through recurrence. Context4Content again, this time from the graphic design side rather than the pedagogical or curatorial one.
There is a genuine tension worth holding against Fiat Corde and the Director’s Cut. The “Quintados sem pintor” thesis is about who is excluded from the frame, life not framed as grievable. Queirós describes a device built for the opposite: a frame engineered so that no combination is left out, where inclusion is systemic by design. Worth asking whether graphic design, when the frame is built well, can achieve a kind of structural inclusion that history painting never could. Or whether this is simply a different register of stakes, decorative pluralism versus the politics of who gets pictured at all.

Source: Porto Design Biennale, Instagram reel on the visual identity of Time is Present — Designing the Common, featuring João Queirós.

Part of index. FrameShot Context4Content

Design/Notes-on-Fiat-Corde.title: Notes on Fiat Corde
Design/Notes-on-Fiat-Corde.content:
Notes on… borrows its posture from Susan Sontag: fragments, a stance rather than a system, a writer moving between inside and outside the thing described

If Fiat Humanitas was the harvest, the Erasmus+ week in Madrid, read back in order, day by day, painting by painting, then this is the sowing. Fiat Corde is not the week. It is the gesture that comes after: taking what a week gives you and turning it into something you do, with students, in a room, twenty minutes from your school.
This note is not the talk.
The talk was ten minutes, in Portuguese, to a room of teachers, on 15 July 2026.

The slides live in Fiat Corde;
The full spoken script, in the language of the room, in Fiat Corde - spoken script.

What follows is the gesture behind the ten minutes, written for those who were not there, and for those who were and want to see the frame around the frame.
It has three movements, because the gesture has three times: what Madrid did to me, what I did with it, and why it travels.

I. What Madrid did to me

I arrived in Madrid on 3 May 2026. The anniversary of the executions Goya painted. The city was celebrating the revolt of the day before. I had come for a course on Active Methodologies. What the city gave me was not on the syllabus.

Madrid did not teach me facts. It did something quieter and harder to name: it placed me in front of images and places already loaded with the right question, and let the meaning shift under me. A plaque at the Royal Palace. the Museum of Prado, hung as an argument rather than a tour. Toledo, holding three faiths in the same stone.
And at the centre of it, this painting.


Isto não é um quadro histórico. É uma reportagem de guerra.
This is not a history painting. It is a war report.

Standing before the Third of May on the day of its anniversary, I understood something I could not yet say cleanly: that the painting’s power is not its subject but its framing. The firing squad has no face, it’s a machine. The condemned man has a face, arms open, a white shirt, lit from below. Goya chose who we see, who we don’t, whose side we feel. Quem decide? Quem paga? Quem fica fora do frame? Who decides, who pays, who is left out of the frame.

No one in Madrid told me this. Madrid did it to me. I was not given a thesis; I was walked into a question. That distinction became the whole method. It even has a name I kept from the course and made my own: Context4Content, context does not illustrate content, context is content.

II. What I did with it
The test came in June. A class of ten, a module on neuromarketing. Attention, emotion, framing: how images make us decide. I did to them what Madrid had done to me. I did not take them to see an exhibition. I took them to a question.
This is the part that does not photograph well: the preparation. Days before, I walked the rooms of the Centro de Arte Oliva with Daniel, from the mediation team. We chose the route. We designed the questions. An entire afternoon of walking through empty galleries before bringing anyone. Dá trabalho. It takes work. But it is the whole difference between an outing and a lesson.

One of the exhibitions was called “Sem Terra à Vista - No Land in Sight”. At the entrance, before the wall bearing the title, the mediator asked:
What do you think this means?

Open sea
Adrift
Emptiness
Pirates
Shipwreck.

Inside, one photograph. An Azorean landscape, green and blue and almost idyllic.

While the students tried to read the image, the mediator handed one of them a newspaper clipping to read aloud: Público, that same day, the prime ministers awaiting Bush at Lajes, the summit still speaking of diplomacy.

The title is a date: 3.16, 16 March 2003.
Two days later the invasion of Iraq began. The official cameras showed four men at a podium, flags, the peacemakers. Augusto Alves da Silva photographed the silence around them.
The landscape changed in front of them.
As the artist wrote of the series:

Photojournalism never produces an impartial image, because no photograph is.


Nada ali estava por acaso. Nada aqui está por acaso.
Nothing there was by chance. Nothing here is by chance.
On the way out, the mediator returned to the same wall and asked the same question.

Migration
Oppression
Control
No control
And pirates (again pirates, but no longer the same pirates).

The same wall, the same question, one hour apart. Context had changed everything.

The mediator had not expected the visit to be so political. Neither had the students.
Habituámo-nos a pedir à arte que nos entretenha.
We have grown used to asking art to entertain us, and it insists on doing its work anyway.

III. Why it travels
Everything above is small: one class, one afternoon, one photograph twenty minutes from the school. The talk existed to argue that the small thing is not small, that this current is old, and runs everywhere.

Goya painted an execution in 1808. Picasso painted Guernica from newspaper photographs, a reporter at a distance. Dumile Feni turned it into African Guernica, apartheid in Picasso’s grammar, beneath a title that was itself the thesis: history doesn’t repeat itself, but it does rhyme. Mohammed Al-Hawajri laid Europe’s museum paintings over photographs of Gaza (including the Madrid execution, happening again).
Guernica. O apartheid. Arrifana. O Iraque. Gaza. Rimam.

And there is a painting with no image, because no one made it. On 17 April 1809, the same troops that Goya painted laid siege to Arrifana (the town where my school now stands). One in five men was shot: “os Quintados”. Goya made the El 3 de mayo en Madrid into universal memory. Arrifana had to wait two centuries for a re-enactment.
Só não teve pintor. It just had no painter.
This is the line the whole talk turns on. Judith Butler, in Frames of War, asks which frames make a life grievable. Arrifana is the answer in the negative: a death no frame rendered mournable, because no one framed it at all.
To decide the frame is to decide who is grieved

Which is why the small classroom gesture is not small. Since October 2023, more than two hundred journalists have been killed in Gaza. More than in both World Wars, Vietnam and Afghanistan combined. And the images keep coming, at the cost of the lives that make them. On World Press Freedom Day, which is also, as it happens, the date of Goya’s painting, the question is no longer academic.


Photojournalism is not neutral.
Art is not neutral.
A pedagogia também não é. Neither is teaching.

The moment I choose what my students see, and from where, and with which question, I have framed. There is no innocent position behind the camera, or in front of the class.

Coda: the unrest of the method

To teach this way is to give up the comfort of neutral ground. It is more work: the afternoon in the empty gallery, the question designed and redesigned, and it offers no safe distance.
If context is content, then every choice of context is a choice of meaning, and the teacher who frames is answerable for the frame.

On the last day in Madrid, in the session on Dissemination Strategy, the coordinator said something I carried home:

We teachers do extraordinary things and are not aware of it.

And then, almost an order:

Write. You are the intellectuals.

This note is part of that writing: the seed, scattered, that gives Footscapes its name.
In Hoje 3 de maio, Patrícia Portela refuses to tell the story in order. She offers many ways to read; in one, you open the book at the page of this painting, choose a detail, and begin the journey there. The reading ends when there is nothing left to discover.
I could have chosen the man in the white shirt. I chose the one who painted him. And I arrived here.
But the method does not let you arrive. Every prepared visit opens the next question; every frame admits the one left outside it. To teach through context is to stay in open water on purpose.
Eu comecei a minha viagem agora. E já não tenho terra à vista.
I have only just begun my journey. And there is no land in sight.

Fiat corde.
Love, love is a verb, love is a doing word.

Expanding the Landscape
Further reading and reference for those who want to go deeper.

On framing as an act: John Berger, Ways of Seeing; Judith Butler, Frames of War (the frame that decides which lives are grievable); Jacques Derrida, The Truth in Painting (the parergon, the frame that produces its object).
On the shot (image and weapon): Paul Virilio, The Vision Machine (the optical arsenal, from the line of sight of the firearm to the camera); Harun Farocki, Images of the World and the Inscription of War (camera, rifle, satellite, surveillance: one optical logic).
On the works: Goya, El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid and El 3 de Mayo (Museo del Prado); Picasso, Guernica, and Dumile Feni, African Guernica (Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia); Mohammed Al-Hawajri, Guernica-Gaza.
On the photograph at the Oliva: Augusto Alves da Silva, 3.16, 2003 (Coleção Norlinda e José Lima).
On the novel that shares the painting: Patrícia Portela, Hoje 3 de maio (2024).
Companion pieces:

Fiat Humanitas (the week that came before)
Fiat Corde (the slides)
Fiat Corde - spoken script (the full script).



Part of Fiat Corde. LearningExperienceDesign ContextForContent

Design/On-Language-Traps.title: On Language Traps
Design/On-Language-Traps.content:
The limits of my language are the limits of my world.
— Ludwig Wittgenstein

This philosopher, named Ludwig Wittgenstein, made an observation that most people spend their lives never quite encountering.
What this means, in practice, is precise and uncomfortable. When you do not have exact words for something, you cannot think clearly about it. You cannot examine it from different angles. You cannot resolve it. You can only feel it, vaguely, repeatedly, without real resolution.
The ancient Greeks had six words for love. Eros, romantic desire. Philia, deep friendship. Storge, familial affection. Pragma, enduring mature love. Philautia, love of self. Agape, unconditional love. English has one. A person navigating a relationship problem with one word for love is working with a tool where six exist. They can feel the difference between what they want and what they have, but cannot name it. So they cannot examine it. So it stays exactly the size of the word they use to describe it.
Same feeling. More language. Completely different clarity.

Wittgenstein also named something he called a language trap. A situation in which the words themselves decide what solutions are possible.
Think of a conflict you keep returning to with someone. How do you describe it? We never communicate. They always do this. I just feel this way. Those words feel accurate. But Wittgenstein would say they are a trap. The moment you describe something in absolute terms, always, never, just, only, the language has already decided what the problem is and locked the mind out of seeing anything else.
He said that human problems, philosophical, personal, relational, are never truly solved. They dissolve. The moment you find precise language for what is happening, the problem does not get answered. It disappears. Because it was never as large as it felt. It was only as large as the words you had for it.

I have been thinking about this in relation to my own practice.
There is a version of me that spent years doing something I could not name. Designing learning experiences, building scaffolded sequences, coordinating multiple stakeholders around a shared educational outcome, facilitating reflection, creating conditions for knowledge to travel from one person to another.
I called it teaching. I called it training. I called it coordination.
The words were not wrong. But they were too small. They described the activity without naming the design intent behind it. And because the words were too small, the practice stayed too small too: contained within institutional roles, invisible to other contexts, impossible to articulate to anyone who did not already share the same frame.
Learning Experience Design is not a new Reflective Practice. It is a precise language for something that already existed.
And that precision changes everything. Not because the work changed. Because the world it can inhabit just got larger.

There is a second language trap I have been sitting with, and that’s why I’m writing in English

Design/Writing-in-English.title: Writing in English
Design/Writing-in-English.content:
What if problems don’t need a solution?

The Footscapes garden is written in English. Deliberately, carefully, with attention to register and rhythm. The comprehension is there. The written production is there. But the spoken production under professional pressure, in an interview room, in a meeting with stakeholders who do not share your first language, that is where the trap closes.
Not because the ideas are absent. Because the words arrive slightly late. And in that gap between thought and utterance, what reads as hesitation is actually translation. The mind working in one language, the mouth performing in another.
Ludwig Wittgenstein might say these too are LanguageTraps . The solution is not fluency as an abstract goal. It is precision in the specific vocabulary of the context you are trying to enter. Talent development. Learning journey . Onboarding. Enablement. Stakeholder alignment. Name the world you want to inhabit. The words will expand it.

The problems you have been carrying for years, the ones that felt too large, too complicated, too familiar to resolve.

What if problems don’t need a solution?
What if they only need a better description?

Experience/Arco-Maior.title: Arco Maior
Experience/Arco-Maior.content:
Arco Maior operates within the PIEF (Programa Integrado de Educação e Formação), a state framework designed for young people at risk of school dropout or exclusion. Its tagline — A cidade responde ao abandono escolar — situates the institution in the urban fabric of Porto rather than in a remedial periphery.
Its pedagogical model is built on project-based learning, interdisciplinary integration and the ROAD - Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento (Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento) as a personal learning roadmap. Each student holds a ROAD: a structured document in which they define learning objectives per project, self-assess progress across disciplines and social-emotional competences, and receive teacher evaluation on a 1–4 scale. The ROAD travels with the student throughout each project and closes with a dual evaluation — the student’s own (A.F.) alongside the pedagogical team’s (A.E.P.) — before a final grade is agreed and signed.
Social-emotional competences are assessed through the CASEL framework, adapted by Arco Maior into three domains: SER (self-awareness and self-regulation, codes S1–S7), CRESCER (relational skills and decision-making, codes C1–C5) and PERTENCER (social competences and agency for change, codes P1–P3). These domains are not background values. They are formally assessed objectives, embedded in the daily activity record alongside disciplinary competences, session by session.
This is not a school that tolerates passivity. Learning here is understood as relational, embodied and purposeful.

Experience/CASEL.title: CASEL
Experience/CASEL.content:
CASEL, the Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning, is an American research and advocacy organisation that developed the most widely used framework for social-emotional learning (SEL) in schools worldwide. Founded in 1994, its framework organises the competences students need to manage their own emotions, build relationships and make responsible decisions.

The Five Core Competences
Self-awareness — recognising one’s own emotions, values and strengths; understanding how thoughts and feelings influence behaviour.
Self-management — regulating emotions, managing stress, setting goals and persisting through difficulties.
Social awareness — understanding others’ perspectives, including people from different backgrounds and cultures; recognising social and ethical norms.
Relationship skills — communicating, cooperating, negotiating conflict and resisting inappropriate social pressure; building and maintaining healthy relationships.
Responsible decision-making — making ethical, constructive choices about personal behaviour and social interactions, considering consequences for self and others.

Arco Maior’s Adaptation
Arco Maior uses CASEL as the assessment backbone of its pedagogical model, reorganised into three domains with individual competence codes:
SER (self and self-regulation): Autoconhecimento (S1–S4) and Autorregulação (S5–S7).
CRESCER (growing): Capacidades Relacionais (C1–C2) and Tomada de Decisões (C3–C5).
PERTENCER (belonging): Competências Sociais (P1–P2) and Agente de Mudança (P3).
Each project at Arco Maior formally maps activities to specific CASEL codes. These appear in the activity grid alongside disciplinary competences, session by session. The ROAD - Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento records and evaluates student progress against them throughout each project.


                  
                  CASEL competences at Arco Maior are not aspirational values — they are assessed objectives. The same codes that appear on a lesson plan appear on a student's final grade document. 
                  
                


Evidence Base
Durlak et al. (2011) conducted a meta-analysis of 213 school-based SEL programmes involving over 270,000 students. Programmes following the CASEL framework produced an 11-percentile-point gain in academic achievement alongside measurable improvements in social-emotional skills, attitudes and behaviour, and reduced conduct problems.

Connections
ROAD - Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento — the Arco Maior tool that records and evaluates CASEL competences per student and per project.
Transforma-te Transforma — a case study in which CASEL functioned as the assessment architecture across seven disciplines simultaneously.
Social Skills — one of the five elements of cooperative learning; overlaps with CASEL’s relationship skills domain.
Reflective Practice — structural complement to CASEL’s self-awareness and self-management competences.
Learner Autonomy — the outcome CASEL’s self-management and responsible decision-making competences work toward.
Empowering Learners — the DigCompEdu area that maps most directly onto CASEL’s responsible decision-making in digital contexts.
Collaborative Learning — the pedagogical structure in which CASEL relationship skills are most visibly practiced.

Experience/ROAD---Roteiro-de-Objetivos-d.filePath: Experience/ROAD - Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento.md
Experience/ROAD---Roteiro-de-Objetivos-d.title: ROAD
Experience/ROAD---Roteiro-de-Objetivos-d.content:
The ROAD makes assessment visible to the student rather than done to them.

The ROAD — Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento (Learning and Development Objectives Roadmap) — is the individual assessment tool used in every project at Arco Maior. It is not a test. It is a structured document that travels with each student throughout a project, recording learning objectives, progress and evaluations across both disciplinary and CASEL social-emotional competences.

How It Works
At the start of each project, the student receives a ROAD specifying the competences to be assessed: one column per participating discipline, one section for CASEL social-emotional competences.
Throughout the project, the student uses the ROAD for self-assessment (Avaliação do Formando, A.F.) — rating their own performance against each competence. The pedagogical team conducts a parallel assessment (Avaliação da Equipa Pedagógica, A.E.P.). Both evaluations are recorded in the same document.
At project end, a final grade is agreed and signed by the student, then countersigned by the team coordinator. The ROAD becomes part of the student’s portfolio.


                  
                  The ROAD makes assessment visible to the student rather than done to them. The dual-column structure — self-assessment alongside teacher assessment — turns evaluation into a conversation rather than a verdict. 
                  
                


What It Reveals
The gap between A.F. and A.E.P. scores is itself pedagogically significant. A student who consistently underrates their own performance reveals something different from one who consistently overrates it. The ROAD is a record of both the work and the student’s relationship to their own work.

Connections
CASEL — the social-emotional framework whose competences the ROAD tracks and evaluates.
Transforma-te Transforma — a case study in which the ROAD structured assessment across seven disciplines and four CASEL competences simultaneously.
Reflective Practice — the ROAD requires structured self-reflection as a formal assessment act; it is the institutional form of Schön’s reflective practitioner applied to students.
Learner Autonomy — the ROAD builds agency: students who assess their own work develop ownership over their learning trajectory.
Scaffolding — the ROAD provides the assessment scaffold within which project-based learning operates at Arco Maior.
Experiential Learning — the ROAD’s self-assessment phase is the formal counterpart to the reflective observation stage in Kolb’s experiential learning cycle.

Experience/Transforma-te-Transforma.title: Transforma-te, Transforma
Experience/Transforma-te-Transforma.content:
An interdisciplinary project. A student assembly. A decision to make something public.

Transforma-te, Transforma is an interdisciplinary project developed at Polo 4 of Arco Maior (Carvalhido, Porto). The topic - bullying - was chosen by the students themselves in an assembly. It was not a teacher’s proposal.
Assessed through the 21CLD framework, the project reached maximum score in five of six dimensions: ICT for Learning, Collaboration, Real-World Problem-Solving, Skilled Communication and Self-Regulation.
Dissemination ran through three publications on the Arco Maior website (May and July 2025) and a presence in the June 2025 edition of the Jornal do Arco Maior — an interview with the board president conducted by a student, editorially coordinated by Filipe Gama.
The article’s central argument: the project succeeded not because of the curriculum, but in spite of usual conditions. The students acted because they were trusted with a real problem.
It is a project worth examining closely, not because it was exceptional, but because it was real.

The Origin
The project did not begin with a curriculum objective. It began with a student assembly.
In that assembly, students named bullying as something they experienced and something they wanted to address. The choice of topic, the identification of relevant experts to interview, the name of the project — all of this came from the students. The teachers’ role was to coordinate the contribution of each discipline within the pedagogical structure already in place, aligning the work with ROAD objectives and CASEL domains.
This matters. The 21st Century Learning Design framework calls this authentic learning: a problem that is real, a solution produced for a real audience, consequences that extend beyond the classroom. The students were not studying bullying. They were acting on it.

The Architecture
Eleven disciplines contributed, each with a distinct role:

Português wrote the interview scripts.
Ciências Sociais built the knowledge base by bringing the police into the classroom.
Marcenaria built the recording studio from OSB board.
Arte e Multimédia created LED neon lettering and the calligraphic posters.
TIC, RIC and Multimédia recorded, edited and published the podcast and produced a teaser in a single morning.
Matemática worked statistical literacy into the theme.
Inglês distinguished constructive from offensive critique.
Educação Física addressed self-defence techniques.
Cozinha and Serviço de Mesa/Bar prepared a coffee-break available during recordings.

The interdisciplinary structure was not decorative. Each discipline’s contribution was load-bearing. Remove any one of them and the product would have been diminished — or would not have existed at all.

Arte e Multimédia produced the set’s visual identity. Students designed the lettering for the LED neon “Arco Maior 4” — measuring the perimeters of calligraphic lines, working from a projected guide onto set paper with string and pins. The class visited Neónia Porto, a cultural and creative space in Porto, for inspiration. The visit was structured around the same CASEL categories used throughout the project — Observação e Atenção, Empatia e Escuta, Respeito, Reconhecer emoções and Analisar e Avaliar Situações — and assessed through a dedicated visit guide. Students were asked to photograph an artwork that provoked a strong emotional response and add it to their portfolio and, later, produced the calligraphic motivational phrases against bullying, drawn posters that would stand in the set and later circulate publicly.
Arte e Multimédia directed the podcast recording session: operating cameras, managing audio, directing the guests through the interview in a studio the school’s own students had built. But the podcast was not the only product. Students’ also produced a teaser: scripted, filmed and edited in a single morning, working from the accumulated experience of the previous sessions. That speed is the tell. The teaser was not planned. It was made possible by the confidence students had built through iteration. It is the clearest evidence of what the 21CLD framework calls self-regulation: not compliance with a task, but the capacity to mobilise what you have already learned and direct it independently.


                  
                  The CASEL framework here is not a taxonomy applied after the fact. It is the architecture. S1, P1, P2 and C3 appear in the Grelha de Atividades against every single session, in every single discipline, across the full six weeks. This is what embedded social-emotional learning looks like from the inside. 
                  
                


The Studio
The recording set deserves its own account.
It was not rented. It was not borrowed. It was built — in a classroom, from OSB board, by the students who would sit in front of it.
Marcenaria began the work by planning, marking, calculating, cutting, assembling and fixing the panels into a standing set. Arte e Multimédia designed and installed the visual elements: the LED strips followed the calligraphic lines. The result carried both the craft of the hands that made it and the content of the minds that designed it.
When the podcast was recorded, the students were not sitting in a borrowed room. They were sitting in something they had made.

The Products
The project produced four primary outputs: two podcast episodes on YouTube, an Instagram Reel and hand-drawn motivational posters.
The podcast is the centrepiece. Two episodes were recorded, edited and published on YouTube. The guests were a CPCJ (Comissão de Proteção de Crianças e Jovens) technician who works directly with some of the students, and the school director. The interviews were conducted by the students themselves, using questions developed across five weeks of work in Português and Ciências Sociais.
A video was produced, documenting the project. It was the first product made public, linked from the May 2025 Arco Maior news item.
An Instagram Reel, published on the @arcomaiorcampanha account and embedded in the July 2025 news item, shows the calligraphic lettering created during the project. The Arco Maior description states the video was produced to put students’ “way of thinking, communicating and being to the test,” and expresses the hope that it “inspires us all to prevention and to action.” What it represents: a public act of communication, produced by young people who had been invisible, addressed to anyone who would listen.
The calligraphic posters and the LED neon lettering formed the visual environment of the studio and beyond. These were not decorations. They were acts of communication — motivational messages about bullying prevention, produced by hand, designed for impact.

Reading the Project Through 21CLD
The 21st Century Learning Design framework (Microsoft Partners in Learning, 2011) offers a useful lens for understanding what this project achieved. Across six dimensions, Transforma-te, Transforma reached the maximum score in five.
ICT for Learning: 5/5. Digital technologies were not supplementary. The podcast format required recording, editing and publication. Without ICT, the product would not have existed. The students were designers of a digital artefact for authentic users — the YouTube audience.
Collaboration: 5/5. The work was genuinely interdependent. The scriptwriters depended on the interviewers. The interviewers depended on the sound operators. The studio was built by one discipline and dressed by another. The 21CLD framework distinguishes real collaboration from group work by asking whether the product fails if any member does not fulfil their role. It would have.
Knowledge Construction: 4/5. Students researched, synthesised and applied knowledge about bullying across disciplines and in a new, real-world context. The score falls short of maximum not because the interdisciplinarity was weak, but because it was not systematically assessed as a formal learning objective across all contributing disciplines.
Real-World Problem-Solving: 4/4. Maximum. Bullying was a real problem, identified by the students themselves, with direct impact on their community. The solutions were implemented and communicated to a real, external audience.
Skilled Communication: 4/4. Maximum. The podcast is multimodal — audio, video, photography, lettering, live interview. It was conceived for a specific audience with a specific communicative intent. Expert testimony was included as supporting evidence.
Self-Regulation: 4/4. Maximum. The ROAD structure meant students had individual learning objectives and success criteria defined in advance. They planned their work, distributed roles and iterated. The project closed on 20 February with a formal self-assessment followed by the pedagogical team’s final ROAD evaluation — a dual-authored record of what each student had actually done.


                  
                  What 21CLD reveals here is not a curriculum design. It reveals what happened when students were trusted with a real problem. 
                  
                


Dissemination
The project was disseminated through three public channels.
The Arco Maior website published two news items directly tied to this project. The first documents the project and links to both the podcast and the student-produced video. The school’s framing is explicit: the students “learned to make Arco Maior a safer, more empathetic and respectful place for everyone.”
The second embeds an Instagram Reel from the @arcomaiorcampanha campaign account — a public channel connected to Polo 4 Carvalhido. The Reel shows the calligraphic lettering created during the project.
A third item documents an interview with Prof. Joaquim Azevedo - founder of Arco Maior - conducted by student Mara as part of the preparation for the June 2025 edition of the Jornal do Arco Maior. That edition, coordinated editorially by Filipe Gama and identified by ISSN, addressed the theme of education and society in 2050. The student interviewing a board president about the future of education is not incidental to the project. It is the project’s logic made visible: young people who started by examining bullying in their own school ended by questioning what education should become.

Connections
This project sits at the intersection of several threads that run through index and beyond.
It demonstrates Project-Based Learning at a level that the 21CLD framework recognises as maximum performance: student-initiated, interdisciplinary, anchored in a real problem, producing artefacts for a real public. It demonstrates CASEL competences not as items on a checklist but as lived practice — the students who ran the podcast were, in the very act of running it, practising self-regulation, empathy and responsible decision-making.
It also demonstrates something that frameworks do not always name clearly: the conditions under which young people — including those in highly vulnerable situations — produce work of genuine quality. Those conditions are not primarily technological or curricular. They are relational. The students acted because they trusted the institution. The institution trusted them back.
The ROAD framework, the CASEL referential and the pedagogical model of Arco Maior did not produce the project. They made it possible for the project to produce itself.

References
Arco Maior. (2025, May 12). Transforma-te e transforma. www.arcomaior.pt/noticias/post.php
Arco Maior. (2025, May 26). Entrevista ao Prof. Joaquim Azevedo sobre o mundo e a educação em 2050. www.arcomaior.pt/noticias/post.php
Arco Maior. (2025, July 9). Transforma-te e Transforma. www.arcomaior.pt/noticias/post.php
Arco Maior. (2024/2025). Grelha das Atividades da Turma 1 — Projeto Transforma-te e Transforma. Documento interno.
Arco Maior. (2024/2025). Planificação Curricular — Transforma-te e Transforma. Documento interno.
Arco Maior. (2024/2025). ROAD — Roteiro de Objetivos de Aprendizagem e Desenvolvimento: Projeto N.º 03 — Transforma-te e Transforma. Documento interno.
Durlak, J. A., Weissberg, R. P., Dymnicki, A. B., Taylor, R. D., & Schellinger, K. B. (2011). The impact of enhancing students’ social and emotional learning: A meta-analysis of school-based universal interventions. Child Development, 82(1), 405–432.
Microsoft Partners in Learning. (2011). 21st Century Learning Design: A guide for creating activities that support learning for the 21st century. Microsoft Corporation.

Home/A-Note-on-Process.title: A Note on Process
Home/A-Note-on-Process.content:
A Note on Process
The notes in this garden were written collaboratively. The ideas, experiences, observations and reflections are mine. The writing involved AI assistants.
This was not automation. It was dialogue. I provided context: course notes, readings, field observations, frameworks encountered during the week and my own reflections on them. AI helped shape that material into notes, clarifying, structuring, finding the right register and sometimes pushing back when something didn’t hold together.
Every note went through iteration. What you read here is the result of that back-and-forth, not a generated output.
I find it appropriate to be transparent about this for two reasons. The first is honesty: these notes carry my voice and my thinking, but not my hand alone. The second is coherence. A garden about learning, methodology and the thoughtful use of technology should model what it advocates. Using AI as a thinking partner, not a shortcut, is part of what I mean when I say technology amplifies the human dimension of learning rather than replacing it.
If you are an educator curious about this process, I am happy to talk about it.
— Filipe Gama

Home/About-me.title: About me
Home/About-me.content:
I am a professional educator and trainer in the areas of design, communication and multimedia, with a background in communication design. My work focuses on creating learning experiences that are practical, participatory and grounded in real contexts.
Over the years, my practice has grown to include active methodologies, audiovisual production, digital literacy and project-based learning. I am interested in how these can work together to produce learning that connects people, disciplines and contexts. I am moving in the direction of Learning Experience Design: how, where, why and what it feels like to learn well.
Footscapes started as documentation of one week. An Erasmus+ KA1 mobility programme in Madrid and Toledo, focused on innovative and active methodologies for teaching and learning. It grew from there. This digital garden collects frameworks, reflections, artworks and field observations from that week and beyond. It is also a working portfolio of how I think and practise.
The underlying ideas are, more or less, simple: knowledge travels and history doesn’t repeat itself, it rhymes.
— Filipe Gama
These notes were written collaboratively. See A Note on Process.
For the trainer side of this practice — experience, frameworks and projects — see Portfolio.

Part of Footscapes. LearningLandscapes

Home/Portfolio.title: Portfolio
Home/Portfolio.content:
A portfolio that keeps building as the practice does, not a document that closes when it is submitted.

This page collects the trainer side of what I do: where I have taught, how I work, and a selection of projects worth examining closely. It exists for anyone considering me as a trainer or collaborator — most recently, a candidatura to teach Produção de Conteúdos Interativos at Escola Profissional Raul Dória.
A full CV is available on request at design.filipegama@gmail.com.

Practice, in brief
Formador profissional since 2011. Communication designer since 2003. The two overlap more than they sit side by side — I teach multimedia, communication and audiovisual production from the position of someone still doing that work, not only someone who once did.
Areas taught: Multimedia, Communication and Digital Service, Marketing and Sales, Tourism, Psychosocial Support, Sociocultural Animation, Audiovisuals.
2025/26, currently: Agrupamento de Escolas de Arrifana (Comunicação e Serviço Digital, Técnico de Juventude), Escola Profissional e Tecnológica Profensino (Comunicação e Serviço Digital), Associação Arco Maior (Arte e Multimédia, two classes).
Earlier: Agrupamentos de Escolas de Arouca, Anadia and Vila Verde; Agrupamento de Escolas nº 1 do Marco de Canaveses; Agrupamento de Escolas Manoel de Oliveira. Corporate spell as Post-Production Team Coordinator at Farfetch, and as communication designer at SIGIA on the WHO’s COVID-19 response.

How I work
Four things travel with me from course to course, from Arouca to Arco Maior to Arrifana:
Direção de cursos. Coordinating pedagogical teams, Formação em Contexto de Trabalho (FCT) partnerships and Provas de Aptidão Profissional (PAP) is, at its core, an articulation job — making sure the pieces a course promises actually connect.
FCT and PAP as real stakes, not paperwork. A work placement or a final project only teaches something if it is treated as consequential. I visit, I question feasibility, I push back on plans that won’t survive contact with a real workplace.
Project-based learning with social-emotional learning built in, not bolted on. At Arco Maior, this is formalised through ROAD and the CASEL framework — competences assessed alongside disciplinary content, session by session, not as an afterthought.
Differentiation as a default, not an accommodation. Students at risk of dropout, a student on the autism spectrum, students newly arrived in Portugal — the starting move is the same: a safe space to experiment, at the student’s own pace, that gradually widens what that student believes they can make and show.

Selected projects
Transforma-te, Transforma — Arco Maior, 2024/25. An 11-discipline project on bullying prevention, chosen by the students themselves. They built their own recording studio, designed the visual identity, and produced two podcast episodes for a real audience. Rated 5/5 on five of six 21st Century Learning Design dimensions. The fullest account of how I work is here.
Wiki de Cinematografia — Agrupamento de Escolas de Arouca, 2022/23. A Notion-based repository built to teach audiovisual production, structured around pre-production, production, post-production and distribution, designed to adapt to different learning paces.
Arouca Geofood — Agrupamento de Escolas de Arouca, 2016/17. A finalist project in the Fundação Ilídio Pinho’s Ciência na Escola award, connecting Multimedia, Cozinha/Pastelaria, Mesa/Bar and Turismo students around the culture and heritage of the Arouca Geopark.
Laying European Table — Agrupamento de Escolas de Arouca, Erasmus+, 2015–17. A two-year project linking schools in Italy, Latvia, Poland, Portugal and Turkey through European food culture, audiovisual production and entrepreneurship.

Connections
About me — the wider context this practice sits inside.
index — more notes on learning in the world.
CASEL, ROAD, 21st Century Learning Design — the frameworks named above.

Part of Footscapes. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: About
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning is an Erasmus+ KA1 structured course organised by English Matters, held across Madrid and Toledo, 3 to 9 May 2026. It sits within a broader European commitment to professional development for educators, framed by the Notes on European Dimension and certified under Erasmus+ quality standards. Forty hours across seven days. Participants were educators. For that week, they were also learners. The distinction mattered.
