Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/African Guernica.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: African Guernica
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Dumile Feni, African Guernica, 1967. Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía, Madrid. Image: Reina Sofía, educational use. museoreinasofia.es
Under copyright until 2061 (Dumile Feni d. 1991). Image used here for educational reference only.

What It Is
“African Guernica” is the name given to a body of work by South African artist Dumile Feni, created under apartheid. The title is not a comparison — it is a proposal for a trans-historical reading of violence.

“History doesn’t repeat itself, it does rhyme.”


The Post-Colonial Displacement
The exhibition shifts the centre of the European narrative.
The suffering represented by Picasso in Guernica stops being exclusively European. It enters into dialogue with colonialism, apartheid, structural racism, displacement, exclusion, and systemic violence. Geographies and systems of power vary; the grammar of violence does not.

The Body as Political Archive
As in Guernica, the bodies in Dumile Feni’s work appear distorted, wounded, fragmented, spectral. The body stops being an anatomical representation and becomes a historical archive — a record of what power does to people over time.

The Reconfiguration of the Canon
The exhibition poses a quiet but serious question: who enters the history of art? Whose suffering receives institutional recognition? Whose universalism is assumed, and whose must be argued for?
Placing African Guernica in dialogue with Picasso does not diminish either work. It expands the concept of cultural memory — showing that the violence Guernica testified to did not end in 1937, and did not stay in Europe.

In the Course
Seen during Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia, Saturday, 9 May 2026. An unexpected and necessary response to the week: European art does not exhaust the world’s suffering.
See also: Guernica, Fiat Humanitas.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Arte contemporânea pós-1975.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Contemporary Art after 1975
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Thematic itinerary on the upper floor of the Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia. Archive, identity, public space, and critical consciousness.

From Representation to Interrogation
After 1975, art stops trying primarily to represent the world. It tries to interrogate systems — political, mediatic, identitary, colonial, economic, institutional.
The work changes form: installation, video, archive, performance, document, spatial intervention. The single object gives way to environments that demand active reading.

The Visitor’s Role Changes
The visitor stops being a contemplative observer and becomes interpreter, participant, critical reader, agent of meaning-making.
Contemporary art rarely provides a single reading. It requires negotiation, inference, contextualisation, and historical awareness. The museum stops being a place of answers and becomes a space of productive difficulty.

Marginalised Voices, Silenced Memories
One of the strongest threads across this floor: the emergence of voices that had been absent from the canon — marginalised identities, hybrid subjectivities, peripheral memories, silenced communities.
The central question is quiet but persistent: who has the right to representation?
This connects directly to African Guernica by Dumile Feni, seen in the same visit — a work that asks the same question from a different continent and a different system of violence.

Art, Public Space, and Political Consciousness
Many works here extend beyond the museum into protest, social archive, media, activism, urbanism, and public sphere. Contemporary art understood in this way is not decoration — it is a form of civic participation.

The Cultural Broth
The most lasting impression from the floor: every work participates in a historical ecology of meanings. Nothing exists in isolation. Each work carries political debates, visual languages, collective memories, social conflicts, cultural imaginaries, communication technologies, urban transformations, and shifts in sensibility.
To read contemporary art is to read the world it came from — and to recognise how much of that world is still present.

Part of Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Avant-Garde Territories.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Avant-Garde Territories
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Thematic itinerary at the Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia. A cultural archaeology of artistic modernity from the late nineteenth century to the first half of the twentieth.

What the Itinerary Constructs
The itinerary does not present a succession of modernist masterpieces. It builds an understanding of why modernism happened — not as aesthetic evolution but as a response to the radical transformation of human experience.
The avant-gardes did not emerge from art schools. They emerged from the modern city: its acceleration, its industrial noise, its masses, its advertising, its printed culture, its mechanised warfare, its international circulation of ideas. Each movement is legible only inside this ecosystem.

The City as Perceptual Laboratory
Urban modernity fragmented perception. The street became simultaneous, saturated, fast, discontinuous. Cubism is not a formal innovation — it translates a new perceptual condition: multiple viewpoints, simultaneity, the collapse of the fixed observer.
Classical perspective assumed stability, centrality, rational order. Modernity destroyed that stability. Art registered the destruction.

Magazines, Posters, and the End of High Culture
One of the strongest dimensions of the itinerary is its refusal to separate fine art from graphic culture, design, advertising, typography, and mass communication.
Avant-garde magazines functioned simultaneously as manifestos, international networks, curatorial spaces, visual laboratories, and political platforms. Modernism spread not through museums but through printed pages, covers, layouts, posters, and technical reproduction.
This brings the historical avant-gardes surprisingly close to contemporary digital culture: rapid circulation, remix, visual identity, transnational community. The medium changes; the logic of cultural diffusion does not.

The Dissolution of Disciplinary Boundaries
The itinerary shows art, architecture, fashion, set design, urban planning, and politics converging — the modern attempt to make art a total intervention in human experience, not a contemplative object.
The ambivalence is left in the room: the same modernism that promised emancipation also opened the door to massification, anonymity, and technocracy.

Modernity and Childhood
Running through the itinerary: a tension between rationalisation and imagination. The avant-gardes repeatedly attempted to recover spontaneity, play, experiment, and undisciplined language.
This connects directly to Picasso:

“Every child is an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.”

Avant-garde modernity is, among other things, an attempt to unlearn perceptual convention. See Notes on Unlearning.

Part of Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Cervantes.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Miguel de Cervantes
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Portrait traditionally attributed to Juan de Jáuregui, c. 1600. Authenticity disputed. Public domain. Wikimedia Commons
Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra (1547–1616). Spanish writer, author of Don Quixote de la Mancha (1605/1615), considered the first modern novel.

“La pluma es la lengua del alma.”
“The quill is the tongue of the soul.”


Narrative Modernity
Don Quixote is considered the first modern novel not because of its form but because its characters evolve. They are not flat. They carry interior transformation, ambiguity, and complexity — qualities that distinguish a character who changes through experience from one who merely enacts a role.
Genuine learning requires the same: transformation, ambiguity, evolution, and the willingness to hold complexity without resolving it prematurely.
Don Quixote is a figure who learns against the resistance of the world. Sancho Panza learns through proximity and concrete experience. Both are models of learning — different kinds, equally real.

Connection to the Course
Cervantes appeared as a cultural reference across Toledo and Madrid. The phrase La pluma es la lengua del alma framed the practice of reflective writing and the Learning Portfolios — the idea that writing is not a record of thought but a mode of thought itself.
See Don Quixote, Sancho Panza.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Diego Velázquez.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Diego Velázquez
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Court painter to King Philip IV of Spain from 1623 until his death in 1660. Velázquez is one of the most technically accomplished painters in European history, and one of the most conceptually ambitious. His paintings raise questions about representation, illusion, power and the act of looking that have not been exhausted in four centuries.
Las Meninas (1656) is the most studied painting in the Prado collection and possibly the most discussed painting in Western art. It depicts the royal family, court attendants, a dog — and the viewer, positioned where the king and queen must be standing. The mirror at the back of the room confirms their presence. What appears to be a scene becomes a meditation on what painting does.
Felipe IV appears in multiple Velázquez portraits in the Prado — a monarch rendered with increasing psychological complexity over the decades of their working relationship.
See Highlights of Spanish Art.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Don Quixote.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Don Quixote
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Cervantes, El ingenioso hidalgo don Quijote de la Mancha, 1605 and 1615.


Gustave Doré, illustration for Don Quixote by Miguel de Cervantes, 1863. Engraving. Public domain. Wikimedia Commons

The Character
Don Quixote de la Mancha is a minor nobleman who has read too many chivalric romances and decides to become a knight-errant. He misreads reality — systematically, persistently, magnificently. Windmills are giants. Inns are castles. A peasant woman is a noblewoman. He acts on the world as he imagines it, not as it is.
This is not simply madness. It is a philosophical position: that imagination has its own kind of truth, and that the world we inhabit is partly the world we choose to see.

The Transformation
The relationship between Don Quixote and his squire Sancho Panza is the novel’s moral engine. Sancho begins grounded, pragmatic, sceptical. Quixote begins idealistic, deluded, relentless. By the end, each has become something of the other.
This mutual transformation — the way learning changes both parties — connects directly to the teacher-learner relationship. The facilitator who never changes is not facilitating. They are performing.

Don Quixote as First Modern Novel
Cervantes built a text that is aware of its own fictionality, that includes readers as characters, that questions the reliability of its own narrators. It was doing in 1605 what postmodern literature claims as recent innovation.
It is also, among other things, a meditation on how stories shape behaviour — how the narratives we consume become the lenses through which we see possibility.

Connections in the Course
The novel connects to Cervantes (the biographical note), Sancho Panza, Context4Content, and the Toledo — The City of Three Cultures visit. The Pérez-Reverte adaptation discussed in the programme connects to Active Methodologies and scaffolding through contemporary retellings.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Dumile Feni.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Dumile Feni
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
South African artist, 1939–1991, born in the Eastern Cape and later exiled from apartheid South Africa. Feni worked primarily in drawing and printmaking, developing a figurative language of extreme expressive power to document human suffering under apartheid.
African Guernica (1967) is his most internationally known work, produced in exile in London. The title is not metaphorical — Feni understood Picasso’s Guernica as a direct precursor and positioned his own response to South African violence within that tradition of bearing witness through art.
Feni’s work is part of the permanent collection of the Reina Sofía, where it appears in dialogue with Picasso’s work and the museum’s broader commitment to art as political testimony.
See African Guernica, Guernica, Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid, 1814. Oil on canvas, 268 × 347 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The painting depicts the popular uprising in Madrid on 2 May 1808, when the city’s residents attacked Mamluk cavalry serving the French occupying forces. Goya painted it six years after the event, not as a witness but as a moral interpreter.
There is no stable centre. The composition is deliberately chaotic: diagonal thrusts, overlapping bodies, horses rearing, weapons clashing at close range. The scene resists the eye’s search for order — and that resistance is the point.

Composition as Argument
Classical battle painting placed a hero at the centre: identifiable, commanding, triumphant. Goya refuses this entirely.
The figures are anonymous. No uniform identifies a leader; no face is elevated above the rest. Resistance is collective, unnamed, improvised. The violence is immediate and unglamorous.
This is the visual logic of testimony rather than commemoration.

The Diptych
El 2 de mayo was painted as the companion piece to El 3 de mayo en Madrid. Together they form a single moral argument about war — the uprising and its consequence. Chaos and execution. Resistance and retribution.
Goya does not celebrate either. He witnesses both.

In the Context of the Course
The painting connects to several threads running through the week:
The question of representation — who gets to name events, how power constructs its version of history — runs from here directly to Las Meninas, Guernica and What is truth in 2026?.
The collective, anonymous figure connects to Universal Design for Learning: learning designed for everyone, not centred on the exceptional individual.
The chaotic composition mirrors the pedagogical value of productive difficulty — the discomfort that precedes understanding.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El 3 de mayo en Madrid.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El 3 de mayo en Madrid
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, El 3 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid, 1814. Oil on canvas, 268 × 347 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The night after the uprising. French soldiers execute Madrid civilians by firing squad. Goya painted this six years after the event, alongside the companion work El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid.

The Logic of the Work
There is no heroism here. No glorification. No narrative arc that converts death into sacrifice.
The man in white at the centre raises his arms — but this is not triumph. It is exposure. The gesture is surrender and defiance at once, and it changes nothing. The soldiers are faceless, their backs turned, their rifles aimed with mechanical precision. On one side, individuals; on the other, a system.
Goya strips war down to what it is: the organised killing of people.

The Line to Guernica
The visual language runs directly forward to Guernica. Monochrome palette, fragmented bodies, the refusal to aestheticise violence. Picasso knew this painting. The moral inheritance is explicit.

Connections in the Course
The dehumanisation of the executioners — uniform, faceless, anonymous — connects to discussions of media literacy and how systems construct the invisibility of their own violence. See What is truth in 2026? and Healthy scepticism.
The question of who receives institutional memory and whose suffering is recorded runs from here to African Guernica and Arte contemporânea pós-1975.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El Descendimiento.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El Descendimiento
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Rogier van der Weyden, El Descendimiento, c. 1435. Oil on panel, 220 × 262 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The body of Christ being lowered from the cross. Ten figures: disciples, women, the Virgin. The composition is compressed into a shallow space, almost theatrical — as if the scene were staged against a flat backdrop rather than set in depth.

Emotion as Cognitive Tool
Van der Weyden’s central technique is the transfer of grief.
Each figure in the painting responds to the same event with a different emotional register: prostrate collapse (the Virgin), silent shock, active service, restrained dignity. The viewer reads across a spectrum of responses to loss.
This is not decoration. It is a structured emotional argument: grief is real, it is shared, it takes different forms in different bodies, and to witness it fully you must inhabit more than one perspective at once.

Connections in the Course
The painting raises the question of empathy as a learning condition. Learning that has no affective dimension tends not to stay. What you feel, you remember. See Carl Rogers — Experiential Learning and Why use a multi-sensory approach?.
The staged, almost choreographed composition also connects to cooperative learning: different roles, different positions, a shared task — and the whole only visible when you hold all perspectives simultaneously.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El Greco.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El Greco
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Greek-born painter who worked primarily in Toledo, Spain, from the 1570s until his death in 1614. His work is characterised by elongated figures, dramatic light, intense colour and spiritual intensity. El Greco was largely overlooked after his death and was rediscovered in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, when modernist artists recognised in his distortions and expressiveness a precursor to their own experiments.
In Toledo, El Greco is a constant presence. El entierro del conde de Orgaz in the church of Santo Tomé is perhaps the most significant single work in the city — a painting that compresses earth and heaven, community and eternity, into a single image. El caballero de la mano en el pecho in the Prado is one of the most psychologically direct portraits of the Spanish Renaissance.
See Highlights of Spanish Art, Toledo — The City of Three Cultures.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El Lavatorio.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El Lavatorio
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Jacopo Tintoretto, El Lavatorio, 1548–1549. Oil on canvas, 210 × 533 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es
Jacopo Tintoretto, 1548–1549. Oil on canvas, 210 × 533 cm. Prado, Madrid.

What It Shows
Christ washing the feet of his disciples at the Last Supper. The scene is set in an architectural space of striking depth — columns, arched ceiling, figures receding into a diagonal that drives the eye from the foreground to a distant vanishing point.

The Diagonal as Experience Design
Tintoretto breaks with the centred, symmetrical composition that conventional religious painting used to signal hierarchy and solemnity.
The diagonal pulls the viewer through the scene. The eye does not rest; it moves. The architectural depth creates a sense of space extending beyond the frame. The experience is immersive rather than contemplative.
This is a deliberate choice about how a viewer encounters the work — and it is a design decision with clear pedagogical resonance: the structure of an experience shapes the quality of the learning.

Connections in the Course
The painting connects to learning experience design: the idea that how learning is structured — its flow, sequence, spatial logic, and emotional pacing — determines what learners take away.
The servant-leadership act (Christ washing feet) also connects to the week’s discussions of role, power, and the dignity of care. See Cooperative Learning and Promotive Interaction.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El caballero de la mano en el pecho.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El caballero de la mano en el pecho
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
El Greco (Doménikos Theotokópoulos), El caballero de la mano en el pecho, c. 1578–1580. Oil on canvas, 81 × 66 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
A man in black, his right hand placed over his heart. The face is direct, composed, without affect. The gesture is an assertion — of honour, loyalty, or sincerity — but the assertion has no external referent. We do not know who he is, what he swears, or to whom.

Identity Without Context
The portrait is remarkable for what it withholds.
The man’s name, rank, and circumstances are unknown. What remains is the gesture itself: a hand on the chest, a declaration made in the grammar of the body. Identity is presented, but not explained.
This creates a productive instability: the viewer must interpret without being told what to conclude. The work resists the comfortable certainty of the label.

Connections in the Course
El Greco’s painting connects to media literacy: images carry claims about identity, trustworthiness, and virtue — but those claims are constructed, and they require critical reading. See Las Meninas, What is truth in 2026?, and Healthy scepticism.
The unknown subject also connects to the week’s recurring interest in invisible or marginalised voices: those who were not recorded fully, whose identity required a gesture rather than a name.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/El entierro del conde de Orgaz.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El entierro del conde de Orgaz
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
!
El Greco (Doménikos Theotokópoulos), El entierro del señor de Orgaz, 1586–1588. Oil on canvas, 480 × 360 cm. Iglesia de Santo Tomé, Toledo. Public domain. Wikimedia Commons

The Work
El Greco painted this for the parish church of Santo Tomé in Toledo, commissioned by the priest Andrés Núñez de Madrid. The subject is a local legend from 1312: when Don Gonzalo Ruiz de Toledo was buried, Saints Stephen and Augustine appeared and lowered his body into the tomb themselves.
The painting is divided into two registers without a dividing line. Below: the burial, attended by a procession of Toledans from El Greco’s own time. Above: the soul of the dead man ascending toward a heavenly court of the Virgin, Christ, John the Baptist, and an assembly of saints. Earth and heaven occupy the same canvas, separated only by composition.
It has never moved. The painting was made for this church, in this city, and remains here. That fact is part of what it is.

Reading the Image
The lower register is a gallery of portraits — recognisable Toledans of the late sixteenth century, rendered with documentary precision. El Greco included himself among them: a face looking directly at the viewer, just above the gauntlet of Saint Stephen. His son Jorge Manuel appears as the young page in the lower left, holding a torch. The boy points toward the saints. On the handkerchief in his pocket: the year 1578, El Greco’s date of arrival in Toledo.
The upper register shifts into a different pictorial language — elongated figures, compressed space, a visionary light that has no natural source. The soul of Don Gonzalo rises as a small, almost transparent form between two worlds.
The two registers do not conflict. They coexist, as the sacred and the civic coexisted in Counter-Reformation Toledo.

Historical and Cultural Context
The work is a product of the Counter-Reformation: a visual argument for the intercession of saints, the efficacy of good works, and the continuity between earthly piety and heavenly reward. The Council of Trent had recently affirmed exactly what Protestants denied — that saints could intervene, that the Church mediated between the living and the dead. El Greco’s painting is also a sermon.
Toledo in 1586 was no longer the city of three cultures it had been before 1492. The expulsions and conversions had remade the city. And yet the painting is saturated with that layered past — a Greek painter, working in Spanish, painting Byzantine elongation into Renaissance naturalism, commissioned by a Catholic church, for a city that had been simultaneously Christian, Muslim, and Jewish. Toledo carries its history in its stones, and El entierro carries Toledo.

As a Learning Object
This painting rewards slow looking. It offers multiple entry points — portraiture, theology, composition, history, local identity — and asks the viewer to hold them simultaneously. It is not a work that explains itself.
It functions as a model for what place-based learning can do: to understand this painting, you need to stand in Toledo. The church is small. The painting fills a wall. The scale changes what you see.


                  
                  Toledo and the course 
                  
                

The visit to Toledo on Saturday, 10 May, brought the group here. Standing in front of El entierro is a different experience from seeing it reproduced. The compression of the space, the candlelight, the proximity of the painting — these are part of the work. They cannot be digitised. See Toledo — The City of Three Cultures and Place-based Learning.



Connections
See El caballero de la mano en el pecho, La Anunciación, La Crucifixión, Toledo — The City of Three Cultures, Place-based Learning, Fiat Humanitas.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Felipe IV.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Felipe IV
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Diego Velázquez, Felipe IV, c. 1653–1657. Oil on canvas, 69 × 56 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The young Philip IV of Spain in formal court dress. One of several portraits Velázquez painted of the king across decades, this early work establishes the visual language of royal self-presentation: erect posture, controlled expression, the body used as an instrument of power.

Pose as Political Language
The portrait is not a record of a person. It is a construction of a role.
The king’s posture, dress, and expression are designed to communicate authority, stability, and legitimacy to anyone who encounters the image — whether in the palace, in reproduction, or in diplomatic contexts. The individual is subsumed into the office.
This is official representation at its most deliberate: the image does not show how the king is, but how power requires the king to appear.

Connections in the Course
The gap between the person and the role connects to ongoing discussions of representation and framing: whose image circulates, who controls its production, and what interests that image serves. See Las Meninas, La familia de Carlos IV, and What is truth in 2026?.
The portrait tradition also provides a historical anchor for contemporary questions about digital self-presentation and the politics of the profile image.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Fra Angelico.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Fra Angelico
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Dominican friar and painter, c. 1395–1455, Florence. Fra Angelico worked within a devotional tradition but brought to it a quality of spiritual clarity and compositional intelligence that has influenced painters across five centuries.
La Anunciación in the Prado is one of his most complete works and a key example of early Renaissance spatial organisation and theological programme. The garden of Eden appears in the left panel — the Fall is depicted alongside the Annunciation, the beginning of salvation.
See Highlights of Spanish Art.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Goya.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Francisco de Goya
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, Autorretrato, c. 1797–1800. Oil on canvas. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es
Francisco de Goya y Lucientes (1746–1828). Spanish painter, court artist to Carlos IV, and one of the sharpest political witnesses in the history of Western art.

Goya as War Reporter
Goya anticipated modern photojournalism — not by documenting events literally, but by capturing their emotional and political truth. He did not seek the heroic. He sought the human.
His work does not glorify. It witnesses, denounces, humanises victims, and documents violence as a system rather than an incident. In this, he stands alongside Picasso as a forerunner of the war correspondent — a recorder of what power does to bodies.

Central Works in This Context

El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid — chaos, not heroism
El 3 de mayo en Madrid — no victors, no heroes; the face given only to the condemned
La familia de Carlos IV — courtly portraiture that barely conceals its own irony


Connections

Picasso — the line from Goya to Picasso is direct: both refuse epic narratives of war
Guernica — Picasso’s inheritance of Goya’s refusal
Prado — where the works were seen
Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia — where the inheritance was completed


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Guernica.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Guernica
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Pablo Picasso, Guernica, 1937. Oil on canvas, 349 × 776 cm. Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía, Madrid. © Succession Picasso / VEGAP, Madrid. Image: Museo Reina Sofía, educational use.

What It Is
Guernica is a response to the bombing of the Basque town of Guernica on 26 April 1937, carried out by Nazi German and Fascist Italian forces in support of Franco during the Spanish Civil War.
Picasso was not present. He built the painting from reports, newspapers, photographs, and indirect testimony. It does not attempt to document the event literally. It constructs emotional and civilisational truth.

The Visual Language
The expressionist distortion is not a stylistic choice — it is the logic of the work. Fragmented bodies, animals in agony, silent screams, spatial collapse: the destruction of form mirrors the destruction of humanity.
The palette is monochrome: grey, black, white. The absence of colour is the absence of life.

The Inheritance of Goya
The line from Goya is direct and deliberate.
As in El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid and El 3 de mayo en Madrid, Guernica rejects heroism, refuses epic narrative, and shows war as chaos, mutilation, and dehumanisation. Modern art becomes a form of moral witness.

The Post-Colonial Extension
The work did not remain enclosed within its European context. African Guernica by Dumile Feni displaces the narrative — the suffering represented by Picasso becomes a dialogue with colonialism, apartheid, and structural violence across different geographies.

“History doesn’t repeat itself, it does rhyme.”


In the Course
Seen during Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia, Saturday, 9 May 2026 — the final day of the programme. Guernica functioned as a point of arrival: art as testimony, as denunciation, as memory. See Fiat Humanitas.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Hieronymus Bosch.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Hieronymus Bosch
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Netherlandish painter, c. 1450–1516, known for his complex, visionary imagery combining theological allegory, folk tradition, satire and what reads, to modern eyes, as surrealism five centuries before the movement.
The Spanish royal collection acquired Bosch’s work early, and the Prado holds the largest and most significant collection outside the Netherlands. Tríptico del Jardín de las delicias (The Garden of Earthly Delights), c. 1490–1510, is his most celebrated work and one of the most analysed paintings in European art. Three panels move from paradise to sin to hell, populated by figures and creatures at a scale that rewards hours of looking.
Bosch’s work has no obvious modern parallel. Its closest contemporary relatives are graphic novels, video games and fever dreams.
See Highlights of Spanish Art.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Jacopo Tintoretto.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Jacopo Tintoretto
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Venetian painter, 1518–1594, known for his dramatic light, dynamic composition and an approach to biblical narrative that emphasised physical energy and spatial complexity.
El Lavatorio (The Washing of Feet), c. 1548–1549, in the Prado is a characteristic example: the moment depicted is intimate and spiritual, but the spatial construction is theatrical and expansive, with figures receding into deep perspective and light cutting across the scene with unusual force.
See Highlights of Spanish Art.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/La Anunciación.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: La Anunciación
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
El Greco (Doménikos Theotokópoulos), La Anunciación, 1576. Oil on canvas, 117 × 98 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the son of God. The scene is set in a loggia of classical columns and arched vaults; a garden opens to the left. Light enters from multiple sources and is carefully distributed across the surface.

Geometric Certainty as Emotional Register
Fra Angelico’s composition is built on mathematical precision: the columns establish rhythm, the arches frame each figure, the tile floor creates perspectival depth. The geometry is not cold — it is reassuring. It produces a sense of ordered meaning, of an event that belongs to an intelligible universe.
The light, distributed evenly, makes every surface readable. Nothing is hidden in shadow.

Multimodality and Visual Design
The painting is a study in how visual design communicates before content does: proportion, rhythm, colour, and spatial organisation all carry meaning independently of the depicted scene.
This connects to principles of instructional design: the structure of a learning environment (its visual logic, spatial arrangement, and pacing) shapes the learner’s experience before a word is read.

Connections in the Course
The careful integration of geometry, colour, and narrative connects to Universal Design for Learning — the idea that multiple means of representation are not an accommodation but a design principle. See also The Role of Cognitive Science in Multisensory Learning and Why use a multi-sensory approach?.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/La Crucifixión.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: La Crucifixión
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
El Greco (Doménikos Theotokópoulos), La Crucifixión, 1597–1600. Oil on canvas, 312 × 169 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
Christ on the cross, set against a dark and turbulent sky. The figure is elongated in El Greco’s characteristic manner — beyond anatomical proportion, toward spiritual intensity. The surrounding figures (Mary, John, Mary Magdalene) respond with contained grief.

Representation Is Not Reproduction
El Greco’s bodies do not follow the rules of classical anatomy. The proportions are deliberately distorted, the light is not natural, the space is not perspectivally coherent.
And yet the painting is immediately, viscerally legible. The distortion serves the truth of the experience: the elongation of the body communicates vertical aspiration, elevation, suffering that exceeds the physical.
This is one of the clearest demonstrations in the Prado that representation is not the same as reproduction. The painter’s choices — which rules to follow, which to break — are arguments about what is most real.

Connections in the Course
The gap between anatomical accuracy and expressive truth connects to teaching practice: transmitting information faithfully is not the same as producing understanding. What we omit, distort, or emphasise in a lesson is always a set of decisions about what matters most.
See Active Methodologies, Curation, and the recurring discussion of what it means to design a learning experience rather than simply deliver content.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/La familia de Carlos IV.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: La familia de Carlos IV
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, La familia de Carlos IV, 1800–1801. Oil on canvas, 280 × 336 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The royal family of Charles IV, posed in formal portrait. Fourteen figures. Goya himself appears in the background at the left, brush in hand — a deliberate echo of Las Meninas.

The Question Underneath the Surface
The painting is technically dazzling and quietly subversive.
Official royal portraiture was an instrument of legitimacy: the family rendered radiant, powerful, unified. Goya complies with the commission — and does something else. The figures are rendered with unflinching realism: the King soft and slightly vacant, the Queen dominant and forward-thrusting, the children candid and unposed. There is no idealisation.
Whether Goya intended this as critique or simply as truthful observation is a question the painting refuses to answer.

Representation and Power
The work opens a conversation about who commissions images, what images are expected to say, and the distance between intended meaning and received meaning. Official representation is always a negotiation between power and the one given the tools to record it.
This connects to Las Meninas, AI Literacy, and What is truth in 2026? — the recurring question of who frames the image and what the frame conceals.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Las Meninas.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Las Meninas
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Diego Velázquez, Las Meninas, 1656. Oil on canvas, 318 × 276 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Is
Diego Velázquez paints himself painting. At the centre of the composition stands the young Infanta Margarita, surrounded by her ladies-in-waiting (the meninas). In the background, a mirror reflects the King and Queen — the subjects of the portrait Velázquez is in the process of making. The viewer occupies the position of the royal couple.
The result: a painting about who is looking, who is being looked at, and who controls the frame.

The Question It Asks
Las Meninas does not resolve the question of its own representation. It suspends it.
Who is the subject of the painting? The Infanta? The royal couple reflected in the mirror? The painter himself? The viewer standing where the sitters would have stood?
The work is a sustained interrogation of observation, framing, and power. It refuses to assign a single stable point of view.

Connections in the Course
The painting connects directly to media literacy: the idea that every image has a frame, every frame has an author, and every author has a position. What we see is always constructed by someone. See What is truth in 2026? and Healthy scepticism.
The question of the invisible observer — who controls what is represented — runs through AI Literacy, Arte contemporânea pós-1975, and the week’s recurring discussion of representation and power.
Velázquez’s self-insertion into the frame also connects to reflective practice: the practitioner visible within their own work, not pretending to invisible objectivity.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Las tres Gracias.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Las tres Gracias
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Peter Paul Rubens, Las tres Gracias, 1635. Oil on panel, 221 × 181 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
Three female figures — the Three Graces of classical mythology — in a circular embrace. The composition turns on itself: no figure is frontal, each connects to the next, the group forms a closed loop.

The Compositional Logic
The circularity is the work’s central argument. Harmony is not hierarchy; it is reciprocity. Each figure supports and is supported by the others. The garland they hold together is an extension of that logic.
Rubens’ characteristic luminous flesh and dynamic line give the figures weight and movement simultaneously — presence without stasis.

Connections in the Course
The circular composition connects to cooperative learning structures: interdependence, mutual support, no single figure dominating. The group as something greater than the sum of its parts.
See Cooperative Learning, Positive Interdependence, and the pedagogical value of structures that produce relational learning.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Peter Paul Rubens.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Peter Paul Rubens
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Flemish Baroque painter, 1577–1640, and one of the most prolific and influential artists of the seventeenth century. Rubens worked across genre, scale and medium with remarkable facility, and his studio produced works for clients across Europe.
The Prado holds several major works. Las tres Gracias (c. 1635) is among his most celebrated — a painting of abundance, sensory pleasure and classical reference that also carries his personal mark: one of the three figures bears the features of his young wife.
The Judgement of Paris presents the mythological contest with characteristic Baroque energy, the goddesses rendered with the same physical presence as the world around them.
See Highlights of Spanish Art.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Picasso.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Pablo Picasso
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Pablo Ruiz Picasso (1881–1973). Spanish painter and sculptor, co-founder of Cubism. In this garden, he appears through two lenses: the artist as witness, and the artist as someone who never stopped being a child.

“Every child is an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.”


Picasso as War Reporter
Picasso did not witness the bombing of Guernica directly. He built Guernica from reports, newspapers, photographs, and indirect testimony. Yet the painting does not try to document the event literally. It constructs emotional and civilisational truth.
The expressionist distortion — fragmented bodies, animals in agony, silent screams, broken spatial logic — translates the collapse of humanity itself. In this, Picasso stands alongside Goya: both refuse the epic narrative of war, both make violence visible by refusing to make it beautiful.

What “Remaining an Artist” Means
The quote became one of the recurring anchors of the week. To remain an artist is to remain:

curious
experimental
playful
open to interpretation
sensitive to what is in front of you

It is the opposite of rigidity. It connects directly to Notes on Unlearning — the course’s implicit argument that good teaching requires unlearning the assumption that we already know how things should be done.

Connections

Goya — shared lineage as witnesses of political violence
Guernica — the central work
African Guernica — the post-colonial extension of Guernica’s inheritance
Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Rogier van der Weyden.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Rogier van der Weyden
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Flemish painter, c. 1400–1464, and one of the most influential artists of the Northern Renaissance. His work is characterised by emotional intensity, detailed drapery, and a precise attention to grief.
El Descendimiento (The Descent from the Cross), c. 1435, is the masterpiece of the Prado’s Flemish collection. The painting depicts the removal of Christ’s body from the cross with extraordinary psychological precision: each figure’s grief is individualised and yet part of a collective choreography of mourning.
See Highlights of Spanish Art.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Sancho Panza.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Sancho Panza
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Squire to Don Quixote in Cervantes’s novel. The grounded, practical, self-interested counterweight to Quixote’s idealism — and, by the end, something much more than that.

The Character
Sancho begins as a peasant farmer who joins Quixote expecting material reward: a governorship, an island, advancement for his family. He is suspicious of abstractions, attentive to his stomach, loyal in a matter-of-fact way that has nothing to do with romance.
His speech is full of proverbs. He carries the accumulated wisdom of ordinary life and is not ashamed of it.

The Transformation
The novel’s central moral movement is the slow convergence between Quixote and Sancho. The idealist learns something of pragmatism; the pragmatist learns something of vision. By the final chapters, Sancho is arguing for the chivalric project his master has abandoned.
This exchange — two people genuinely changed by sustained contact — is a model of what learning relationships, at their best, produce. Neither party exits unchanged.

Connections in the Course
See Don Quixote, Cervantes, Context4Content, and the broader theme of mutual transformation in Carl Rogers — Experiential Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/The Judgement of Paris.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Judgement of Paris
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Peter Paul Rubens, The Judgement of Paris, 1638–1639. Oil on panel, 199 × 379 cm. Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Shows
The myth of Paris, prince of Troy, choosing the most beautiful among three goddesses: Juno, Minerva and Venus. Paris awards Venus the golden apple. The choice that sets the Trojan War in motion.

Subjectivity as Subject
The painting’s core problem is that Paris must judge — and any judgement of this kind is irreducibly subjective, partial, and consequential.
Rubens does not adjudicate. He shows the moment of decision itself: three figures presented for evaluation, the judge looking, the choice not yet made. The painting holds the viewer inside that moment of interpretive responsibility.

Connections in the Course
The myth connects to critical thinking: the capacity to evaluate competing options without assuming any single criterion is universal or neutral. Who sets the standard? Whose definition of value counts?
This links to Healthy scepticism, AI Literacy (training data reflects whose judgements?), and the broader theme of interpretation as an active, positioned act.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/Tríptico del Jardín de las delicias.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Tríptico del Jardín de las delicias
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Hieronymus Bosch, El jardín de las delicias, c. 1490–1510. Oil on panel, 220 × 389 cm (open). Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid. museodelprado.es

What It Is
A triptych in three panels: Eden on the left, an imagined world of earthly pleasure at the centre, Hell on the right. The work is painted at a scale that demands slow navigation. There is no correct reading sequence, no single interpretive line.

The Pedagogical Explosive
The Garden of Earthly Delights is what inquiry-based learning looks like as an object.
Every centimetre of the surface contains something unexpected. A fish carrying a human. A bird the size of a building. Groups engaged in activity that resists categorisation. The eye moves not toward resolution but toward further questions.
It does not explain itself. It invites — and demands — extended, active attention.

Non-Linear Visual Hypertext
The triptych operates as a non-linear structure: you enter anywhere, follow threads, return, discover something missed. The experience is closer to hypertext navigation than to linear reading.
This makes it an unusually productive model for thinking about inquiry-based learning and place-based learning: knowledge as a territory to be explored rather than a sequence to be followed.

Connections in the Course
The Garden connects to Active Methodologies, Inquiry-Based Learning, and the discussion of learning through exploration and productive difficulty.
It also connects to the week’s recurring question of imagination: what Bosch constructed in paint in 1490 required no external prompt — only the willingness to follow an associative, exploratory logic. See Picasso’s question about what childhood knows.

Part of Highlights of Spanish Art, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Art and Museums/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Museums & Visual Culture
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Museums & Visual Culture
Art was not decoration in this course. It was methodology.
The paintings appeared before they were named. A quiz on the first evening asked about Goya — not as art history, but as a war reporter. It was El 3 de mayo en Madrid, in Madrid, the city where the painting was made. That coincidence was not accidental. The coordinator confirmed it on Thursday morning: the programme had always been built this way.
By the end of the week, Las Meninas had appeared in a quiz, a Padlet activity, a Prado visit, a Portfolio Development mind map and a slide deck. Not as an illustration of visual literacy. As a structure for thinking about who is watching, who is watched, and what is happening outside the frame. In a classroom, those questions are never purely aesthetic.
This section collects the works, the artists and the cultural spaces that became part of the week’s argument.

Museo del Prado
The guided visit on Wednesday afternoon — Highlights of Spanish Art — moved through fourteen works across six centuries. The sequence was curatorial, not chronological: a slow argument about how images construct meaning.
Artists and works:

Fra Angelico: certainty, gold, the sacred as legible — the week’s starting point
Rogier van der Weyden — El Descendimiento: grief made sculptural, emotion as compositional force
Hieronymus Bosch — Tríptico del Jardín de las delicias: a world without stable ground, meaning that refuses to resolve
El Greco — El caballero de la mano en el pecho, La Anunciación, La Crucifixión, El entierro del conde de Orgaz: the painter who arrived from elsewhere and defined the place
Jacopo Tintoretto — El Lavatorio: perspective as power, who kneels and who does not
Peter Paul Rubens — Las tres Gracias, The Judgement of Paris: the body as argument, cooperation as circular form
Diego Velázquez — Felipe IV, Las Meninas: the question that ends the visit and opens everything else
Goya — El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid, El 3 de mayo en Madrid, La familia de Carlos IV: witness, irony, the refusal to glorify


Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía
Saturday afternoon. Self-guided, with an audioguide as scaffold. Four to five hours after the course had officially ended.

Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia: the afternoon in full
Avant-Garde Territories: the ruptures that made the twentieth century visible
Guernica: the room the course had been building toward since Sunday evening
African Guernica: Dumile Feni, 1967 — what the image looks like from elsewhere
Arte contemporânea pós-1975: what came after


Toledo
El Greco spent most of his working life in Toledo. The visit to his city — and to the church where El entierro del conde de Orgaz hangs in a darkened room — completed a thread that had run since Wednesday.

Literary & Cultural Figures
The week’s reference points were not only visual.

Cervantes — “La pluma es la lengua del alma.” Writing as the externalisation of thought.
Don Quixote and Sancho Panza: the idealist and the pragmatist, the two figures every educator navigates between.
Picasso — “Every child is born an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.”


Key Themes

Curation: the act of selecting, sequencing and contextualising as pedagogical design
Visual Learning: not using images, but developing the capacity to read them
Place-based Learning: the museum as learning environment, not destination
Multisensory Learning: how embodied encounter with art differs from description
Context4Content: culture at both registers — the canonical and the everyday


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Context4content/Fernando II de Aragón and Isabel I de Castilla.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Fernando II de Aragón and Isabel I de Castilla
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The Catholic Monarchs of Spain, ruling jointly from 1474 (Castile) and 1479 (Aragon).

Their union created the political entity that would become Spain. Their reign produced the completion of the Reconquista (1492), the expulsion of the Jews (1492), the expulsion of the Moors (1502), the sponsorship of Columbus’s voyages, and the foundation of the Spanish Inquisition.
The same year — 1492 — that marks the arrival of Spanish power in the Americas also marks the forced departure of communities who had shaped Toledo — The City of Three Cultures for centuries.
Power and cultural loss arrived together.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Context4content/Gastronomy notes — Madrid as Memory, Ritual and Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Gastronomy notes — Madrid as Memory, Ritual and Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Food as pedagogical space. These are notes from eating in Madrid — not as tourism but as attention.

Pan con Tomate
A piece of toasted bread, rubbed with raw tomato, finished with olive oil and salt. Simple, direct, irreducible. The pleasure is in the quality of each element and the clarity of the combination.
There is a pedagogical argument here about reduction: complexity is not always depth. Sometimes the discipline of simplicity produces something more nourishing than elaboration.

Churros
Eaten standing, at the counter of a churrería, with hot chocolate thick enough to coat a spoon. The ritual matters as much as the food. Shared posture, shared time, shared temperature. The embodied dimension of a social practice.

Marzipan in Toledo
Toledo’s marzipan is ground almonds and sugar, pressed into shapes that carry centuries of institutional memory. Tasted at the end of the Toledo visit, in the context of Marzipan - The Sweet Bread of Toledo legend, it became something other than a sweet: a lesson in how craft, crisis, and community produce culture.
The tasting was structured as a mindfulness exercise. See Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise.

The Neighbourhoods
Eating in Malasaña, Lavapiés, and La Latina is eating in three different cities. Each neighbourhood has its own rhythm, its own population, its own relationship to money and time. Madrid as a city is not singular — it is a federation of sensibilities.
Los Porfiados — a small restaurant in Lavapiés where the cooking was honest and the room was full of regulars. Food as neighbourhood institution.
Taberna Mariano — traditional, unassuming, exactly what it has always been. Stability as a form of quality.
El Matador — a recurring anchor for tapas and cañas. The kind of place that earns its loyalty through consistency.
El Mono de la Pila — an unexpected ceviche after a failed attempt at 8 Patas in Mercado Antón Martín. The best meals are often found through detour.
El Búho / La Buha — cheese and caramelised onion toast: informal, precise, unhurried.
A small ceramic kiki at Los Porfiados created an unexpected emotional bridge between home and travel. Objects as markers of belonging, even far from home.

The Pedagogical Argument
Gastronomy is not supplementary to the learning. It is part of it.
Sharing food is one of the oldest human learning environments: the conditions of trust, relaxation, and reciprocity that a good meal creates are precisely the conditions that Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis identifies as necessary for acquisition.
Eating together lowers the filter.

Connections in the Course
See Why use a multi-sensory approach?, Place-based Learning, Toledo — The City of Three Cultures, and Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Context4content/Marzipan - The Sweet Bread of Toledo.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Sweet Bread of Toledo
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A legend. A lesson in how culture is made under constraint.

The Story
The year is 1212. The Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa has just ended. The Christian armies of Alfonso VIII have defeated the Almohad forces in a decisive engagement that shifts the balance of the Iberian Peninsula. Toledo is left depleted — supplies exhausted, the city strained by the cost of war.
In the Convent of San Clemente, the nuns face a practical problem: there is little flour left, but there are almonds and sugar. From necessity and ingenuity, they grind the almonds, combine them with sugar, and press the mixture into shapes.
Marzipan.

Culture Made from Constraint
The legend is almost certainly apocryphal — the actual origins of marzipan are contested and likely older, with roots in Persian and Arab confectionery traditions that arrived in Europe through exactly the kind of cultural exchange Toledo itself represents.
But the story carries something true: craft is often born from scarcity. The constraint produces the form. The limitation forces the invention.

The Pedagogical Echo
This is what good instructional design frequently looks like: working with the materials available, in the time available, for the learners present — and producing something that is not a compromise but a genuine creation.
Constraints are not the opposite of creativity. They are frequently its condition.

Connections in the Course
See Toledo — The City of Three Cultures, Gastronomy notes — Madrid as Memory, Ritual and Learning, Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise, and the broader theme of Place-based Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Context4content/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Context4Content
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Context4Content
Context precedes content. Experience precedes explanation. Observation precedes abstraction.
Context4Content is not one methodology among others. It is the frame that made the whole week coherent.
Its premise: learning does not need to be applied to reality. It is constructed from reality. The city, the museum, the meal, the market, the school corridor, the painting on the wall of a bar — none of these were illustrations of content. They were the content. The frameworks that were named in the classroom — Place-based Learning, Situated Learning, Experiential Learning, Multisensory Learning — were not being prepared for eventual application. They were already in operation in the street outside.
The coordinator confirmed it on Thursday morning: the programme had always been built this way.

The Framework
The full methodological description lives in Context4Content (Frameworks). What follows here is the lived version — the section of the garden where Context4Content is not described but demonstrated.
The framework operates through a cycle: immersion, observation, interpretation, articulation, reflection. Each pass through the cycle deepens both the understanding of place and the understanding of pedagogy. The educator’s role shifts from content deliverer to learning curator. The learner shifts from receiver to active reader of context.

The City as Curriculum
Madrid
Madrid entered the programme before the programme began. El Rastro on Sunday morning — before any session, before any framework had been named — was already a field project. The market, the pan con tomate, the streets of La Latina: an ethnographic encounter with no task card.
Monday afternoon formalised it. Images of Madrid was a walking tour structured as a quiz across eight locations: Plaza Mayor, Restaurante Botín, the 2 de Mayo plaque, the Royal Palace, Puerta del Sol, Km0. Participants documented through Seesaw, navigated through Genially. The tour was designed to be non-linear: different routes, different experiences, the same city differently read.
Monday evening offered something no one had planned. Sala Equis — a cinema bar in Madrid, discovered by the group — had Metropolis projected on the wall above the bar. Silent. The central line of the film on loop: The mediator between the head and the hands must be the heart. Nobody had organised it. Nobody could have.
Tuesday afternoon was listed in the programme as autonomous portfolio work. What it became was Casa de Campo. A park. Space, movement, deceleration. No task, no output. The morning’s frameworks continued, quietly, at a different pace.
Gastronomy ran across the entire week as its own thread. Bocadillos de calamares as bonus points. Cañas after the walking tour. Churros and hot chocolate at San Ginés. Restaurante Botín. The ritual of eating as a pedagogical act: food as identity system, sensory learning through taste and memory, the discipline of simplicity that produces something more nourishing than elaboration.
Toledo
Friday afternoon. The city as palimpsest.
Toledo — The City of Three Cultures holds nine centuries of layered civilisation in its streets. Christian, Jewish, and Moorish traditions in the same buildings, the same distances, the same silences between them. The coexistence was neither frictionless nor equal — it was contested, negotiated, and eventually ended by force. But it produced a city that is architecturally and culturally plural in ways that remain visible today.
Toledo explained Context4Content without using the term.
The marzipan tasted in a morning classroom session was recognised again in an actual shop window. The marzipan is a recipe as cultural history: almonds, sugar, a convent, an absent ingredient, a gesture of survival. Its origin story is inseparable from the expulsions of 1492 — the same year Fernando II de Aragón and Isabel I de Castilla completed the Reconquista, expelled the Jewish and Moorish communities, and sponsored Columbus. Power and cultural loss arrived together in the same calendar year.
El Greco spent most of his working life in Toledo. The visit to the church where El entierro del conde de Orgaz hangs in a darkened room completed a thread that had run since Wednesday at the Prado.

The Museums
Highlights of Spanish Art at the Museo del Prado on Wednesday afternoon was Context4Content as curatorial argument. Fourteen works across six centuries, sequenced not chronologically but thematically: a slow argument about representation, perspective and the act of looking. The meeting point was the statue of Goya at the entrance — which was itself the first work of art.
By the end of the week, Las Meninas had appeared in a quiz, a Padlet activity, the Prado visit, a portfolio mind map and a slide deck. Not as an illustration of visual literacy. As a structure for thinking about who is watching, who is watched, and what happens outside the frame.
Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia on Saturday was self-guided, with an audioguide as scaffold. The course had officially ended at midday. Four to five hours followed across all floors: Avant-Garde Territories, Guernica, the African Guernica by Dumile Feni, Contemporary Art post-1975. Context4Content, after the end.

The School
IES Príncipe Felipe on Thursday morning took the frameworks into an institutional field site. Classrooms, laboratories and corridors as objects of study. UDL visible in the spatial layout. CLIL in the multilingual encounter with the school director. Cooperative learning in the student wifi programme. The frameworks made architectural.

Related Frameworks
Context4Content draws on and connects to:

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Dissemination/Disseminare.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Disseminare
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Italian for “to scatter seeds.” The etymological root of the concept of Dissemination in the Erasmus+ context — and the more honest word for what sharing professional practice actually means.

What It Means
Disseminare is not:

imposing
centralising
normalising

It is:

scattering
launching
cultivating
allowing growth

The metaphor matters. Seeds do not grow where they are thrown — they grow where conditions allow. Dissemination, understood this way, is an act of trust in others’ contexts, not a delivery of conclusions.

Rhizomatic Thinking
The metaphor connects to the Erasmus+ Spirit as a rhizomatic ecosystem. Small experiences of mobility circulate, connect, and transform other practices — not through direct transmission, but through the kind of lateral, unpredictable growth that rhizomes produce.
Disseminare is not broadcast. It is seeding.

Pedagogical Implication
Dissemination understood this way is not bureaucratic compliance. It is civic and professional responsibility — making the invisible work of teachers visible to the world that benefits from it.
See Dissemination, Dissemination Strategy, Dissemination vs Communication.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Dissemination/Dissemination Strategy.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Dissemination Strategy
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Developed during Dissemination, Saturday, 9 May 2026.

What to Disseminate

“Our experience of Europe — and of the world.”

Not “an activity”. A lived European experience. The 9+1 countries represented in the room — Bulgaria, Croatia, Portugal, France, Czech Republic, Germany, Italy, Slovakia, Greece, Spain — were not just a list of origins. They were Europe as a relational experience: co-presence, comparison, cultural negotiation, and shared pedagogical practice.
What is worth sharing is not the programme. It is the understanding that emerges from being in it.

Who to Reach
Dissemination is most powerful when it crosses the usual boundaries of professional circulation.
Inside the school: students, staff, headteacher, school board, parent association.
Beyond the school: educational authorities, professional journals and websites, local radio, social media, the general public.
The “Do you have 5 minutes?” approach — a short, structured conversation with a colleague — is a low-barrier starting point. Not every dissemination act needs to be a conference presentation.

How to Share

Lessons that embed what was learned
eTwinning and online community participation
Conferences, seminars, school meetings
E-newsletters, reports, articles
Handbooks

One proposition that emerged: Making a Handbook of Good Education in Europe — a pedagogical manifesto in document form. To document practice is to produce professional culture.

Connections

Dissemination
Disseminare
European Dimension in Education

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Dissemination/Dissemination.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Dissemination
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
“Dissemination means to spread widely.”
Erasmus+ Programme Guide

Dissemination in the Erasmus+ context is often described in bureaucratic terms — a reporting requirement, an output obligation. The Saturday session reframed it entirely.
Dissemination is not sharing results. It is legitimising the work teachers do — making visible what is otherwise closed inside classrooms and invisible to the world that depends on it.

“Teachers make grandiose things but we’re not aware.” (Eduardo, course participant)


What Dissemination Is Not
It is not:

sharing deliverables
fulfilling bureaucratic Erasmus+ requirements
presenting products at the end of a project

These are the forms dissemination takes when it has been reduced to compliance. They are not its purpose.

Dissemination vs Communication
Two related but distinct ideas:
Dissemination — making practice available to the professional and scientific community. Peer-to-peer. Within education.
Communication — making education visible to civil society. Beyond the profession. Outward.
In most schools, professional knowledge circulates internally — among teachers, within the building, through documents no one outside reads. The course argued that education must become publicly visible. This is both a professional responsibility and a political act.
See Disseminare for the etymological and conceptual root.

Connections

Dissemination
Dissemination Strategy
European Dimension in Education


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Dissemination/Micro-dissemination.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Micro-dissemination
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Educational change does not always begin with a conference or a publication. It begins in the corridor. In the staffroom. In a five-minute conversation.

The Informal Dimension
The “Do you have 5 minutes?” approach is the most accessible form of dissemination: a structured, low-barrier conversation with a colleague about something you learned, something that worked, something worth sharing.
No slides required. No institutional approval. No audience larger than one.

You Are the Intellectual
One of the session’s most direct challenges to participants: many teachers do not think of themselves as producers of knowledge. They see themselves as curriculum executors — people who implement what others design.
The session pushed back on this. Hard.
A teacher who reflects carefully on their practice, documents what works, and shares it with others is doing something intellectually significant. The knowledge they produce is grounded in real classrooms, real learners, real constraints. It is not less valuable than academic research — it is differently valuable, and often more immediately useful.

TikTok — Tic-tac — Tuk-tuk

Even said playfully, the point stands: contemporary dissemination demands new formats. A one-minute video, a social media post, a newsletter — these are legitimate channels for professional knowledge. Refusing to use them because they feel informal is how professional knowledge stays trapped in rooms it never leaves.

Connections in the Course
See Dissemination, Dissemination Strategy, Disseminare, and European Dimension in Education.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes
