Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Casa de Campo.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Casa de Campo
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The afternoon listed portfolio work. What it became was a walk through Casa de Campo, the largest public park in Madrid. A former royal hunting estate, now a vast green corridor at the city’s edge.
No task. No reflection prompt. No output. Just the city at a different pace.
After two days of dense social and cognitive intensity, the walk offered something the programme had not explicitly scheduled: space for consolidation. The conversations were quieter. The observation more lateral. The thinking less directed. The session continued, at a different pace, in a different register.

Palacio Real viewed from Casa de Campo | Day 3 © Filipe Gama
See Place-based Learning, Experiential Learning and Multisensory Learning.

Part of Programme, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/El Matador.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El Matador
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Context4Content, Lowercase
The evening was cañas and tapas at El Matador, near Sol.
The coordinator had confirmed in the morning what the week had been built on: culture at two registers. The Prado and the plaza. The paintings and the semáforos. The guided visits and the unguided evenings. Both intentional. Both part of the Context4Content design.
El Matador near Sol was the lowercase end of the same sentence that had started at the Prado.

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/El Rastro.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: El Rastro
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Sunday, 3 May 2026. La Latina, Madrid.
The course had not yet begun. The Portuguese group had found each other the night before, the way Erasmus+ groups always do, through a message thread and a vague plan for breakfast. Sunday morning brought pan con tomate at La Latina and then, naturally, El Rastro.
El Rastro is Madrid’s oldest and largest street market, running every Sunday through the streets below La Latina. It is loud, dense and entirely uncurated. Stalls selling second-hand tools, vinyl records, vintage clothing, ceramics, phone cases and objects of uncertain origin stretch down Calle de la Ribera de Curtidores and spill into the surrounding streets. The crowd is a mix of locals, tourists, collectors and people who simply came for the walk.

El Rastro, Sunday morning | Day 1 © André Almeida
Nobody planned a lesson. But the learning started immediately.
The market created conditions for exactly the kind of language use that CLIL and Scaffolding theory describe but rarely gets to observe in its natural state. Prices negotiated in Spanish by non-speakers. Greetings exchanged through gesture when words ran out. Signs read and misread. Directions asked and partially understood. The communicative pressure was real, low-stakes and productive. This is what BICS looks like when it is actually working: informal, spontaneous, embedded in context, driven by genuine need.
But El Rastro also offered something less visible. Walking through a Sunday market in a city that is not your own requires a particular kind of attention. You read the space differently. You notice what is sold and what that reveals about who lives here. You notice the informal economy, the sustainability logic of reuse, the way heritage and everyday life share the same street without ceremony. That is CALP territory: intercultural competence, urban literacy, critical observation of public space.
The market sits in one of Madrid’s oldest neighbourhoods. La Latina carries layers of history that the walk surfaced without announcing them. The cobblestones, the medieval street pattern, the churches alongside the market stalls. Context4Content  before the term had been introduced. The city as document, the street as curriculum.
There is something worth noting about beginning this way, informally, without structure, before the programme started. Place-based Learning does not require a task card. It requires presence and attention. El Rastro offered both, freely, on a Sunday morning.
The pan con tomate was excellent.

References

BICS and CALP
CLIL
Scaffolding
Place-based Learning
Context4Content
Experiential Learning
Informal Learning
Cultural Immersion
Multimodal Literacy
European Dimension in Education


Field Learning Projects. Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Highlights of Spanish Art.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Highlights of Spanish Art
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis

The meeting point was the Goya statue, in Prado | Day 4 © André Almeida
The meeting point at 15:30 was the statue of Goya outside the Prado. The place to meet was also the first work of art.

Erasmus+ team at the Prado entrance | Day 4 © English Matters
The guided visit moved through fourteen works across six centuries. Fra Angelico, Hieronymus Bosch, Rogier van der Weyden, El Greco, Tintoretto, Peter Paul Rubens, Diego Velázquez, Goya. The sequence was not alphabetical or chronological. It was curatorial: a slow movement through questions about how images construct meaning, what representation conceals as much as it reveals, and how the act of looking is itself a cognitive and ethical act.

Erasmus+ team inside the Prado, during a curated guide by Sofia (ES) | Day 4 © Team
Las Meninas waited near the end. The painting that asks who is watching, who is being watched, and what is happening outside the frame. By the time the group stood in front of it, those questions had been arriving for four days.

Sofia (ES) presented a remarkable curated storyline of artworks during the “Highlights of the Spanish Art” | Day 4 © Team
See Highlights of Spanish Art for the full sequence of works and their readings.

Field learning project. Prado, Madrid. Wednesday, 6 May 2026.
Works encountered: Goya, Diego Velázquez, El Greco, Rogier van der Weyden, Fra Angelico, Peter Paul Rubens, Hieronymus Bosch, Jacopo Tintoretto.

Museo del Prado — Guided Visit, Wednesday 6 May 2026
The meeting point was the statue of Goya at the main entrance of the Prado. Not an arbitrary choice of landmark. The programme had been building toward this moment since the quiz on Sunday evening, since the plaque in front of the Royal Palace on Monday afternoon, since the Goya puzzle on the UDL session screen that same morning.
Standing at the statue before entering, the figure of Goya himself became the first curatorial statement of the visit: not Goya the court painter, not Goya the celebrated portraitist of the aristocracy, but Goya the observer. The one who stayed.

The Visit as Curated Learning
The guided visit moved through fourteen works across six centuries. The sequence was not alphabetical, not strictly chronological, and not driven by room layout alone. It was a curatorial argument.
From Fra Angelico’s ordered light to Goya’s fractured darkness. From the geometry of transcendence to the chaos of the firing squad. Each work extended or complicated the previous one: how images construct space, how they direct the eye, how they produce emotion, what they conceal, what they reveal about the people who made them and the conditions in which they were made.
What the visit demonstrated was what the week had been arguing since Day 1: that context is content. The Prado’s collection did not illustrate the programme. It was the programme, delivered at a different scale and at a different pace.
Every work in the sequence required the same operations the sessions had been practising: observation, inference, interpretation, negotiation of meaning. The museum became an extension of the classroom without any of the classroom’s furniture.

The Works
Fra Angelico — The Annunciation (c. 1426)
A loggia. Two figures. The geometry is deliberate and transparent: columns, arches, a rational perspective that conducts the eye inward and upward. Light enters from the left and does not scatter. It arrives.
Fra Angelico constructs sacred space through architectural order. The viewer is placed. The composition does not ask you to wander. It asks you to attend. Blue for the divine and transcendent; gold for revelation; symmetry as an argument for the existence of meaning.
The work opens the visit by establishing what visual language does: it organises attention before it delivers content.

Rogier van der Weyden — The Descent from the Cross (c. 1435)
Ten figures, compressed into a shallow space that feels like a stage. The bodies of Christ and Mary mirror each other precisely — the same diagonal, the same lost consciousness, the same collapse. Van der Weyden used that echo to produce empathy structurally rather than sentimentally.
Grief made legible through form. The painting does not ask you to understand what is happening. It makes you feel the weight before you process the image.
The pedagogical resonance: learning that involves emotion is not softer than learning that does not. It is more durable.

El Greco — La Crucifixión (c. 1597–1600)
El Greco does not paint the body. He elongates it. The figures reach toward something the canvas cannot contain. The palette is cold and luminous: blues, silvers, a darkness that is not absence but presence of a different kind.
El Greco is after intensity, not accuracy. The distortion is the argument: that spiritual experience cannot be adequately represented through naturalistic proportion. Representation does not equal reproduction. The form is chosen for what it does to the viewer, not for how faithfully it records the subject.

El Greco — The Nobleman with his Hand on his Chest (c. 1580)
A man. Dark clothing, white ruff, one hand resting on his chest. The gesture carries honour, sincerity, an interior life disclosed through a single physical act.
The portrait is almost entirely a face and a hand. El Greco removes everything that is not essential to the subject’s self-presentation. What remains: dignity, restraint, and the question of what a person chooses to show and what they withhold.

Tintoretto — El Lavatorio (1548–1549)
Tintoretto refuses static composition. A point of flight pulls the scene toward the background and the canvas threatens to expand beyond its edges. Bodies at diagonals. Space that moves.
Everything in the painting appears to be in the act of becoming something else. The architectural space is not a room containing figures; it is a force field in which they are caught.

Hieronymus Bosch — The Garden of Earthly Delights (c. 1490–1510)
Three panels. Left: Eden. Centre: a world given over to pleasure, excess and strange invention. Right: Hell.
There is no correct way to read this work. That is not a failure of the painting. It is the painting’s method. The triptych is constructed to be navigated rather than read — the eye wanders, discovers, backtracks, misreads, reconsiders. Figures that seem comic become sinister. Patterns that seem decorative dissolve into violence.
El Bosco produced something that functions like a proto-hypertext: a non-linear visual structure that rewards slow, recursive attention. Every time you return, you find something you missed.

Diego Velázquez — Vulcan’s Forge (1630)
Apollo has just arrived in Vulcan’s workshop. The moment Velázquez chose to paint is the instant of revelation: every figure in the room reacting at a different speed, with a different knowledge, to news they did not expect.
Velázquez paints theatre, not mythology. The figures are working men interrupted, not allegorical types. The forge is a real forge. The faces are real faces.

Peter Paul Rubens — The Judgement of Paris (c. 1639)
Three goddesses in a landscape. A shepherd adjudicating beauty. The myth is a pretext; the painting is a study in bodies, fabric, movement, and the politics of desire.
The myth ends in the Trojan War. Rubens painted the moment before, when the choice still seemed like a game.

Diego Velázquez — Portrait of Philip IV (c. 1653–1654)
Armour. Baton. Formality held in every posture. Velázquez painted Philip IV many times across his career, each portrait a negotiation between the official demands of royal representation and what a painter of his acuity could not help but see.

Diego Velázquez — Las Meninas (1656)
The painting the visit had been moving toward.
A large canvas. A girl at its centre, surrounded by attendants, a dog, a dwarf, two figures in the background, a mirror on the back wall reflecting the king and queen, and, to the left, a painter who is painting something we cannot see.
The questions the work produces: Who is the subject? Who is the observer? Where is the viewer standing? What is outside the frame? What does the mirror show, and why? Who is being painted?
Every answer generates another question. The painting is constructed to make the act of looking conscious of itself. It does not allow passive viewing. It insists on your position as a viewer, your complicity in the act of representation, and the impossibility of neutral observation.
Las Meninas is, among other things, an argument about media literacy: that every image has a frame, a point of view, a choice about what to include and what to exclude, and an audience whose position the maker has already accounted for. The painting knows you are there. It has always known.

Francisco de Goya — Charles IV of Spain and His Family (1800–1801)
The royal family, assembled in formation. The painter, half-visible in the shadows to the left, is present in his own composition — an echo of Velázquez in Las Meninas that Goya almost certainly intended.
Who controls representation? The sitter commissions. The painter looks. The painting is what happens in between.

Francisco de Goya — The Second of May 1808 (1814)
2 May 1808. Madrid. The popular uprising against Napoleon’s occupying forces.
Goya paints chaos rather than heroism. There is no compositional order, no legible centre, no single figure to admire. The resistance is collective, anonymous and desperate.
“Rather than glorifying war, it strips it bare. There are no winners, no saviour to admire. Only bloodshed and confusion.”
Goya was in Madrid. He saw the aftermath. He waited six years to paint what he had witnessed.

Francisco de Goya — The Third of May 1808 (1814)
A night execution. French soldiers, their backs to us, their rifles levelled. A kneeling man, his arms thrown wide, his white shirt luminous in the lantern light.
The Second of May shows the instant of confrontation. The Third shows what follows.
There are no soldiers’ faces. They have been deliberately denied individuality. They are a mechanism. The victims, by contrast, are specific. Goya chose who gets a face in this painting, and the choice is a moral argument.
This is what the quiz was pointing at on Sunday evening. This is what the plaque commemorated at the Royal Palace on Monday. This is what the UDL session had on screen that morning.

Peter Paul Rubens — The Three Graces (c. 1635)
The three figures hold each other, turn together, a circular composition with no fixed beginning or end. Harmony produced through interconnection. Beauty as movement, as relation, as something that exists between bodies rather than in any one of them.
After Goya’s darkness, something that circles and endures.

The Curatorial Logic
The arc ran from Fra Angelico’s certainty through Bosch’s impossibility through Velázquez’s careful observation to Goya’s refusal to comfort. The works chosen were not merely representative of Spanish art or Western painting. They were chosen to do something: to build a set of questions about representation, perspective, power and the act of looking that the programme had been asking in different registers all week.
The Prado did not supplement the course. On Wednesday afternoon, it was the course.

See Goya, Diego Velázquez, El Greco, Hieronymus Bosch, Fra Angelico, Peter Paul Rubens, Jacopo Tintoretto, Rogier van der Weyden, Las Meninas, El 3 de mayo en Madrid, El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid, Tríptico del Jardín de las delicias, Context4Content, Visual Learning, Fiat Humanitas, Place-based Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/IES Príncipe Felipe.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: IES Príncipe Felipe
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The school that had the week in its bones without knowing it.
IES Príncipe Felipe is a hybrid institution: secondary education, vocational training, artistic programmes, bilingual teaching, Bachillerato Internacional, and Erasmus+ placements, all under one roof. It was established in its current form in 1997, in Fuencarral–El Pardo, a socially heterogeneous neighbourhood that makes the school’s official discourse on inclusion legible against a real context rather than an aspirational one.
The visit moved through corridors and laboratories. Television production. Makeup and aesthetics. Social care. Audiovisual. The labs were not workshops appended to a real curriculum. They were the curriculum made spatial. Each room declared something about what the school considered worth learning: image-making, human care, body, craft, mediation. Competencies that Portuguese vocational education often treats as peripheral, here presented as central to the school’s identity.
The director did not speak English. The language coordinator mediated. What could have been an awkward administrative moment became, instead, a live demonstration of what the week had been teaching: that CLIL is not about anglicisation. It is about negotiated meaning, shared across language difference, carried by whoever in the room has the right bridge.
The “alumnos wifi” appeared in conversation: student volunteers who manage the school’s digital infrastructure, troubleshoot in classrooms, and support the technology the institution runs on. Not passive users. Co-managers. The phrase carried an entire pedagogy in two words.
Erasmus+ was not a programme the school ran. It was part of how the school described itself. The institutional text reads: “todos nuestros estudiantes deberían de tener una oportunidad europea.” Europe as pedagogical project, not administrative category.
The comparison with Portugal was not made out loud. But it was present in every room. The FP visible, equipped, proud, internationalised. Not marginal.

IES Príncipe Felipe
School Visit — Thursday, 7 May 2026
A school that was built on the same arguments the week had been making, without having attended the same course.
The IES Príncipe Felipe is a public secondary and vocational institution in Fuencarral–El Pardo, a socially heterogeneous district in north Madrid. It was formed in 1997 through the merger of a bachillerato institute and a vocational training centre. That origin is not a historical footnote. It is the institution’s architectural logic: the school has never treated academic and vocational education as separate tracks. They coexist under one roof, sharing infrastructure, culture and, critically, the same claim to legitimacy.

Team arrival at IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team

The Hybrid Model
The IES offers, simultaneously:

Secondary education (ESO and Bachillerato)
Bachillerato Internacional
Formación Profesional across multiple families: audiovisual production, aesthetics and beauty, social and community services, administration and finance
Bilingual teaching programme (from 2014/15)
Erasmus+ institutional programme
Innovation in Technology designation (from 2010/11)

What this means in practice: a student at the IES can move between an academic trajectory and a vocational one without changing schools, without crossing a symbolic boundary that in most European systems marks the difference between education for thinking and education for doing. The IES refuses that distinction structurally.

Pedagogia Espacializada — The Labs as Curriculum
The visit moved through specialist laboratories. Television production. Makeup, aesthetics and hairdressing. Social integration and early childhood education. Audiovisual production and lighting. Administration.
These rooms are not ancillary. They are the argument.
Each lab encodes something about what the school considers worth learning. Image-making, human care, the body, craft, technical mediation, social support: competencies that academic tradition has systematically undervalued, here treated as the core of a professional education. The space itself teaches. Students enter a lab and their posture changes, their language changes, their relationship to the task changes. This is what the working notes called “pedagogia espacializada”: the curriculum written into architecture.

School presentation, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team
The connection to Universal Design for Learning is direct. Multiple Means of Action and Expression are not an add-on in this building. They are the building. Students demonstrate what they know by making things, caring for people, managing equipment, producing images. The assessment follows the mode of knowing rather than forcing every mode of knowing through the same assessment funnel.

The Director and the Coordinator — CLIL in the Real World
The director of the school did not speak English.

IES Príncipe Felipe language’s coordinator | Day 5 © Filipe Gama
In a week that had devoted considerable time to CLIL — to scaffolding, to language as a cognitive tool, to the BICS/CALP distinction — this was an unexpectedly precise illustration. The language coordinator stepped in as bridge. The conversation continued, multilingual, negotiated, functional.
This is what CLIL looks like outside the idealised classroom: content — in this case, the school’s programmes, its philosophy, its history — crossed by language difference, managed collectively, with whoever holds the linguistic bridge taking a mediating role. No one suggested the director’s lack of English disqualified the school’s bilingual programme. The point was precisely that bilingualism and internationalisation are not the same thing, and that one can build a genuinely European institution without requiring every person in it to perform the same linguistic competence.

”Alumnos Wifi”
The school designates a group of student volunteers as “alumnos wifi.”
They manage classroom technology, troubleshoot digital infrastructure, and provide technical support to teaching staff and to other students. The role is voluntary, formally recognised, and integrated into the school’s technological identity.

Filipe (PT) testing a computer desk, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team
The pedagogical significance is exact: learner autonomy taken to its institutional limit. These students are not receiving instruction in digital competences. They are exercising them as agents within the institution. The school treats student expertise as a resource rather than an exception. The “alumnos wifi” are a form of peer mentoring, a form of cooperative learning, and a form of active methodology, operating simultaneously, without any of those labels being invoked.

Erasmus+ as Institutional Identity
The school does not run an Erasmus+ programme. It identifies itself through one.
The institutional documentation is explicit: “todos nuestros estudiantes deberían de tener una oportunidad europea.” The sentence frames European mobility not as an optional enhancement for high-achieving students but as something every student at the school is entitled to access.

An exhibition of students’ Erasmus+ experience pictures, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Filipe Gama
For vocational students, this takes a specific form: placements in European companies, not exchanges to European universities. The professional trajectory is internationalised directly. A student in audiovisual production, aesthetics or social care completes a work placement somewhere in Europe. The Erasmus+ Spirit the week had discussed — as philosophical disposition, as recognition of diversity, as democratic commitment — is here an operational component of a vocational qualification.

The Prado and the Audiovisual Lab — A Thread Completed
In the working notes from the visit, this connection was named directly: Las Meninas and the audiovisual laboratory are asking the same questions.

A “Las Meninas” artwork interpretation spotted at IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team
Las Meninas asks: Who is watching? Who is being watched? What is happening outside the frame? What does the mirror show? Who is the painter painting?

Maria Grazia (IT), Claudia (IT), Mara (IT), Carsten (DE) and Megan (UK) in the TV set at IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team

The media control room, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Filipe Gama
The audiovisual lab asks: Where do you place the camera? What does the frame include and exclude? Who controls the image? How is the viewer positioned? What does the light do to what it illuminates?
These are not analogous questions. They are the same question, asked in different centuries with different tools.
Goya’s paintings — the 2 and 3 of May, the Pinturas Negras, the portraits of the royal family — raise questions about representation, narrative, propaganda and the construction of reality through visual means. The audiovisual curriculum at the IES is, among other things, a technical education in exactly those operations. Media literacy and visual literacy are not separate subjects that happen to share vocabulary. They are one project running across several centuries.

The PT/ES Comparison — Unsaid but Present
The comparison between Portuguese and Spanish vocational education was not part of the official visit agenda. It was present in every room.
In Spain, the Formación Profesional has institutional visibility, dedicated infrastructure, social legitimacy, and a direct path to European mobility. The IES presents its FP cycles as central to its identity, not as an alternative for students who didn’t make it elsewhere.
In Portugal, vocational education has been expanding, but the symbolic hierarchy — academic over vocational, university over everything else — remains largely intact. A student in a Curso Profissional in Portugal does not typically encounter a school that describes itself through that course as its primary institutional face.

Megan (UK) highlighting differences in European education systems, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team
This is not a critique. It is an observation the visit made available, quietly, through contrast.

Synthesis
The IES Príncipe Felipe functioned on Thursday morning as the week’s most comprehensive test case.

Student locker customisation culture, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Filipe Gama
Every methodological principle the sessions had introduced was either visible in the school’s practice or illuminated by its absence. UDL in the multimodal labs. CLIL in the negotiated encounter at the director’s door. Cooperative learning in the “alumnos wifi” programme. Active methodologies in rooms that made passivity architecturally difficult. The European Dimension in a school that sends vocational students to European companies and means it.

Another student locker, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Filipe Gama
The school did not illustrate the course. It extended it. The morning showed that methodology without institutional culture, without infrastructure, without symbolic legitimacy, is a set of techniques suspended in mid-air. The IES had built the air into a building.

Yet another student locker, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Filipe Gama

References

Universal Design for Learning
CLIL
Cooperative Learning
Active Methodologies
The SAMR Model
Context4Content
Las Meninas
Goya
Visual Learning
Erasmus+ Spirit
European Dimension in Education
Place-based Learning
Learner Autonomy

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Images of Madrid.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Images of Madrid
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
At four in the afternoon, the classroom disappeared.

By afternoon, the argument left the room entirely and took the group into the city, where Madrid stopped being a backdrop and became the curriculum itself. The streets, the monuments, the food, the movement through urban space: all of it functioning as content, as context, as learning environment.
Among the locations: the Royal Palace, and a plaque. One of the quiz questions asked what had happened there on 2 May 1808. The answer pointed directly to Goya, to the companion paintings that would be waiting at the Prado later in the week, and to the thread of art as testimony that was already running, quietly, through the whole programme.
The day’s underlying logic can be read as a single arc:
From the educator’s identity, to the competences that shape learning, to the city as the space where both are tested in real conditions.

For Images of Madrid - a walking tour, the group split into two and moved into the city with phones, a Seesaw app, a Genially map and a quiz structure built around eight locations. The activity lasted ninety minutes, officially. In practice, it lasted as long as anyone wanted it to.
What the structure did not announce was how pedagogically complete it was. The walking tour combined a scavenger hunt, Inquiry-Based Learning, game-based elements, Collaborative Learning and digital documentation, without any of those labels being visible to participants. The gamification was light: one point per photo, one per correct answer, bonus points for creativity. Enough to structure attention and motivate movement, not enough to override curiosity.
The route itself was a curated argument about Madrid. Plaza Mayor, Restaurante Botín, the Royal Palace, Plaza de Oriente, Puerta del Sol. Each location carried a question that required active observation. Which parts of the bear are gold? How did Galileo solve the structural problem of the rearing horse? What happened here on 2 May 1808?
This last question was not incidental. The Royal Palace plaque commemorating the 2nd of May connects directly to Goya, to the works that would appear later in the week at the Prado, and to the political and cultural threads that run through the whole programme. The city was already reading itself as curriculum before anyone named it that way.
The Genially map transformed Madrid into an interactive interface, giving the tour a non-linear quality. Participants could approach locations in different orders, make different decisions, notice different things. The Seesaw platform collected the results as a shared portfolio: photos, answers and creative responses that became a collective memory of the afternoon.
What made the activity genuinely rich was the pedagogy of observation it demanded. Not passing through. Looking. Reading the golden parts of a statue. Finding a plaque on the ground. Working out a structural solution from visual evidence. The city rewarded attention, and that is itself a Place-based Learning argument: that environments teach when learners are asked to read them carefully.
There is a question worth holding, even so. The narrative Madrid that the route presented was monumental, imperial and largely celebratory. The buildings are real. The history they carry is also real, and more contested than a quiz can hold. What the tour could not quite accommodate was the other Madrid: the social tensions, the colonial histories embedded in those same streets, the questions a more critical urban literacy would ask. That is not an argument against the activity. It is an argument for what might follow it.
The afternoon ended, with cañas and tapas, and a few countries sitting around a table comparing notes on what they had seen.

Mara (IT), Filipe (PT), André (PT), Teresa (PT), Claudia (IT), Julia (DE), Mónica (PT) and Sílvia (IT) having some “cañas” after Images of Madrid | Day 2 © Team

That, too, was learning.


References

Place-based Learning
Experiential Learning
Context4Content
European Dimension in Education
Inquiry-Based Learning
Collaborative Learning
Seesaw
Genially
Goya
Walking Pedagogy
