Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Metropolis.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Metropolis
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Fritz Lang, 1927. Silent film. Screened at Sala Equis, Madrid, 4 May 2026.

The Film
Metropolis depicts a city divided into two worlds that never meet: the elevated towers where the ruling class lives and works, and the underground machine halls where workers maintain the city’s infrastructure at the cost of their own exhaustion and lives.

Set photograph from Metropolis (1926). Photograph by Horst von Harbou. Public domain. Wikimedia Commons
The mediating figure — Freder, the privileged son who descends to witness the workers’ conditions — carries the film’s central line:

“The mediator between head and hands must be the heart.”

That line describes a teacher. It describes a CLIL facilitator, an Erasmus+ coordinator, a student as an active agent. It describes the gap between theory and practice, between school and community, between technical skill and human skill, and the person whose job it is to hold that gap open long enough for something to cross it.

Technology and Humanity
Lang made Metropolis at the height of German Expressionism and on the eve of the political catastrophes that would define the 20th century. The film’s vision of dehumanising technology — the worker as machine component, the machine as false god — was a warning shaped from observation.

Brigitte Helm as Maria on the set of Metropolis (1926). Photograph by Horst von Harbou. Public domain. Wikimedia Commons
Watching it in 2026 requires no great effort of imagination. The machines are different; the division of labour and the question of who bears its costs are not. Innovation is not neutral. The question is always: in whose service? This sits directly alongside current conversations about AI Literacy and the automation of educational processes.

Set photograph from Metropolis (1926). Photograph by Horst von Harbou. Public domain. Wikimedia Commons

Silent Film as Multimodal Pedagogy
The film is silent. That detail matters more than it first appears. Metropolis communicates entirely through image, gesture, symbol and narrative structure. No linguistic competence is required to read it — precisely what Visual Learning argues: that meaning travels through channels that language does not monopolise.
A silent film projected in a cinema in Madrid, watched by educators from different countries, is a small demonstration of that argument in practice. Language is not the barrier. Attention is the skill.

Vocational Education
The film’s argument about the relationship between hand and mind has particular resonance for vocational education. The degradation of manual work in Metropolis is the degradation of the worker who is considered a mechanism rather than a person.
Vocational education, at its best, refuses that degradation. It insists that the person who builds, makes, cooks, tends, repairs, and maintains the world deserves the full dignity of learning — and the full apparatus of good pedagogy.

Sala Equis as Learning Context
Sala Equis — a cinema in a building that was once a pornographic theatre in the transition years after Franco — is itself a document of social change. Watching a 1927 silent film in this space, in Madrid, in May 2026, was one of those moments where the layers of context do not merely surround the experience. They are the experience.
Metropolis was made in 1927. It has not dated.

See Place-based Learning, Visual Learning, AI Literacy, Fiat Humanitas, Intercultural Education, European Dimension in Education, Sala Equis.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The course had ended. This was personal.
An audioguide rented at the door. All the floors. Four to five hours alone in the building, moving at own pace through the argument the museum makes about modernism, trauma, representation and the expansion of historical memory.
Avant-Garde Territories built the ecology of modernism — not a sequence of masterworks but a cultural network of city, magazine, manifesto, poster, architecture and film. Cubism as the visual language of the fragmented modern metropolis. The Delaunays collapsing the boundary between art and daily life. Miró preserving play. Dalí opening into the unconscious. Bohemia as experimental community. The avant-garde magazines as proto-internet: rapid, transnational, distributed.

Guernica room, Reina Sofía | Day 7 © Filipe Gama
Then Guernica. Picasso working from newspapers and testimony, not direct witness — the same position Goya occupied painting El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid and El 3 de mayo en Madrid, six years after the events. The same refusal of heroism. The same insistence on the body, fragmented and specific, over the cause. The tradition of the war reporter who will not produce the official version.

History doesn’t repeat itself | Day 7 © Filipe Gama
Then Dumile Feni — the African Guernica. “History doesn’t repeat itself, it does rhyme.” The same expressionist language of suffering bodies, displaced from Europe to Africa, from fascist bombardment to colonialism and apartheid. The question the exhibition asks: which suffering enters museums? Which tragedies are universalised? Whose bodies are invisible in the canon of historical pain?

Guernica, Reina Sofía | Day 7 © André Almeida
The triptych the Reina Sofia completes across its walls: Goya, Picasso, Feni. Three centuries. Three different geographies of violence. The same refusal. The same rhyme.
Contemporary Art post-1975 changed the register: installation, video, archive, performance. The object giving way to the interrogation of systems. The visitor pulled into interpretive labour. Minorities, memory, identity, postcoloniality — the question of who has the right to representation, running through every room.
The observation that held the whole afternoon together: culture is not a supplement to education. It is a technology for reading the world.

Richard Serra, vanished sculpture, Reina Sofía | Day 7 © Filipe Gama
See Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia for the full notes.

Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia
Solo Visit with Audioguide — Saturday Afternoon, 9 May 2026
4–5 hours. All the floors. The audioguide running.
If the Prado gave the week its genealogy of representation — Fra Angelico’s ordered light through to Goya’s refusal to comfort — the Reina Sofia gave it the next chapter: the genealogy of critical modernity. The two museums are in direct conversation. The Prado ends roughly where the Reina Sofia begins. Goya is the hinge.
The observation noted in the working notes: “hello curadoria e scaffolding?” The audioguide is exactly that. It orients attention, selects routes, contextualises, sequences interpretation, activates connections, provides the vocabulary the image alone does not supply, regulates cognitive load. It is Universal Design for Learning applied to a museum visit: multiple means of access, learner-controlled pace, contextual support built in from the start. It is also CLIL in practice — specialised language scaffolded against real objects, meaning built through the encounter rather than prepared in advance.

I. Avant-Garde Territories — City, Fragmentation and the Invention of Modernity
The “Avant-Garde Territories” floor does not present a sequence of masterworks. It builds an ecology.
What becomes visible is that the historical avant-gardes did not emerge from individual genius. They emerged from a transformation of the total human environment: the growth of cities, the acceleration of time, industrialisation, urban masses, print culture, advertising, mechanised war, the international circulation of ideas through magazines and exhibitions and manifestos. The works on the walls are inseparable from the city outside the walls.
The city as perceptual laboratory. The modern metropolis fragments experience. The street is simultaneous, noisy, saturated, discontinuous. The Cubist move — multiple viewpoints, decomposed perspective, simultaneous representation of what cannot be seen from a single position — is not a formal innovation appended to reality. It is a translation of a new perceptual condition. The Renaissance perspective assumed a fixed observer: stable, central, commanding. The modern city dissolved that stability. Cubism recorded the dissolution.
Pre-Cubism searched for structure and geometric clarity in the aftermath of Impressionism’s dissolution of form. Cubism exploded it into multiplicity. Post-Cubism absorbed the lesson and made fragmented perspective a way of thinking about the world rather than just a technique for representing it.
Revistas, cartazes, artes menores. One of the floor’s most important arguments is the refusal to rank. The avant-garde magazines — little reviews, manifestos, typographic experiments, international networks of print — appear alongside the paintings. They were, the museum insists, the infrastructure through which modernism actually spread: not through museums, but through pages, covers, layouts, photographs, reproductions. A proto-internet of cultural circulation: rapid, remixable, transnational, community-distributed.
This collapses the boundary between high culture and communication design, between art and propaganda, between the museum object and the printed ephemeral. The same boundary that the week had been putting pressure on since Sunday. The wall between Goya’s paintings and a Padlet Arcade using Guernica is thinner than it appears.
Sonia and Robert Delaunay represent the dissolution of another border: between art, fashion, textile, performance and daily life. The modernist project, at its most expansive, was not about producing objects for contemplation. It was about redesigning experience. “Das artes menores” — the working notes caught the connection: the museum resists the hierarchy that places painting above design above craft above decoration. The same argument that the IES Príncipe Felipe made with its aesthetics and hairdressing labs.
Joan Miró preserves something that the avant-garde kept trying to recover: play, childhood, a language that has not yet been disciplined into convention. Miró is Picasso’s claim made painterly — “Every child is born an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.” The work looks easy. It is the result of a sustained and deliberate unlearning of everything that formal training installs.
Salvador Dalí takes the project somewhere else entirely: the unconscious, dream logic, desire, paranoia, the structures that resist rational order. If Cubism responds to the external fragmentation of the modern city, Surrealism responds to the internal fragmentation of modern psychology. The self, it turns out, is also not a fixed observer from a single stable position.
Bohemia runs through the floor as an organisational principle. The avant-gardes were not schools. They were experimental communities — networks of shared practice, shared risk, shared refusal of the dominant order. What they produced required the conditions of proximity, exchange and collective permission-giving. The IES Príncipe Felipe’s “alumnos wifi” is one contemporary echo. The course that just ended is another.

II. Guernica and the African Guernica — Art as Historical Conscience
The Guernica room functions as the museum’s moral centre.
Guernica (Picasso, 1937) is a 7.7-metre painting made in response to the Nazi aerial bombardment of a Basque town on 26 April 1937. Picasso was not there. He constructed the work from newspaper reports, photographs, and testimony. The fact matters: Guernica is not documentary evidence. It is mediated witness — the same position Goya occupied when he painted the 2 and 3 de Mayo six years after the events.
Both painters worked from memory, testimony and moral urgency rather than direct observation. Both produced works that refuse heroism. Both gave the body — fragmented, agonised, specific — more weight than the cause. The tradition running from Goya through Picasso is a tradition of the war reporter who refuses the official version: who says, this is what it looked like, this is what it did to people, there are no clean hands here.
The formal language of Guernica — monochrome, Cubist fragmentation, screaming mouths, distorted limbs, a horse collapsing, a bull watching, a lamp like an eye — constructs what the working notes called “verdade emocional e civilizacional”: emotional and civilisational truth. Not what happened at a specific location on a specific day. What happens when human beings decide to bomb civilian populations. The universal compressed into the particular.
Dumile Feni — The African Guernica. The exhibition title is the thesis: “History doesn’t repeat itself, it does rhyme.”
Dumile Feni (1942–1991) was a South African artist who developed his practice under apartheid, in exile, in the same visual language as the European expressionist tradition — distorted, suffering, spectral bodies — but pointed at a different geography of violence: colonialism, apartheid, structural racism, the systematic erasure of African subjectivity from the canon of historical pain.
The dialogue the museum stages between Guernica and the African Guernica asks several questions at once. Which suffering enters museums? Which tragedies are universalised? Which victims are rendered invisible by the architecture of cultural memory? Picasso’s painting became the 20th century’s image of civilian suffering. Feni’s work, made in the same period and with the same moral intensity, remained largely outside the canon that Picasso occupied.
The triptych the Reina Sofia makes visible across its walls: Goya at the Prado (Napoleonic war, 1808), Picasso at the Reina Sofia (Fascist bombardment, 1937), Dumile Feni at the Reina Sofia (colonial and apartheid violence, same century). Three war reporters. Three different centuries and geographies. The same refusal of the heroic narrative. The rhyme that history produces, moving through different bodies and different languages, not repeating but recognisably itself.
The connection to Toledo the day before is exact. The same question: who gets a face? Goya gave faces to the victims of the Third of May and denied them to the firing squad. Picasso gave faces to the suffering of Guernica and denied them to the mechanism of the bombardment. Feni gave faces to the suffering that the dominant canon had chosen not to see. Toledo showed what happens when three traditions share space; the African Guernica shows what happens when one of them controls the frame.

III. Contemporary Art, 1975–Present — Archive, Identity and the Interrogation of Systems
The post-1975 floor is a different kind of claim.
The work here is often not painting or sculpture in the conventional sense. It is installation, video, performance, document, spatial intervention, archive. The object has given way to the process. The finished work has given way to the act of asking.
What contemporary art after 1975 does, the floor argues, is no longer primarily to represent the world. It interrogates the systems through which the world is organised and made legible: political systems, media systems, colonial systems, identity systems, institutional systems. The museum itself is one of those systems — and some of the works on the floor are, implicitly or explicitly, about the museum housing them.
The visitor changes function. In the Prado, looking was the primary act. The works were delivered; the viewer attended. In the contemporary collection, the viewer is pulled into interpretive labour. The work rarely provides a single reading. It requires negotiation, inference, contextualisation, historical consciousness. The “multiple means of action and expression” that UDL describes as a principle of good design are here the default conditions of engagement.
“Os públicos. As minorias e a consciência.” The note captures what the floor insists on: the emergence of voices and bodies and memories that the modernist canon systematically excluded. Gender, postcoloniality, hybrid identities, silenced memories, peripheral subjectivities. The question “who has the right to representation?” runs through the floor as its organising argument. It connects directly to the European Dimension session’s discussion of dissemination: whose work is visible, whose practice is legitimised, whose experience enters the archive.
The “caldo cultural.” The observation that marked the whole day was this: each work in the museum participates in a larger ecology of meanings. Nothing here is isolated. Every painting carries debates, languages, memories, conflicts, technologies, urban transformations, shifts in sensibility. The audioguide made this visible by tracing the threads. Without it, the works could be encountered individually, aesthetically, quietly. With it, they became what they are: nodes in a network of historical argument.
This is what the week had been practising since Sunday: reading the network, not just the object. Reading Las Meninas for what it says about power and representation, not just for its extraordinary technique. Reading the 3 de Mayo for what it says about who gets a face in the official record. Reading Toledo for what its architecture says about coexistence and its limits. The Reina Sofia made explicit what the week had been developing as a method: that culture is not a supplement to education. It is a technology for reading the world.

The Full Arc
Prado on Wednesday. Reina Sofia on Saturday. The two museums form a single argument across five days.
The Prado: how images produce meaning, from Fra Angelico’s geometric certainty to Goya’s refusal of comfort. Six centuries of representation as a tool for constructing reality.
The Reina Sofia: how the modern world broke that certainty and what artists did with the fragments. The city as perceptual laboratory. The war as subject that resists heroism. The body as archive of historical violence. The museum as a system that can expand or contract the canon of whose suffering counts.
Between them: the IES Príncipe Felipe’s audiovisual labs, where students learn to produce the images the Prado and Reina Sofia teach you to read. The presentation with the artworks as visual anchors. The coordinator’s confirmation that the art was intentional. The phrase “history rhymes” on a wall in the Reina Sofia, which had been the thread since 3 May in Madrid, in the city where Goya painted what happened on 3 May, in 1808.
Aprender é aprender a interpretar sistemas culturais. Learning is learning to read cultural systems. The Reina Sofia said it on a Saturday afternoon, five days after the week began, and it was exactly what the week had been saying all along.

References

Guernica
Picasso
Dumile Feni
Goya
El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid
El 3 de mayo en Madrid
Las Meninas
El Greco
Cubism
Visual Learning
Media Literacy
AI Literacy
Universal Design for Learning
CLIL
Scaffolding
Context4Content
Heritage as Curriculum
Place-based Learning
Fiat Humanitas
European Dimension in Education


Part of Dissemination & Reflection, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Sala Equis.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Sala Equis
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Monday, 4 May 2026. Calle del Duque de Alba, Madrid.
Sala Equis is not easy to categorise. It was a cinema before it was a bar, and it is still both, and neither exclusively. The building dates from the early twentieth century and spent decades as one of Madrid’s adult cinemas before being converted into what it is now: a cultural space with mismatched furniture, exposed brick, films projected on the walls, and the kind of atmosphere that only exists in cities that have learned to repurpose their own history rather than erase it.

Cool ambient at Sala Equis | Day 2 © Filipe Gama
We arrived after the walking tour, after the cañas, after the city had already done a full day’s work on us. Julia, a German colleague and Erasmus+ participant, had mentioned the place. We went.
What followed was several hours of conversation that had nothing to do with the programme and everything to do with why any of us were there. Art, culture, war, music, past experiences, the things that shape a person before they become a teacher. At some point we discovered we were both fans of dEUS, the Belgian band, and the conversation shifted into that particular register that only shared musical taste opens. I used a line I had been carrying for a while: They told us we were the dreamers but we’re the ones that don’t sleep. Proudly woke.
On the wall in front of us us, Metropolis was playing. Fritz Lang’s 1927 film, silent, projected without sound into a room full of people talking. The image of a deeply technological and profoundly unequal city, its workers invisible below ground, its elites untouched above. The central argument of the film is carried in a single line: The mediator between the head and the hands must be the heart. It played on the wall while we talked about education.

Julia (DE) ordering cañas, while “Metropolis” was being projected above the bar, Sala Equis | Day 2 © Filipe Gama
That was not planned. But it was Context4Content in its most honest form: the city offering content without being asked, the context doing the teaching while the learners are busy living.
Sala Equis is the kind of place that Place-based Learning theory describes but rarely gets to visit. A space where informal learning, social encounter, cultural circulation and shared memory happen simultaneously, without anyone designing the experience. The film was a layer. The conversation was the curriculum. The place made both possible.

Warm neon lights at Sala Equis | Day 2 © Filipe Gama
There is something in this that connects directly to what the week kept returning to: that learning does not require a room, a facilitator or a task card. It requires presence, attention and the willingness to stay in the conversation long enough for something to surface.
We stayed a long time.

References Context4Content • Place-based Learning • Informal Learning • Cultural Immersion • Visual Learning • Intercultural Education • European Dimension in Education • Metropolis • Sala Equis

Field Note. Part of index. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/Toledo — The City of Three Cultures.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Toledo — The City of Three Cultures
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The city was introduced in the morning. The afternoon was the introduction made walkable.
Toledo is the week’s final and most compressed act of Context4Content: Jewish, Moorish and Christian traditions in the same streets, the same buildings, the same objects. The Mudéjar synagogue built by Islamic craftsmen for a Jewish community in a Christian kingdom. The marzipan tasted in a Madrid classroom, recognised again in the actual shop window the session had predicted. El Greco’s El entierro del conde de Orgaz in a darkened church — the painter who came from elsewhere and made the city’s defining image, asking who mediates between the earthly and the sacred, who gets a face and who does not.
That last question runs directly into Goya. The three traditions that shared Toledo are the same three traditions contesting Jerusalem in May 2026. The El 3 de mayo en Madrid had been in this course since Sunday evening — introduced on 3 May, in the same city, on the same date. Presented as a painting with no winners and no saviour. Only what happened. Toledo closes the arc: a city that shows what coexistence built and what destroyed it, and what remains when the communities that made it are gone.
The guide had been introduced that morning: the timeline, the word cloud, the frozen scenes, the marzipan. Arriving in Toledo was arriving somewhere the session had already been.
History does not repeat itself. But it rhymes. The time-place bend was not accidental. The coordinator had confirmed it.

Toledo — The City of Three Cultures
Afternoon Visit — Friday, 8 May 2026
Toledo is not a destination. It is a document.

Erasmus+ team before entering Toledo | Day 6 © English Matters
Not a document to be read passively — a three-dimensional one, walked, smelled, tasted, felt underfoot. The city encodes, in its streets and buildings and contradictions, a history that the morning session had introduced in a room in Madrid. Arriving in Toledo was arriving somewhere the course had already begun to describe.

Julia (DE) excited about arriving in Toledo | Day 6 © English Matters
The pedagogical principle: not learning about Toledo, but learning through Toledo. Context4Content at its fullest extension.

The City as Laboratory
Toledo worked on Friday as a space of observation, a narrative source, a sensory stimulus, and a living historical object simultaneously. The experience was not a guided tour in the conventional sense. It was what the working notes called “aprendizagem incorporada fisicamente”: learning that passed through walking, observing, feeling the texture of the urban surface, hearing the sounds, encountering the marzipan again in the actual shop window, dealing with heat and crowd and silence and rain.

Toledo old town | Day 6 © André Almeida
The tension the afternoon produced was real: on one hand, Toledo as sensory excess — visual density, historical density, multiple cultures layered over centuries, constant stimulus, constant information. On the other, the invitation carried from the morning’s mindfulness session: slow down, attend, be present. A pedagogy of contemplation operating inside an environment designed to overwhelm it.
That tension was not a failure of the programme. It was the programme. Learning to attend consciously within conditions that resist attention is not a classroom skill. It requires the actual conditions.

The Marzipan
Toledo’s marzipan is the city’s intangible heritage in edible form.
The origin stories vary — Arab confectioners, Moorish courts, convent kitchens maintaining the recipe through the expulsions and the centuries. What is consistent is the survival: marzipan as material continuity through political rupture. The recipe is older than the version of Toledo that exists today. It predates the expulsion of the Jews in 1492. It predates the forced conversions. It crossed the cultural boundary intact.
Tasted in the morning in Madrid, encountered again in the shop window the session had predicted, bought in the city: the marzipan moved from pedagogical device to sensory memory to lived recognition. The morning’s prediction activity and the afternoon’s reality collapsed into a single remembered experience. That is what place-based learning produces when it works: not knowledge about a place, but a place encoded in memory.

The Historical Layers
Roman and Visigothic — Toledo served as a Roman administrative centre and later as the capital of the Visigothic kingdom. The grid of the old city and several foundational structures survive from this period.
Moorish — Al-Ándalus brought Toledo into the Islamic world from 711. The city became a centre of learning, translation, and cultural exchange. Arabic scientific, philosophical, and literary texts were translated into Latin here, transmitting classical Greek knowledge to medieval Europe.
Christian Reconquest — Alfonso VI took Toledo in 1085. The city became the capital of Castile, and the seat of the Catholic Church in Spain. The Cathedral of Toledo, begun in 1226, is one of the great Gothic structures of Europe.
The Golden Age — In the 16th century, Toledo attracted El Greco, who settled here and painted the city with visionary intensity. The city became a centre of artistic and intellectual life.

The Three Cultures — Coexistence and Its Limits
For a period in Toledo’s medieval history, Jewish, Moorish and Christian communities shared the same city, the same craftspeople, the same translation projects, the same air. The Synagogue of El Tránsito — built in the 14th century for the Jewish community of Toledo — was constructed by Moorish craftsmen (its interior is pure Mudéjar) in a Christian kingdom. The object itself is an argument: three traditions required to coexist in one building before it could exist at all.

Our guide highlighting the details of the Sinagoga del Tránsito, built by Moorish craftsmen for the Jewish community, in a Christian kingdom | Day 6 © English Matters
Toledo does not allow the word “coexistence” to be used without qualification. The period of convivencia was real, and its material record is extraordinary. It was also always under pressure, interrupted by persecutions, ended definitively by the expulsion of the Jews in 1492 and the subsequent forced conversion or expulsion of Muslims across the following century. What survived was the architecture — the Mudéjar vaults, the Hebrew inscriptions, the mosques converted to churches and back and again.

Toledo shopping area contrasts with town’s heritage | Day 6 © André Almeida
The city is therefore not a monument to harmony. It is a monument to the complexity of what happens when traditions are in genuine contact over time: what gets built, what gets destroyed, what persists in unexpected forms, what cannot be erased even when the community that made it is gone.

El entierro del conde de Orgaz — El Greco
The painting is in Toledo. It belongs to Toledo. It could not have been painted elsewhere.
El entierro del conde de Orgaz (El Greco, 1586–1588) hangs in the Iglesia de Santo Tomé, where it has always been. It was commissioned to commemorate a local legend: that when Don Gonzalo Ruiz de Toledo, Count of Orgaz, was buried in 1323, the saints Augustine and Stephen descended from heaven to lay the body in the ground themselves. El Greco painted the legend, and in painting it he also painted Toledo — the faces in the earthly half of the composition are identifiable portraits of the city’s nobility, his contemporaries, the people he lived among. His own son appears at the lower left, looking out directly at the viewer, pointing downward at the burial scene.

Maria Grázia (IT), in front of “El entierro del Señor de Orgaz” by El Greco, Toledo | Day 6 © English Matters
The painting is divided between two registers: the earthly and the divine. Below: the count’s body, the mourners, the clergy, the portraits of real Toledo faces. Above: a compressed, luminous heaven in El Greco’s characteristic elongated style, Christ and the Virgin, angels, souls ascending. Between them: a single angel carrying a newborn soul upward through the divide.
El Greco was Greek. He trained in Venice. He arrived in Toledo and stayed, becoming one of the city’s defining painters. He is, in his own biography, a figure of cultural crossing — Byzantine, Venetian, Spanish, Toledan. His style makes no sense without all of those layers. The painting that became Toledo’s defining image was made by someone from somewhere else.

El Greco painting, Toledo | Day 6 © André Almeida
What The Burial of the Count of Orgaz asks: what is the relationship between the earthly and the sacred? Who mediates? What kind of life earns divine attention? Who gets a face, and who is denied one?
The last question runs directly into Goya.

The Thread — Jerusalem, Goya, Toledo, Now
The three Abrahamic traditions that shared Toledo are the same three traditions whose relationship continues to generate the most acute conflicts of the contemporary world. Jewish, Christian, Islamic: in Toledo they built the Mudéjar synagogue together, translated Aristotle into Latin via Arabic, produced a shared intellectual culture that survived into the expulsions. In Jerusalem, the same three traditions lay claim to the same stones, the same hills, the same stories of origin. The geometry of the conflict is identical. The resolution is not.

A Palestine flag spotted in Toledo | Day 6 © Filipe Gama
Toledo in 2026 sits in the shadow of Gaza, whether the guided tour acknowledges this or not. The city that is presented as a model of coexistence is also the city that ended with expulsion, forced conversion, and the erasure of the communities that built it. The lesson is not “look at what was possible.” The lesson is more complicated: look at what was built, look at what destroyed it, look at what remains when the people who made it are gone.
Goya had been in this course since Sunday evening: introduced at the quiz, identified as a war reporter, encountered on the plaque at the Royal Palace, used to unlock the Prado visit’s logic, present in two of the most devastating paintings in the museum. The 2 and 3 de Mayo were Madrid’s catastrophe, painted six years after the events, submitted with an official justification that the paintings themselves systematically refused. “Rather than glorifying war, it strips it bare. There are no winners, no saviour to admire. Only bloodshed and confusion.”
The quiz had been answered on 3 May 2026 — the same date. In the same city. That was not accidental, and the course coordinator had confirmed it was not accidental: Context4Content at both registers, the canonical and the everyday, designed to make time and place bend together.
Toledo closes the arc. The city of three cultures is also the city that shows what happens when coexistence breaks down and the architecture survives the communities that made it. Goya showed what happens when an army moves through a city and neither side emerges with clean hands. The three faiths that contested Toledo are the same three faiths contesting Jerusalem in May 2026, in the same week the course was running, in the same week the group stood in front of The Third of May and heard: no winners, no saviour. Only what happened.

Getting close to the Cathedral of Saint Mary, Toledo | Day 6 © André Almeida
History does not repeat itself. But it rhymes. The time-place bend is not a metaphor. It is what happens when you choose Toledo as the last act of a course that began with Goya.

Place-Based Learning — What Toledo Produced
The working notes named it directly: learning through Toledo, not about it.
What the afternoon produced was not information about the Three Cultures, transferable to any other context, portable as a set of facts. It produced an encoded experience: the weight of the city underfoot, the smell of the streets, the marzipan in the shop the session had predicted, the Mudéjar ceiling of the synagogue, El Greco’s faces in a darkened church, the sky above the Tagus gorge. Those sensory traces are the encoding. They are what makes the knowledge durable.
The cognitive science argument from the morning session confirmed it: multisensory engagement produces more resilient memory traces. The pedagogical argument from the whole week confirmed it: context is not the setting in which content is delivered. Context is content. Toledo was not where the week ended. Toledo was the week’s final argument, made in three dimensions, walked through rather than read.

References

El Greco
El entierro del conde de Orgaz
Goya
El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid
El 3 de mayo en Madrid
Las Meninas
Context4Content
Place-based Learning
Multisensory Learning
Multisensory Learning
Heritage as Curriculum
Visual Learning
Mindfulness
Embodied Learning
Erasmus+ Spirit
European Dimension in Education
Cervantes
Fiat Humanitas


Part of Place-Based Learning & Cultural Heritage, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Field Learning Projects/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Field Learning Projects
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Field Learning Projects
The field learning projects were not enrichment activities added to the programme. They were the programme.
Each project applied the Context4Content principle that learning does not need to be applied to reality — it is constructed from reality. The city, the market, the museum, the school, the painting on the wall of a bar: all of them functioned as primary learning environments. Participants were not visitors to these spaces. They were readers of them.
The projects ran at two registers. Some were designed and structured — task cards, a quiz format, a scavenger hunt, a Seesaw portfolio, a guided narrative. Others were unplanned: encounters that the city offered and the group accepted. Both registers were intentional. The coordinator confirmed it on Thursday morning: the programme had always been built this way.

Structured Field Projects
El Rastro — Sunday morning, before the course began. La Latina, pan con tomate, the Sunday market. No task card. The first learning of the week arrived before learning was scheduled.
Images of Madrid — Monday afternoon. A walking tour structured as a quiz and scavenger hunt across eight locations: Plaza Mayor, Restaurante Botín, Plaza de la Villa, Almudena Cathedral, the Royal Palace and the 2 de Mayo plaque, Plaza de Oriente, Puerta del Sol, Km0. Participants used Seesaw for documentation and a Genially interactive map as their guide. The tour was designed to be non-linear: different routes, different experiences.
Highlights of Spanish Art — Wednesday afternoon at the Museo del Prado. Fourteen works across six centuries, curated into a single argument about representation, perspective and the act of looking. The meeting point was the statue of Goya at the entrance — which was itself the first work of art. The route moved through Fra Angelico, Hieronymus Bosch, Rogier van der Weyden, El Greco, Jacopo Tintoretto, Peter Paul Rubens, Diego Velázquez and Goya, ending — after four days of accumulation — in front of Las Meninas.
IES Príncipe Felipe — Thursday morning. A school visit as field research. Classrooms, laboratories and corridors as objects of study: UDL in the spatial layout, CLIL in the multilingual encounter with the director, cooperative learning in the student wifi programme. The frameworks made architectural.
Toledo — The City of Three Cultures — Friday afternoon. The city as palimpsest: Jewish, Moorish and Christian traditions in the same streets, the same buildings, the same objects. The marzipan tasted in the morning session was recognised again in the actual shop window. El Greco’s El entierro del conde de Orgaz in a darkened church. The city explained Context4Content without a framework.

Unplanned Encounters
Sala Equis — Monday evening. A cinema bar in Madrid, discovered by the group. Metropolis projected on the wall above the bar, silent. The central line of the film on loop: The mediator between the head and the hands must be the heart. Nobody had planned it. Nobody could have.
Casa de Campo — Tuesday afternoon. Listed in the programme as “Autonomous Portfolio Work.” What it became was a park. Space, movement, deceleration. No task, no output, no reflection prompt. The morning’s frameworks continued, quietly, at a different pace.
Gastronomy — running across the week. Bocadillos de calamares as bonus points. Cañas after the walking tour. Chocolatería San Ginés. Restaurante Botín. Los Porfiados on Thursday. Taberna Mariano in Toledo. Marzipan before Toledo, recognised again inside it. Food as Context4Content, lowercase.
Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia — Saturday afternoon. Self-guided, with an audioguide as scaffold. Four to five hours across all floors: Avant-Garde Territories, Guernica, the African Guernica, Contemporary Art post-1975. The course had ended at midday. The learning continued until the building closed.

The Logic of the Programme
The field learning projects were sequenced, not random.
Sunday began in a market before it moved into a classroom. Monday took the classroom frameworks into the streets. Wednesday took them into a museum — and had placed a painting on screen that morning as a preview of what was waiting at the entrance. Thursday took them into a school. Friday took them into a city that had been holding the week’s argument in its stones for nine centuries.
Saturday took no one anywhere. The course ended and one person stayed in the Reina Sofía for four hours alone.

See Context4Content for the methodological framework. See Field Learning Projects for observations from the field.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/21st Century Learning Design.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: 21st Century Learning Design
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The framework does not prescribe what to teach. It describes what learning looks like when it is working.

21st Century Learning Design (21CLD) is a research-based framework developed by Microsoft Partners in Learning in collaboration with SRI International (2011). It provides educators and researchers with structured rubrics for evaluating how well a learning activity develops the competences students need in contemporary professional and civic life.

The Six Dimensions
Collaboration — the extent to which students work together in ways that require genuine interdependence. The framework distinguishes real collaboration, where the product fails if any member does not contribute, from parallel group work, where individual outputs are merely added together. Maximum score: 5.
Knowledge Construction — the extent to which students build new understanding rather than reproduce existing information. Assessed by whether students apply knowledge to new contexts, generate interpretations, or produce original analysis. Maximum score: 5.
Self-Regulation — the extent to which students plan their own work, set goals, monitor progress and iterate. A high score requires that students have had prior opportunities to practice the skills they now apply independently. Maximum score: 4.
Real-World Problem-Solving — the extent to which students engage with authentic problems that have impact beyond the classroom. The highest scores require problems identified by students themselves, solutions with real-world implementation, and a genuine external audience. Maximum score: 4.
Skilled Communication — the extent to which students communicate for a specific purpose and audience, using evidence and multiple modes. Maximum score: 4.
ICT for Learning — the extent to which digital technologies are integral to the learning task, not supplementary to it. Maximum score: 5.


                  
                  The 21CLD framework's most useful function is diagnostic: it reveals which design decisions produce which learning outcomes, and what would have to change to reach the next level. A score of 3 on Collaboration is not a failure — it is a description of where the ceiling was and why. 
                  
                


Connections in the Course
21CLD was the focus of a session of the e-Learning and b-Learning Design course (May 2025): a decision-tree activity in which participants analysed their own learning designs against each dimension. See Session 8 — 21CLD Analysis.

Connections
21st Century Skills — the broader competence landscape the 21CLD framework operationalises through rubrics.
Collaborative Learning — the pedagogical model that maps most directly onto the Collaboration dimension.
Multimodal Literacy — essential to the Skilled Communication dimension’s highest performance levels.
Inquiry-Based Learning — natural alignment with the Real-World Problem-Solving and Knowledge Construction dimensions.
Situated Learning — the theoretical grounding for why real-world context matters across multiple 21CLD dimensions.
Constructivist Pedagogy — the epistemological foundation for the Knowledge Construction dimension.
Transforma-te Transforma — a case study in which a student-led project at Arco Maior reached maximum score in five of six dimensions.

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/21st Century Skills.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: 21st Century Skills
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A framework for the competencies learners need to thrive in contemporary professional and civic life. Commonly grouped into four clusters: the 4Cs of Learning, Literacy Skills, Life Skills, and Career Skills.

The 4Cs of Learning
Critical Thinking — working together toward a shared goal; combining individual strengths through collaborative structures. The social dimension of knowledge construction.
Creativity — the ability to evaluate evidence, separate fact from opinion, and resist the pull of uncritical acceptance. Asking why, how do we know, and whose perspective is this.
Communication — conveying ideas with clarity and purpose; reading the audience and adjusting register, format, and complexity accordingly.
Collaboration — generating original ideas, approaches, and solutions; thinking outside established conventions; taking creative risks.
Note: the source materials for this section used inconsistent labelling across files. The above reflects the corrected mapping of content to competency.

Literacy Skills
Information Literacy — locating, evaluating, and applying information from multiple sources, including the capacity to distinguish reliable from unreliable content. Connects to Healthy scepticism and AI Literacy.
Media Literacy — understanding how media messages are constructed, what interests they serve, and how they shape perception. Connects to What is truth in 2026? and Las Meninas.
Technology Literacy — digital proficiency; the capacity to use tools independently and to conduct independent research. Connects to Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence and the five DigComp areas.

Life Skills
Flexibility — adapting to new contexts, navigating social situations with appropriate register, and maintaining productive working relationships across difference.
Initiative — intrinsic motivation, personal work ethic, and the capacity to act without waiting for permission or instruction.
Productivity — managing time and attention; completing work to standard within available constraints.
Leadership — setting goals for a group, guiding collaborative effort, and taking responsibility for shared outcomes.

Expression
The ability to demonstrate learning and communicate understanding in multiple formats — written, verbal, visual, spatial, and performative. Connects to Universal Design for Learning (multiple means of action and expression) and Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences.

Connections in the Course
The 21st Century Skills framework ran through the entire week: cooperative tasks built collaboration and communication; museum visits required critical and creative thinking; reflective writing and multimodal outputs developed expression. See Future of Jobs Report 2025 for the current labour market evidence for these skills.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes
