Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A structured sensory experience using marzipan as the object of focused, slow attention. Practised in Toledo during the course visit, at the end of a day of field learning.

The Eight Steps
1. Hold — receive the marzipan in your palm. Do not eat it yet. Simply hold it.
2. Observe — look at it. Notice its colour, its shape, the surface texture, the marks left by the mould.
3. Touch — press it gently between your fingers. Notice the resistance, the density, the slight give.
4. Smell — bring it close. Breathe in. The almond, the sugar, something else underneath.
5. Taste the surface — touch it to your lips, then to the tip of your tongue. Before biting.
6. Bite — slowly. Notice the texture as it breaks, the first flood of sweetness.
7. Chew — deliberately. Keep attention on what is in your mouth.
8. Reflect — what did you notice? What changed between step 1 and step 7? What would you have missed if you had simply eaten it?

The Pedagogical Argument
Mindful eating is not a wellness exercise in disguise. It is a lesson in the difference between experiencing and consuming.
The same distinction applies to learning: we frequently move through content too quickly to actually encounter it. Slowing down — holding, observing, tasting before concluding — is a transferable practice.

Connections in the Course
See 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding Exercise, A Mindfulness Moment — Using Our Senses to Feel Grounded, Gastronomy notes — Madrid as Memory, Ritual and Learning, Marzipan - The Sweet Bread of Toledo, and Why use a multi-sensory approach?.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Meaningful Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Meaningful Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Learning that connects new information to what the learner already knows, producing durable understanding rather than temporary memorisation.

The Distinction
Rote learning deposits information in memory without connecting it to existing knowledge. It is retrieved only as long as the memory trace is fresh, and it does not generalise.
Meaningful learning integrates new knowledge into existing cognitive structures. It can be retrieved in new contexts, applied to unfamiliar problems, and built upon.

“The most important single factor influencing learning is what the learner already knows. Ascertain this and teach accordingly.” — David Ausubel — Meaningful Learning


The Conditions for Meaningful Learning
Three conditions are necessary. The material must be logically meaningful — it must have an inherent structure that makes connection possible. The learner must have relevant prior knowledge to connect it to. The learner must choose to engage meaningfully — not simply memorise.
All three are design responsibilities as much as learner responsibilities.

Connections in the Course
Meaningful learning frames the week’s pedagogical approach: activating prior knowledge before introducing frameworks, contextualising theory through experience, and building connections across disciplines and artworks.
See David Ausubel — Meaningful Learning, Experiential Learning, Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development, and Scaffolding.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Media Literacy.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Media Literacy
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The capacity to access, analyse, evaluate and create media in a variety of forms. Media literacy goes beyond the ability to use media tools — it includes critical understanding of how media shapes perception, constructs narrative and carries ideological weight.
Media literacy overlaps with AI Literacy and Visual Learning: all three involve the ability to read representations critically rather than passively.
Key questions in media literacy education:

Who produced this message, and why?
What techniques are used to attract and hold attention?
What values, perspectives or assumptions are embedded?
What is absent or omitted?
How might different audiences interpret this differently?

In the context of this course, media literacy connects to the analysis of visual art as political communication — particularly in the work of Goya, Picasso and the readings of Guernica and African Guernica.
PISA 2029 will assess media and AI literacy alongside traditional academic competences.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Mindfulness.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Mindfulness
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Mindfulness in an educational context is the practice of deliberate, non-judgmental attention — to breath, body, sensation, and present experience. It is used to regulate affect, reduce cognitive overload, and bring learners fully into the present moment before engaging with learning tasks.
It is not relaxation. It is attention training.

Why It Matters in Learning
The affective state of the learner conditions what is learned. A group arriving anxious, distracted, or dysregulated cannot engage at the same level as one that is settled and present. Brief mindfulness practices at the start of a session shift the group’s baseline.
This connects directly to Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis: when the affective filter is high (anxiety, self-consciousness, fear), input does not reach the learner. Mindfulness lowers the filter.

Practices Used in the Course
The course integrated several brief mindfulness and grounding practices:

5-4-3-2-1 Grounding Exercise — sensory anchoring through five senses
A Mindfulness Moment — Using Our Senses to Feel Grounded — sensory awareness practice
Beach Ball Breathing Technique — breath regulation through visual and kinaesthetic cue
Box Breathing Technique — structured breath pattern for calm focus
Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise — sensory memory and imaginative engagement




                  
                  Mindfulness as transition 
                  
                

Mindfulness moments work best as transitions — between arrival and work, between activity and reflection, between a challenging moment and the next task. They mark a shift in register, and that marking is itself pedagogically significant.



Connections
See Active Methodologies, Multisensory Learning, The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning, Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis, Tableau.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Multimodal Literacy.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Multimodal Literacy
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Multimodal Literacy
Multimodal literacy extends the concept of literacy beyond the written word. It describes the capacity to read, interpret and produce meaning across multiple modes: text, image, sound, gesture, movement, spatial arrangement.
In educational contexts, multimodal literacy becomes relevant wherever learning involves more than written language — which, in practice, is almost everywhere. The city, the museum, the film, the infographic, the soundscape: all require multimodal reading.
In this course, multimodal literacy appeared most explicitly in Visual Learning and in the Place and Sensory Learning session.

See Visual Learning, Media Literacy, Multisensory Learning, Place and Sensory Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Multiple Means of Action or Expression.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Multiple Means of Action or Expression
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Students demonstrate their learning in various ways:

a poster

Canva

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Multiple Means of Engagement.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Multiple Means of Engagement
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Stimulating students’ interests and motivation for learning in a variety of ways:

Share learning goals
Create and follow classroom routines
Know their strengths and weaknesses

Give choice
Expect change
Never assume


Silent and mindful moments
Flexible classrooms (comfort, atmosphere)
Reflection and feedback tools (Exit tickets)


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Multiple Means of Representation.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Multiple Means of Representation
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Present information and content in multiple formats so that all students can access it:

A poster
Images or charts
Digital tools

Digital books

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Multisensory Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Multisensory Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Multisensory Learning engages more than one sensory channel simultaneously — visual, auditory, kinaesthetic, olfactory, tactile — to produce deeper encoding and more durable retention.
The premise: when multiple senses are activated together, the brain forms richer associative networks. Memory becomes more accessible because it is anchored in more than one modality.

In Practice
The course activated multisensory learning across almost every field experience.
At the Prado: visual attention + verbal description + spatial navigation. Standing in front of Las Meninas at full scale, in real light, is not the same as seeing a reproduction on screen.
In Toledo: sound of stone streets, smell of old buildings, spatial compression of medieval architecture, the scale of El entierro del conde de Orgaz in the small church where it was painted.
Mindfulness exercises: breath, body, sound, texture. The five senses used deliberately as anchors for attention.
Gastronomy: taste, smell, and memory as a single event. Food as a multisensory cultural text.



                  
                  Multisensory is not decoration 
                  
                

Engaging multiple senses is not about making lessons more entertaining. It is about providing more retrieval routes for the same information. A learner who tasted, heard, and saw something has more pathways back to it than a learner who only read about it.



Related Notes
See Why use a multi-sensory approach?, The Role of Cognitive Science in Multisensory Learning, Mindfulness, Active Methodologies, Context4Content.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Notes on Curating Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Curating Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Curation is selection with intention.
A curator does not create the objects in their care. They select, sequence, contextualise, and leave space for the visitor’s own encounter with what is there.
The strongest teaching observed during the course operated on the same logic. The educator was not the source of meaning — they were the designer of conditions in which meaning could emerge.

What Curating Learning Looks Like
A learning curator makes decisions about:

Selection — which experiences, objects, spaces, and questions to bring into the learning environment
Sequence — in what order, at what pace, with what gaps between
Framing — how much to explain before the encounter, and how much to leave for the encounter itself
Space — where learning happens shapes what can be learned
Silence — what not to say, and when not to speak

The course introduced this concretely through the Audio Guide as Scaffolding exercise at the Prado: participants wrote their own audio guides for a painting. To explain something to someone else, you first have to decide what matters. The exercise collapsed the distance between interpreter and object.
The Prado visit worked as curated learning. A single room, a single painting, time to look before speaking. The educator’s job was to stay out of the way until the learner had seen something.

The Risk of Over-Curating
A curator who labels everything, explains every object, and leaves no room for the visitor’s own reading has stopped curating and started narrating. The same applies in teaching.
Over-scaffolding collapses the ZPD. Over-explanation eliminates inquiry. The educator who cannot tolerate ambiguity in learners tends to fill silence that should belong to the learner.



                  
                  Relation to Reflective Practice 
                  
                

Curating learning requires sustained self-observation. The educator must notice when they are explaining out of habit rather than necessity, when they are filling silence out of discomfort, and when they are choosing activities for their own satisfaction rather than the learner’s growth. See Reflective Practice.



The Hidden Curriculum of the Course
The course design itself was a model of curated learning. The educators did not teach about active methodologies — they enacted them. Every session, field visit, and transition was designed. Nothing was arbitrary.
This is perhaps the most transferable lesson from the week: the curriculum is not the slides. It is the entire designed experience — including the walk to the museum, the choice of restaurant, the moment of open time in the city.

Connections
See Active Methodologies, Context4Content, Reflective Practice, Scaffolding, Place-based Learning, Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development, Fiat Humanitas.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Numbered Heads Together.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Numbered Heads Together
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Numbered Heads Together is a Cooperative Learning structure developed by Spencer Kagan. It combines individual accountability with group interdependence: every member must be prepared to respond, because any one of them may be called upon.
How it works

Students are organised into small groups. Each member receives a number (1, 2, 3, 4).
The teacher poses a question or problem.
Groups discuss and ensure every member understands and can explain the answer.
The teacher calls a number at random. The student with that number in each group responds.

The random selection is the structural key: it prevents the group from delegating response to its strongest member. Everyone must be ready.
Why it matters pedagogically
Numbered Heads Together operationalises two of the five pillars of Cooperative Learning: positive interdependence (the group succeeds or fails together) and individual accountability (no one can coast on another’s work).
Questions the technique tends to surface in practice:

How do you form groups that balance ability and participation?
When has cooperative learning worked well in your classroom, and what made it work?
How do you maintain individual accountability without undermining group trust?
What are the main challenges, and how do you address them?

Connections
See Cooperative Learning, Forming Cooperative Groups, Individual and Group Accountability and Positive Interdependence.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/OECD AI Literacy Framework.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: OECD AI Literacy Framework
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The OECD framework for AI literacy draws on DigComp, UNESCO’s work, and Digital Promise research. It maps AI literacy across three dimensions.

Three Dimensions
Knowledge — understanding what AI is and how it works: machine learning, training data, model outputs, probabilistic reasoning, and the difference between narrow and general AI.
Skills — the practical capacity to use AI tools critically and effectively: prompting, evaluating outputs, identifying bias, understanding limitations, and applying AI to real-world problems.
Attitudes — the values and dispositions needed to engage with AI as a citizen and professional: openness to learning, ethical awareness, critical scepticism, and a sense of personal and civic responsibility toward AI’s societal effects.

The Interdependence of the Three
Knowledge without skills remains theoretical. Skills without knowledge produce uncritical use. Both without appropriate attitudes risk harm at scale. The framework treats the three as inseparable.

Connections in the Course
The OECD framework runs alongside UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies and AI Literacy as complementary maps of the same territory. The attitudinal dimension connects to Healthy scepticism and What is truth in 2026?.
The skills dimension connects directly to the week’s practical AI sessions: AI for Teaching, station rotation tools, and the use of generative AI in lesson design.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Pass the Page.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Pass the Page
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Students complete a worksheet, exercise, diagram…together by passing around the page.
Each students completes one part and passes the page to the next student.
Then the student must check, correct and answer the next part.


This engaging cooperative learning strategy is widely known as “Pass the Paper” or “Round Table”. It requires students to build on each other’s ideas, verify previous work, and actively collaborate to complete an assignment together.
How to Implement the Strategy

Form Groups: Divide students into small groups of 3 to 4 and give each group a single worksheet, graphic organizer, or diagram.
First Contribution: Student 1 completes the first section, problem, or step of the activity.
Pass and Check: Student 1 passes the page to Student 2. Student 2 must first read, check, and correct Student 1’s work before moving to the next part.
Continue the Cycle: Student 2 completes the next part, then passes the paper to Student 3, who checks the previous answers and answers the next part. This repeats until the entire page is complete.
Final Review: Once the document makes a full circle, the entire group reviews the completed page together to reach a final consensus.


Why Teachers Use It

Accountability: Students are less likely to disengage because their peers are directly waiting on their contribution.
Immediate Feedback: Requiring students to check prior work catches mistakes early in the learning process.
Peer Teaching: Students naturally explain concepts to one another as they correct and justify answers before passing the page.


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Pens in the Middle.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Pens in the Middle
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A cooperative learning technique that regulates turn-taking and ensures every participant contributes. Simple to set up, powerful in its effects.

How It Works
Groups of three to five participants sit around a table. Each person places their pen (or another small object) in the centre.
The rule: when you speak, you take your pen from the centre. When you have finished speaking, you return it. You cannot speak again until everyone else has placed their pen in the centre at least once — that is, until everyone has contributed.

What It Does
The rule makes participation visible and equitable. It prevents dominant speakers from monopolising the floor without requiring the facilitator to intervene. It creates natural pauses that make space for quieter participants. It distributes accountability: each person is both a speaker and a keeper of the group’s balance.
The physical act of picking up and returning the pen makes the social contract concrete and legible to everyone in the group.

Debrief
After the task: what did you notice about the pattern of participation? Who spoke first, who waited? Did the rule change how you listened? What happened in the silences?

Connections in the Course
See Cooperative Learning, Social Skills, Positive Interdependence, Individual and Group Accountability, and Group Processing.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Place-based Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Place-based Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
An educational approach that uses the local environment — the immediate community, landscape, city, or cultural context — as the starting point and ongoing resource for learning.

The Core Argument
Place-based learning positions context as curriculum. The street, the museum, the market, the neighbourhood, and the city are not settings for learning — they are the subject of learning.
This is not fieldwork as supplement. It is fieldwork as foundation.

The Pedagogical Logic
When learners encounter knowledge in the place where it is meaningful — studying urban history in a city square, encountering art in a museum, tasting food that carries cultural memory — the learning is contextualised, embodied, and more likely to endure.
Place provides what the classroom, by design, cannot: irreducible specificity. The thing itself, in its actual location, carrying its actual weight.

Connections in the Course
The entire Learning Landscapes week was structured as place-based learning: Madrid and Toledo as classrooms, the Prado and Reina Sofia as pedagogical environments, gastronomy and architecture as content.
See Experiential Learning, Context4Content, Curation, Toledo — The City of Three Cultures, and Gastronomy notes — Madrid as Memory, Ritual and Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Positive Interdependence.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Positive Interdependence

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Priority Diamond.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Priority Diamond
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A cooperative critical thinking technique in which groups rank nine quotations or statements by arranging them in a diamond pattern — one at the top (most important), two below, three in the middle, two below that, and one at the bottom (least important, or most contested).

The Structure
        [1]
      [2] [3]
    [4] [5] [6]
      [7] [8]
        [9]

The arrangement is not a simple ranking — it is a spatial argument. The three in the middle are “neither central nor marginal.” The two at the bottom are not necessarily wrong — they are the statements the group found hardest to place at the top.

The Nine Quotations Used in the Course
The quotations used during the programme centred on education, learning, and the future:
“Education is the most powerful weapon you can use to change the world.” — source debated.
“Tell me and I forget. Teach me and I remember. Involve me and I learn.” — attributed to Benjamin Franklin, origin debated.
“The function of education is to teach one to think intensively and to think critically.” — Martin Luther King Jr.
“Education is not filling a bucket but lighting a fire.” — W.B. Yeats (attributed).
“Intelligence plus character — that is the goal of true education.” — Martin Luther King Jr.
“The mediator between head and hands must be the heart.” — Fritz Lang, Metropolis.
“Every child is an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.” — Picasso.
“If we teach today as we taught yesterday, we rob our children of tomorrow.” — John Dewey.
“The quill is the tongue of the soul.” — Cervantes.

What the Technique Develops
Argumentation — the group must defend each placement. Active listening — disagreement must be resolved through dialogue, not volume. Tolerance for ambiguity — the diamond structure allows for “important but complex” rather than forcing binary judgement. Critical thinking — the process reveals which values each participant holds most centrally.

Connections in the Course
See Cooperative Learning, Active Methodologies, Social Skills, 21st Century Skills, and Metropolis.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Professional Engagement.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Professional Engagement
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
From the DigCompEdu framework, Area 1: Professional Engagement. Four competency areas for using digital technologies to enhance professional practice beyond the classroom.

1.1 — Organisational Communication
Using digital technologies to enhance communication with learners, families, and third parties. Contributing to collaborative organisational communication strategies.
The shift here is from broadcasting information to designing communication: choosing the right channel, register, and format for the intended audience.

1.2 — Professional Collaboration
Using digital technologies to engage in collaboration with other educators — sharing knowledge and experience, and collaboratively developing pedagogical practice.
This is the dissemination impulse expressed as a daily professional habit: what you learn from your colleagues, you share. What you develop together, you document.
See  and Disseminare.

1.3 — Reflective Practice
Individually and collectively reflecting on, critically assessing, and actively developing one’s own digital pedagogical practice and that of the wider educational community.
Reflective practice is not journal-keeping for its own sake. It is the professional discipline of using experience as data — and adjusting accordingly. See Reflective Practice.

1.4 — Digital Continuous Professional Development
Using digital sources and resources for ongoing professional learning and development.
The professional who stops learning has already begun to fall behind. In 2026, this is not an abstract warning.

Connections in the Course
See Digital Resources, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, Dissemination Strategy, and .

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Promotive Interaction.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Promotive Interaction

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Quiz-Quiz-Trade.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Quiz-Quiz-Trade
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Quiz-Quiz-Trade is a highly interactive, cooperative learning routine where students use flashcards or notes to quiz each other, coach peers, and trade cards before rotating to new partners. This kinesthetic activity boosts information retention and allows students to socialize while reviewing course material.
How to Play (The 7-Step Process)

Prepare Cards: Create a set of flashcards or notes. Put a question, vocabulary term, or prompt on one side, and the answer or definition on the back.
Distribute: Give every student exactly one card.
Stand Up & Pair Up: Students stand up, raise their hands, and find the nearest classmate who is also raising a hand.
Quiz: Partner A shows the question/term to Partner B. Partner B tries to answer. Partner A provides helpful clues if Partner B is stuck, and praises them when they get it right.
Switch Roles: The partners swap roles so that Partner B quizzes Partner A.
Trade Cards: Once both students have answered and coached each other, they trade flashcards.
Repeat: Students raise their hands to find a new partner and repeat the process.

Best Practices & Variations

Low-Pressure Prompts: Instead of vocabulary terms, try using Post-it notes with discussion prompts (e.g., “Write three ways to prioritize mental health”). Students share their answers, swap Post-its, and collect new ideas from multiple classmates.
Partner Formations: To manage movement, you can have students form two face-to-face lines. After each trade, one entire line simply shifts one step to the right to pair up with a brand-new partner.
Timer Use: Use a timer or play music to signal when it is time for students to find a new partner and keep the room’s energy high.


For tips on how to get the pacing and student interaction just right:
youtu.be/Nt8tIkyhV7A

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Reflective Practice.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Reflective Practice
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Reflective practice, developed by Donald Schön (1983), describes the capacity of professionals to examine their own actions in order to engage in a process of continuous learning. It is not self-criticism — it is disciplined self-observation in service of growth.
Schön distinguished two forms:

Reflection-in-action: thinking while doing; adjusting in the moment
Reflection-on-action: thinking after the fact; making sense of what happened


“The practitioner allows himself to experience surprise, puzzlement, or confusion in a situation which he finds uncertain or unique. He reflects on the phenomenon before him, and on the prior understandings which have been implicit in his behaviour.”
— Schön, The Reflective Practitioner (1983, p. 68)


In the Course
Reflection was woven into the structure of the week rather than added at the end. Field notes, learning portfolios, cooperative debriefs, and the format of this digital garden are all forms of reflective practice.
The assumption was that experience without reflection does not become learning. It becomes memory without meaning.



                  
                  Reflection is not summary 
                  
                

Summarising what happened is not reflection. Reflection asks what it meant, what was unexpected, what it reveals about assumptions, and what it might change in future practice. The question is not “what did I do?” but “what did I learn about learning?”



Connections
See Context4Content, Active Methodologies, Carl Rogers — Experiential Learning, Notes on Curating Learning, Experiential Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/SELFIE Tool for Teachers.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: SELFIE Tool for Teachers
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
SELFIE (Self-reflection on Effective Learning by Fostering the use of Innovative Educational technologies) is a free, customisable self-reflection tool developed by the European Commission to help educators assess their digital competence.
SELFIE for Teachers is distinct from the school-level SELFIE tool. It focuses on the individual educator’s digital practices across teaching, learning and professional development.
The tool is aligned with DigCompEdu and covers areas including:

use of digital tools in classroom practice
digital assessment and feedback
creating and adapting digital resources
professional learning and collaboration
supporting learners’ digital competence

SELFIE for Teachers is not a test. It produces a personal profile — a starting point for reflection, not a score to be optimised.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Scaffolding.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Scaffolding
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Temporary, calibrated support that enables a learner to perform something they could not yet do alone — and that is progressively withdrawn as competence develops.

The Origin
The concept was developed by Jerome Bruner, David Wood, and Gail Ross in the 1970s, building directly on Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development’s insight that learning happens at the edge of current capacity, not within it.
The metaphor is structural: a builder’s scaffold holds a wall in place while the mortar sets, then comes down. The scaffold is not the building. It is what makes the building possible. See Scaffolding (Seamus Heaney).

What Scaffolding Is Not
Scaffolding is not simplification. Reducing difficulty is not scaffolding — it is just making things easier. Scaffolding keeps the challenge and provides support for reaching it.
Scaffolding is not permanent. Support that never withdraws produces dependence, not competence. The goal of every scaffold is its own removal.

Forms of Scaffolding in Practice
Verbal scaffolding — questioning, modelling, narrating thinking aloud.
Visual scaffolding — diagrams, graphic organisers, sentence frames.
Peer scaffolding — a more capable peer working alongside a learner at their ZPD.
Environmental scaffolding — designing the learning space and sequence to make progression possible.
Technological scaffolding — digital tools that mediate access to complex content.
For CLIL-specific strategies, see Estratégias de Scaffolding para CLIL e Alunos Multilingues. For the audio guide as a scaffolding model, see Audio Guide as Scaffolding.

Connections in the Course
Scaffolding runs through everything: Cooperative Learning (peer scaffolding), CLIL (language support within content), Universal Design for Learning (structural scaffolding for all learners), and The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning (the Learning Zone requires both challenge and support).

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Scavenger Hunt.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Scavenger Hunt
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A cooperative learning technique in which groups search a physical or digital space to locate, identify, or collect specific items or information. Used as an activation strategy, a field learning structure, or an icebreaker.

The Pedagogical Logic
A scavenger hunt replaces passive reception with active discovery. Participants must look, decide, move, and collaborate — producing the kind of engaged, embodied learning that a lecture cannot.
In a museum or city context, the hunt gives learners a purpose and a structure for observation: instead of walking past without looking, they are looking for something. The constraint of the task focuses attention.

In the Course
Used during field learning visits as a structured attention exercise: participants were given specific works to find, questions to answer through careful looking, or connections between objects to identify.

Connections in the Course
See Active Methodologies, Place-based Learning, Highlights of Spanish Art, and Curation.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Shared Reading.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Shared Reading
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
This activity is effective for reading long and challenging texts.
The students take turns to have one of three roles: reader, summariser and corrector.
The roles are rotated as they continue through the text, sentence by sentence or paragraph by paragraph depending on the level of the students.
