Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Situated Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Situated Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Situated Learning, developed by Jean Lave and Etienne Wenger (1991), argues that knowledge is inseparable from the context in which it is learned and used. Learning is not the transfer of abstract knowledge into a neutral mind — it is participation in communities of practice, embedded in specific social and cultural settings.
The implication for education: authentic contexts produce more durable and transferable learning than decontextualised instruction.

“Knowledge is inseparable from activity and the context, culture, and community in which it is developed and deployed.”
— Lave & Wenger, Situated Learning (1991)


Connection to the Course
The entire week in Madrid was an exercise in situated learning. Visiting the Prado is not the same as reading about it. Standing in Toledo is not the same as watching a documentary. The learning was situated because it happened in the place it was about.

Connections
See Context4Content, Place-based Learning, Experiential Learning, Constructivist Pedagogy, Active Methodologies.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Social Skills.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Social Skills

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Stephen Krashen (1941–), American linguist, proposed the Affective Filter Hypothesis as part of his broader Input Hypothesis model of second language acquisition.

The Core Argument
Language acquisition is blocked by emotional states. Three affective variables determine how much comprehensible input a learner is able to receive and process:
Motivation — learners who want to learn acquire language more successfully. Extrinsic pressure alone does not produce acquisition.
Self-confidence — learners who believe they can succeed maintain a lower filter and engage more freely with input.
Anxiety — high anxiety raises the affective filter, blocking input from reaching the language acquisition device even when it is comprehensible.
When the filter is low, input gets through. When the filter is high, learners may be present and receiving language but not acquiring it.

Implications for Classroom Practice
The classroom environment is not neutral. An anxious, competitive, or humiliating environment raises the affective filter for every learner — particularly for those who already carry additional barriers: students with SEND, learners with little L1 literacy, recent immigrants, or anyone whose identity is not reflected in the curriculum.
Creating a low-filter environment — through trust, inclusion, routine, and genuine respect for prior knowledge — is not pastoral care. It is the prerequisite for learning.

Connections in the Course
Krashen’s hypothesis connects directly to CLIL (affective conditions for multilingual learning), Universal Design for Learning (reducing barriers), and Carl Rogers — Experiential Learning (the relational conditions for meaningful learning).
See also The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning: the Comfort Zone and the Learning Zone are, in part, affective states. The Frustration Zone is a high-filter environment.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Tableau.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Tableau
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
You can tell a story using words, but we also tell stories using images.
Tableau is a theatre technique of telling a story in frozen pictures. Students use their body position and facial expression to portray a specific part of a story.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Teaching and Learning (DigCompEdu).md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Teaching and Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
From the DigCompEdu framework, Area 3: Teaching and Learning. Four competency areas for integrating digital technologies into pedagogical practice.

3.1 — Teaching
Planning and implementing digital tools and resources in the teaching process to enhance effectiveness. Managing digital teaching interventions appropriately. Experimenting with and developing new formats and pedagogical methods.
The emphasis on experimenting is significant: digital pedagogy is not a solved problem. The practitioner is a researcher of their own practice.

3.2 — Guidance
Using digital technologies to enhance interaction with learners, individually and collectively, inside and outside the session. Offering timely and targeted guidance and support. Developing new formats for that support.
The shift from scheduled instruction to ongoing guidance changes the temporal structure of the teacher-learner relationship.

3.3 — Collaborative Learning
Using digital technologies to foster learner collaboration. Enabling learners to use digital tools as part of collaborative work — for communication, cooperation, and collaborative knowledge creation.
See Cooperative Learning and 21st Century Skills.

3.4 — Self-Regulated Learning
Using digital technologies to support learners’ self-regulated learning: helping them plan, monitor, and reflect on their own learning; provide evidence of progress; share insights; and produce creative responses.
Self-regulation is both a competence and a disposition. Active Methodologies and Empowering Learners address this from different angles.

Connections in the Course
See Professional Engagement, Digital Resources, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, Assessment, and Empowering Learners.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Team Interview.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Team Interview
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Students or teacher prepare questions about a topic and the person ‘being interviewed’ must answer them.
Take turns in being the interviewee and interviewer.

Topic: What forms of assessment do you use?


Pair Interviews
Students pair up.
Student A interviews Student B on a specific topic for a set time.


Role Reversal
Pairs reverse roles;
Student B interviews Student A for the same amount of time.


Team Sharing
Pairs join another pair to form a group of four.
Each student shares their partner’s findings with the group.



The “Team Interview” is a highly interactive activity designed to build communication skills, active listening, and empathy. Students or teachers write a list of questions about an agreed-upon topic (e.g., historical events, science concepts, or personal hobbies) and take turns acting as the interviewer and interviewee.


How to Set Up the Activity


Preparation: Have the teacher or students brainstorm a list of 5 to 10 open-ended questions based on the chosen theme.
Pair Up: Divide the group into pairs. Assign one person to be the “Interviewer” and the other to be the “Interviewee.”
First Round: The interviewer asks the prepared questions. The interviewee must answer thoughtfully, and the interviewer can take notes or ask spontaneous follow-up questions.
Switch Roles: After a set time (e.g., 5 to 10 minutes), the pair switches roles so the original interviewee gets a turn in the hot seat.



Example Topic Ideas & Questions

Depending on the learning objective, you can tailor the questions to test knowledge or build relationships:

Academic/Subject-Focused:

“If you were a historical figure from the [specific era], what would be your biggest priority?”
“How does the water cycle work in real life, and why is it important?”


Communication & Personal Interests:

“What is one goal you are hoping to accomplish this year, and why?”
“If you could only eat one food for the rest of your life, what would it be?” [1]



For tips on how to keep an interview engaging and ensure the questions lead to deep, meaningful conversations:


Alternative Formats

To keep the activity dynamic, try these variations:

The Three-Step Interview: Students work in groups of three. Student A interviews Student B, Student B answers, and Student C takes notes. They rotate until everyone has played all three roles.
Speed Interviews: Participants form two lines facing each other. They have 3 minutes to interview the person across from them. When the timer sounds, one line shifts down to interview a new partner.


Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Challenge alone produces frustration. Support alone produces comfort. Only their combination - high challenge, high support - produces learning. This is the design problem of every lesson.

Transclude of teaching-learning-zones.excalidraw
Diagram by Filipe Gama, 2026. Based on the Four Zones of Teaching and Learning.
The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning
A framework developed by Luca Mariani (1997) mapping the relationship between challenge level and support level in a learning environment. Four zones emerge from the intersection of these two dimensions.
Learning Zone: high challenge, high support. The optimal condition: the task stretches the learner, and the scaffolding is in place to make success possible. This is where growth happens. Connects directly to Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development.
Frustration Zone: high challenge, low support. The task exceeds the learner’s current capacity, and no support is available. The result is anxiety, disengagement, and shutdown. A common consequence of poor differentiation or insufficient scaffolding.
Comfort Zone: low challenge, high support. The learner is safe but not growing. Tasks are too easy; there is nothing to stretch toward. Boredom sets in. Often mistaken for a good learning environment.
Boredom Zone: low challenge, low support. Neither stimulating nor secure. The learner is neither engaged nor scaffolded. The least productive of the four conditions.

The Pedagogical Argument
The goal of instructional design is to keep learners in the Learning Zone — not by removing difficulty, but by calibrating support to match the level of challenge.
This is a dynamic condition. The same task places different learners in different zones. Differentiation is the practice of managing this complexity intentionally.


                  
                  Krashen and the Frustration Zone 
                  
                

The Frustration Zone is precisely what Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis describes: when anxiety is high and support is low, the affective filter rises and learning stops. Keeping learners in the Learning Zone is also keeping the filter down.



Connections in Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning
The four zones frame the week’s use of Cooperative Learning (peers provide support), Scaffolding, CLIL (language support within content challenge), and Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis (the Frustration Zone is a high-filter environment).
See also Universal Design for Learning for a framework that addresses the same problem from a design perspective.

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/The Learning Pyramid.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Learning Pyramid
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A model of average retention rates associated with different learning methods, commonly attributed to the National Training Laboratories (NTL) Institute, USA.

The Pyramid





































MethodAverage RetentionLecture5%Reading10%Audiovisual20%Demonstration30%Discussion group50%Practice by doing75%Teaching others90%
The model distinguishes passive methods (lecture, reading, audiovisual) from active methods (discussion, practice, teaching others). Retention rates increase as learners become more active participants in their own learning.

A Note on the Evidence
The specific percentages circulate widely but their empirical basis is contested. No peer-reviewed study has validated these exact figures. The NTL itself has not produced the original research documentation.
The model remains pedagogically useful as a directional argument — active learning produces more durable understanding than passive reception — even if the precise numbers should not be treated as scientific fact.
This is itself a lesson in healthy scepticism: a model being widely cited is not the same as it being well evidenced. See Healthy scepticism and AI Literacy.

Connections in the Course
The pyramid underpins the week’s commitment to Active Methodologies: collaborative tasks, museum field learning, peer teaching, and cooperative techniques all sit at the high-retention end of the model. The lecture was not the primary mode.
See also Cooperative Learning and The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/The Role of Cognitive Science in Multisensory Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Role of Cognitive Science in Multisensory Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The cognitive science basis for multisensory learning rests primarily on dual coding theory, developed by Allan Paivio (1971).

Dual Coding Theory
Paivio proposed that the brain uses two distinct but interconnected systems to process information: a verbal system (for language and linguistic information) and a non-verbal system (for images, spatial information, and sensory input).
When information is encoded through both systems simultaneously — when a concept is read and also seen as an image, heard and also felt physically — the resulting memory trace is stronger, more distributed, and more easily retrieved.
The implication for learning design: combining verbal and visual (or other sensory) modes does not simply add variety. It produces qualitatively richer encoding.

The Working Memory Connection
Working memory has limited capacity. Overloading a single channel (presenting dense text while also speaking, for example) degrades processing. Distributing information across multiple sensory channels makes more efficient use of available cognitive capacity.
Multisensory design is, among other things, cognitive load management.

Connections in the Course
Dual coding theory connects to Why use a multi-sensory approach?, Universal Design for Learning, and the design of the museum visits as experiences that combined visual attention, movement, verbal reflection, and social meaning-making.
See also Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences for a parallel, broader argument about cognitive diversity.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/The SAMR Model.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The SAMR Model
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Developed by Ruben Puentedura, SAMR describes four levels at which technology integrates into learning — from simple replacement to genuine transformation.

The Four Levels
Substitution — technology acts as a direct substitute for an existing tool, with no functional change. A student types an essay instead of handwriting it.
Augmentation — technology acts as a substitute with some functional improvement. The same essay now has spell-check, word count, and easy revision.
Modification — technology allows for significant task redesign. The essay becomes a collaborative document, edited simultaneously by peers, with comment threads and revision history.
Redefinition — technology creates tasks that were previously inconceivable. The student publishes to a real audience, receives responses from across Europe, and iterates based on genuine feedback.

The Distinction That Matters
The first two levels (Substitution and Augmentation) involve enhancement — doing the same things better. The second two (Modification and Redefinition) involve transformation — doing things that were not previously possible.
SAMR is not a hierarchy of virtue: Substitution is sometimes the right tool. The question is whether the choice is intentional and appropriate.

Connections in the Course
The SAMR model underpins the week’s approach to AI Literacy and Digital Resources: technology is not inherently transformative; its value depends on the pedagogical decisions surrounding its use.
See also Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence and UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Think-Pair-Share.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Think-Pair-Share
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
“What was the process from thinking to sharing?
Did your expression change or improve?”


Think-Pair-Share is a collaborative learning strategy where participants independently reflect on a prompt (Think), discuss their ideas with a partner (Pair), and present their synthesized thoughts to the broader group (Share). It maximizes engagement and builds confidence in a safe environment.

The 3-Step Process

Think: The instructor poses an open-ended question. Participants reflect and gather their thoughts independently, often jotting down notes.
Pair: Participants turn to a partner to compare notes, debate viewpoints, and synthesize their answers into a joint response.
Share: Pairs present their combined insights to the larger group, allowing for a structured and inclusive collective discussion

Why It Works

Maximizes Participation: It ensures every single person is involved rather than just the most vocal participants.
Builds Confidence: Rehearsing a response with one partner reduces the anxiety of speaking directly to a large group.
Enhances Comprehension: It gives time to process information critically and form deeper insights.

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/UDL — Three Principles.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: 3 Principles for Universal Design for Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Multiple Means of Engagement
Stimulate motivation and sustained enthusiasm for learning by promoting various ways of engaging with material.


Multiple Means of Representation
Present information and content in a variety of ways to support understanding by students with different learning styles/abilities.


Multiple Means of Action or Expression
Offer options for students to demonstrate their learning in various ways (e.g. allow choice of assessment type).



Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Published by UNESCO as part of its broader work on AI in education. The framework maps competencies for both students and teachers across five levels of AI literacy.

For Students
The framework identifies five progressive competency levels:
Level 1 — AI Awareness: Understanding that AI exists, what it does, and where it appears in daily life.
Level 2 — AI Use: Ability to use AI tools effectively, evaluate their outputs critically, and understand their limitations.
Level 3 — AI Understanding: Conceptual understanding of how AI systems work — training data, algorithms, outputs, biases.
Level 4 — AI Evaluation: Capacity to assess AI applications ethically and critically: fairness, transparency, accountability, societal impact.
Level 5 — AI Creation: Ability to design, build, or meaningfully contribute to AI systems and their governance.

For Teachers
The teacher framework runs in parallel, with additional emphasis on:
Integrating AI tools into pedagogical practice with critical awareness. Designing learning experiences that develop students’ AI literacies. Modelling critical and ethical engagement with AI outputs. Understanding institutional and policy dimensions of AI in education.

Connections in the Course
The UNESCO framework connects to AI Literacy, OECD AI Literacy Framework, and Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence. The week’s approach to AI — practical use alongside critical reflection — sits at Levels 2–4.
The question “What is truth in 2026?” is a Level 4 question. See What is truth in 2026? and Healthy scepticism.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Universal Design for Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Universal Design for Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
“Universal Design is the design and composition of an environment so that it can be accessed, understood and used to the greatest extent possible by all people regardless of their age, size, ability or disability.”
Centre for Excellence in Universal Design, Ireland

UDL is a set of principles for curriculum development that gives all individuals equal opportunities to learn. It minimises barriers and maximises outcomes by starting from one foundational premise: every learner is highly variable.
No learner is just one thing. We all have strengths and weaknesses. UDL is not a special needs programme — it is a mindset that makes education work better for everyone.

Equality vs Equity
Equality assumes everyone benefits from the same support. Equity gives different support to reach the same access. UDL goes further: it removes the structural barriers so that no accommodation is needed in the first place.


                  
                  Equality · Equity · UDL 
                  
                

Equality gives everyone the same step. Equity gives taller steps to shorter people. UDL removes the need for steps altogether — by designing an environment that works for everyone from the start.



The Three Principles
Multiple Means of Engagement — the why of learning. Stimulate motivation and sustained effort through flexible ways of engaging with material. Recognise that interest, persistence, and self-regulation are not fixed traits — they are conditions to be designed.
Multiple Means of Representation — the what of learning. Present information in multiple formats: text, image, audio, movement. Learners perceive and process differently; no single format reaches all.
Multiple Means of Action or Expression — the how of learning. Offer learners different ways to demonstrate what they know. Choice of assessment type is not a lowering of standards — it is recognition that performance is variable across modes.

Three Levels of Implementation
UDL operates across the system, not just the classroom.
At the macro level: legislation, national standards, policy adoption.
At the meso level: school governance, institutional procedures, community links.
At the micro level: classroom design, teaching practices, assistive technologies, co-design with learners.
Real UDL requires alignment across all three.



                  
                  UDL and the Prado 
                  
                

The Goya puzzle activity at the Prado was UDL in action: multiple entry points (visual, tactile, narrative), multiple modes (looking, discussing, arranging), one shared experience. No learner was excluded by their starting point.


Application: Learner Variability in Practice
One session example: working with immigrant students.
Avoid segregation — place students in mainstream classes and provide support within them. Build inclusive classrooms physically (visual aids, language labels) and emotionally (valuing home cultures and languages). Use peer mentoring, breaking-the-ice activities, and resources designed to work for everyone from the start.
UDL in action is not about adding accommodations. It is about designing so that fewer accommodations are ever needed.

Connection to the Course
Addressed in Universal Design for Learning (Wednesday). Connected to Learner Variability, Inclusive Education, Scaffolding, Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences.
The film Hidden Potential was used as a discussion anchor. The Goya puzzle activity at the Prado was itself an example of UDL in practice — multiple entry points, multiple modes, one shared experience.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Urban Pedagogy.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Urban Pedagogy
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Urban pedagogy treats the city as a learning environment — not a backdrop for learning, but an active site of knowledge. The built environment, public space, human flow, commercial culture, and historical sediment all carry meaning that can be read, interpreted, and learned from.
The educator’s role in this context is the curator: selecting which spaces to enter, framing the question to bring to them, and leaving room for the learner’s own encounter.

The City as Text
A city can be read. Its architecture, naming conventions, commercial signage, street art, and patterns of use all encode social, political, and historical information. Urban pedagogy develops the literacy to read these signs — not abstractly, but while walking through them.
Key capacities it develops:

spatial awareness and orientation
observation and attention to detail
semiotic reading (signs, symbols, representations)
historical inference from material culture
critical questioning of what is visible and what is not


In Practice
The Images of Madrid walking tour (Day 1) was structured urban pedagogy. Participants moved through the city with a task, a group, and a defined observational frame. The city was the classroom. The scavenger hunt format used game mechanics to keep the inquiry active. See Gamification, Place-based Learning.

Connections
See Context4Content, Place-based Learning, Inquiry-Based Learning, Gamification, Intercultural Education, Situated Learning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Visual Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Visual Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Visual Learning is not simply learning that uses images. It is the development of the capacity to read images — to extract meaning from composition, colour, symbol, spatial arrangement, and absence.
In the context of this course, art was used as methodology, not decoration. The Prado and the Reina Sofía were field sites, not museums to visit. The question was never “what is this?” but “what does this do, and how?”

Visual Literacy
Visual literacy is the ability to interpret, analyse, and produce meaning through visual media. It includes:

reading composition and spatial relationships
recognising symbol, allegory, and narrative convention
questioning representation: who is shown, and how? Who is absent?
understanding the historical and social context of an image
producing visual work with intention


In Practice
At the Prado: slow looking at a single work. Sitting with a painting long enough for the familiar to become strange.
Images of Madrid (Day 1): photography as a tool for observation and analysis. The camera as a thinking instrument, not a recording device.
Guernica and its dialogues: reading Picasso’s visual language; reading Dumile Feni and Al-Hawajri in response to it. Visual argument across time and geography.



                  
                  Art as methodology 
                  
                

The shift from “art as content” to “art as methodology” is significant. When learners are asked not to learn about art but to learn through art — to use it as a tool for thinking — the work changes. So does the learner.



Connections
See Active Methodologies, Inquiry-Based Learning, Multisensory Learning, Context4Content, Las Meninas, Guernica, Fiat Humanitas.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Lev Vygotsky (1896–1934), Soviet psychologist. His concept of the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) remains one of the most generative frameworks in educational theory.

“Proximal refers to skills the learner is close to mastering.”


The Zone of Proximal Development

“What a child can do with assistance today, she will be able to do by herself tomorrow.”

The ZPD is the space between what a learner can do independently and what they can do with the support of a more capable other — a teacher, peer, or well-designed tool.

“The zone of proximal development refers to the difference between what a learner can do without help and what he or she can achieve with guidance and encouragement from a skilled partner.”

Learning does not happen at the level of what is already mastered. It happens at the edge: where the task is just beyond current reach, but within reach with support.

“Learning is more than the acquisition of the ability to think; it is the acquisition of many specialized abilities for thinking about a variety of things.”




                  
                  The ZPD in one image 
                  
                

Think of three concentric circles. The innermost: what the learner can do alone. The middle ring: the Zone of Proximal Development — what they can do with support. The outer ring: what is not yet reachable. Learning happens in the middle ring. The teacher’s job is to work there.


Scaffolding as the Operative Concept
The ZPD implies scaffolding: temporary, calibrated support that enables the learner to perform what they could not perform alone — and that is withdrawn progressively as competence develops.
Good scaffolding is not simplification. It is structured support at the right level of challenge.


                  
                  ZPD and the peer 
                  
                

Vygotsky’s “more capable other” does not have to be the teacher. A peer who is slightly further ahead sits inside the learner’s ZPD and can provide exactly the right level of challenge. This is the theoretical foundation of Cooperative Learning.



Connections in the Course
The ZPD runs through every technique and methodology encountered in the week: cooperative learning (the more capable peer), CLIL (language support within content learning), scaffolding strategies, and the design of graduated challenge in museum visits and field projects.
See Scaffolding, The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning, CLIL, and Estratégias de Scaffolding para CLIL e Alunos Multilingues.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Walking Pedagogy.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Walking Pedagogy
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Walking Pedagogy
Walking pedagogy treats the walk — through a city, a landscape, a museum — as a pedagogical structure, not a transit between learning moments.
Walking changes the relationship between learner and environment. The pace slows, peripheral attention widens, unexpected encounters become possible. The learner is not reading about a place; they are moving through it.
In this course, the Images of Madrid walking tour was the most structured instance. El Rastro on Sunday morning and Casa de Campo on Tuesday afternoon were the unstructured ones. All three were pedagogically intentional.

See Place-based Learning, Context4Content, Situated Learning, Embodied Learning, Field Learning Projects.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/Why use a multi-sensory approach?.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Why Use a Multi-Sensory Approach?
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The case for multisensory learning is grounded in cognitive science, not pedagogical fashion.

Memory and Recall
The brain encodes information through multiple pathways simultaneously. When an experience engages sight, sound, touch, smell, and movement together, it creates a richer and more interconnected memory trace. Retrieval is easier because there are more routes back to the same information.
A single-channel input (reading alone, or listening alone) produces thinner encoding. Multisensory input produces denser, more durable memory.

Engagement and Attention
Sensory variety sustains attention. A learning environment that shifts between visual, auditory, kinaesthetic, and social modes reduces the cognitive fatigue that comes from sustained single-channel processing.
Novelty and sensory change are neurologically alerting — they signal that something worth attending to is happening.

Inclusion
Multisensory design is inherently more inclusive. Learners with dyslexia, ADHD, processing differences, or limited language access benefit significantly from alternative entry points. What begins as an accommodation becomes a design principle that improves learning for everyone.
This connects directly to Universal Design for Learning: design for the margins, benefit the centre.

Connections in the Course
The multisensory principle underpins the week’s use of museum visits (visual, spatial, affective), mindfulness exercises (bodily and sensory anchoring), gastronomy as pedagogy (Gastronomy notes — Madrid as Memory, Ritual and Learning), and the marzipan tasting as a structured sensory experience.
See The Role of Cognitive Science in Multisensory Learning for the theoretical basis.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Frameworks/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Frameworks
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Educational Frameworks


                  
                  John Dewey 
                  
                

“If we teach today as we taught yesterday, we rob our children of tomorrow.”


The frameworks in this garden are not a curriculum. They are lenses. Each one was introduced during the course and then tested against real contexts: a classroom in Madrid, a museum in the Prado, the streets of Toledo.
Some of these frameworks you already use without naming them. That’s the point. Naming them gives you a handle. A handle gives you a way to share them with others.


                  
                  Active Methodologies 
                  
                

Learning as something you do, not something that happens to you.
Active Methodologies is the umbrella. The course didn’t introduce these as theory. It enacted them, session after session, so you experienced them as a learner before reflecting on them as an educator.
Structures for cooperative work included Think-Pair-Share, Jigsaw, Let’s Have a Date, Pass the Page, Shared Reading, Team Interview and Numbered Heads Together. Forming Cooperative Groups was its own conversation: random, teacher-selected or self-selected, each with implications for equity and dynamics. Some appear multiple times, each instance as a methodology in action rather than a description of one.
Also worth noting: Freewrite, Priority Diamond and Quiz-Quiz-Trade as lightweight structures for reflection, critical thinking and review.




                  
                  Cooperative Learning 
                  
                

Cooperative Learning rests on five pillars: positive interdependence, individual and group accountability, promotive interaction, social skills and group processing.
The theoretical anchors include Vygotsky’s Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development, Ausubel’s Meaningful Learning, Rogers’s Experiential Learning and Krashen’s Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis. Gardner’s Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences also appeared here, as a reminder that learners are not one thing.




                  
                  Inclusive and Differentiated Learning 
                  
                

Designing for everyone, not adjusting for some.
Universal Design for Learning starts from one assumption: every learner is highly variable. The three principles of UDL, multiple means of engagement, representation and expression, were explored through a film, group discussion and a direct comparison between traditional and UDL classroom activities.
Related frameworks: CLIL, Scaffolding and Multiple Means of Representation. On the CLIL side, BICS and CALP surfaced as one of the more practical distinctions of the week, alongside Expressing Opinions using PREP as a scaffolding structure for expressing and organising opinions.




                  
                  Technology and Digital Competences 
                  
                

Which door are you trying to open? Which key do you need?
The The SAMR Model (Substitution, Augmentation, Modification, Redefinition) framed how technology integration is a spectrum, not a checklist. DigCompEdu, the European Digital Competence Framework for Educators, and the SELFIE Tool for Teachers provided a self-assessment lens.
AI Literacy was positioned as distinct from digital literacy, drawing on UNESCO, OECD and the EU AI Act frameworks, with a horizon marker: PISA 2029 will assess media and AI literacy.
Tools explored in practice: Mentimeter, NotebookLM, Curipod, Padlet Arcade, Genially, Webwise. Not as mandatory tools to adopt, but as examples of SAMR levels in action.




                  
                  Literacies 
                  
                

Reading the world, not just the word.
Visual Literacy, AI Literacy and Media Literacy belong together. The Prado made visual literacy concrete in ways no slide could. A Goya puzzle, a fragmented narrative, a painting reassembled before visiting the original. The question “what can you see, what do you think is happening?” is both a CLIL strategy and a visual literacy practice.




                  
                  Experiential Learning 
                  
                

The body learns too.
Place-based Learning, Sensory Learning, Embodied Learning and Mindfulness share a thread: context is not a backdrop. It is part of the learning itself.
Tableau appeared as a theatre technique for embodied storytelling. The marzapan tasting was a mindfulness exercise and a history lesson at the same time. The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique and beach ball breathing were introduced as practical tools for sensory regulation, with a direct note on Sensory overload and the learners for whom sensory richness is not an asset.
Toledo was not a field trip. It was a methodology.




                  
                  Still finding their place 
                  
                

Dissemination and Notes on European Dimension resist easy categorisation. They are frameworks for what happens after learning and for the larger context that makes this kind of learning possible. They will find their place.



Part of Footscapes. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Portfolio Development/Perspective & Reflection — Innovative and Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Perspective & Reflection — Innovative and Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The presentation was designed as a visual and sensory argument, not just a slide deck.
Each slide is anchored in a painting seen that week at the Prado. The works don’t illustrate the concepts — they carry them. The transition from slide 1 to slide 2 is a zoom-out of Las Meninas: a detail becomes a whole composition, asking the viewer to step back and see the bigger picture. It was Sofia, the Prado guide, who had said exactly that — different vantage points change everything you think you understand about a painting, a classroom, a student. The zoom-out is her argument made visible.
Between slides, the frames that surround each painting extend beyond the image — lines suggesting walls, ceiling, floor, vanishing points. Perspective lines as architecture. The presentation itself practises what it argues: that the way you frame something determines what you can see inside it.
The final moment is a multisensory activity. The audience is asked to produce a sound based on an image they are about to see. The image is a green pedestrian traffic light — the one found throughout Madrid, with its distinctive piu piu piu of small birds. The group reacted before they understood why. That is synaesthesia in practice — the same principle introduced during the Place and Sensory Learning session the morning before. The presentation did not explain the concept. It activated it.

Slide 1 — Teaching as Active, Reflective and Collaborative Practice
Visual: Velázquez, Las Meninas, zoomed in.

Teaching as active, reflective and collaborative practice
Connecting art, language, culture and pedagogy
Learning through observation, interaction and experience

Erasmus+ Team — Portugal, Italy, Germany
Slide 2 — From Micro to Macro
Visual: Las Meninas, zoom-out.

From details to universal meaning
The bigger picture
Observation and interpretation
Connecting disciplines
Visual literacy

Small details lead students toward broader cultural, social and interdisciplinary understanding.
Slide 3 — The Teacher as Artist and Facilitator
Visual: El Greco, El caballero de la mano en el pecho.

Teacher as observer and as facilitator
Self-observation and vision for students
Empathy and building new habits
Mindfulness in the classroom and classroom as creative space

Teaching is both an artistic and human process grounded in empathy, creativity and reflection.
Slide 4 — Perspective, Inclusion and UDL
Visual: Jacopo Tintoretto, El Lavatorio.

Universal Design for Learning and SEN
Learning through multiple perspectives
Style and substance
Teacher as facilitator

Inclusive learning environments emerge when teaching adapts to different perspectives and needs.
Slide 5 — Communication, CLIL and Digital Competences
Visual: Hieronymus Bosch, Tríptico del Jardín de las delicias.

CLIL, CALP and BICS
Scaffolding
Cooperative and interactive methodologies
Communication and digital competences

Communication, structure and collaboration support meaningful multilingual learning.
Slide 6 — Context, Culture and European Experience
Visual: Goya, El 2 de mayo de 1808 en Madrid.

Spain: Madrid and Toledo
History, atmosphere and school visits
Discovery and immersive experiences
Time and space
European dimension

Learning becomes more meaningful when connected to culture, mobility and shared European experiences.
Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Portfolio Development/Portfolio Development.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Portfolio Development
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Portfolio Development
First Draft — Tuesday, 6 May 2026
Tuesday lunchtime. No agenda, no structure yet.
The first drafts happened between sessions — over lunch, on phones, on paper. The assignment was to document the week’s learning in portfolio form. What came out were fragments: keywords, sketches, frameworks half-remembered from the morning, questions without answers.

Draft presentation, lunch | Day 3 © Filipe Gama

Team first drafts during lunch | Day 3 © Team
The question at this stage was not “what do we present?” but “what did we actually do this week?”

Mind Map — Thursday, 7 May 2026
Thursday afternoon. The two groups merged.
Eight participants had spent the week in two separate groups — two Portuguese, two German and four Italian participants — parallel in programme but not in experience. At lunch on Thursday they decided that presenting two portfolios made no sense. The afternoon became a single collaborative act of synthesis.

Presentation draft, team work | Day 5 © Team
The format became a mind map. Central node: “Innovative and Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning.” Radiating outward: Perspective, Frameworks, Teacher, Content and Context, Time and Space, Communication, Cooperation, The Bigger Picture.

Filipe (PT), Sílvia (IT), Mónica (PT), Julia (DE) and Maria Grazia (IT) brainstorming | Day 5 © Team
When the question arose of what to anchor the visual argument, the artworks offered themselves. Las Meninas for perspective. El 3 de mayo en Madrid for context and time. Las tres Gracias for cooperation — three figures in relation, a circular composition with no fixed beginning or end.

A mind map of metaphors and visual anchors

Refinements — Friday, 8 May 2026
Friday evening. The team separated to refine independently.
The structure established on Thursday remained intact. What changed was the precision: the language of each node tightened, the slide transitions clarified, the opening activity finalised. The presentation stayed anchored to the same three paintings. Finished in the early hours of Saturday morning.

Presentation — Saturday, 9 May 2026
Delivered in the last formal session of the course, to the course tutor Megan and to the parallel AI for Education group.
“I love a metaphor,” Megan replied.
See Perspective & Reflection — Innovative and Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning for the full presentation note.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/03-05-2026 - Sunday/20260503 - European Dimension.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on European Dimension
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The course established its posture before it named its methodology. Madrid is not a neutral location. The city was chosen. So was the framing. Before any framework was introduced, the room was already organized around a single idea: learning is European, professional identity is shared and the space belongs to everyone equally.

Portuguese team opens a “European song contest” with Barco Negro, by Amália | Day 1 © English Matters
The phrase that kept returning was from teachers to teachers. No hierarchy. No stage. A community of practice that moves across borders and brings something back.
European Dimension made this explicit. Images of Spain grounded it in place. The chronological journey since Altamira was panoramic and largely institutional, but its throughline was clear: coexistence produces culture. Toledo, Córdoba and the Alhambra not as interruptions in a national narrative, but as evidence of what happens when cultures build on each other rather than erase each other. Context4Content  before the term had been introduced. The monument as document. The city as curriculum. The past as pedagogical resource.

Notes on European Dimension

Promoting Intercultural Learning and the European Dimension in Education.

Madrid is not a neutral location. The city was chosen. So was the framing. Before any methodology was named, the course established a posture: learning is European, professional identity is shared and the room belongs to everyone equally.
The European Dimension is not a policy. It is a posture.

English Matters describes itself as working From teachers to teachers. The phrase appeared on the second slide and kept returning throughout the week. It is worth pausing on what it actually means.
Transclude of English-Matters
9+1 countries | 14 schools | 28 participants
Transclude of From-teachers-to-teachers
The course structure followed from this. Three axes ran through the whole week: the European Dimension, the Context4Content approach to Field Learning Projects, and the Learning Portfolios as instrument of reflection and dissemination. These were not modules. They were postures, present in every session whether named or not.
The European Dimension itself was framed not as policy or bureaucracy but as a set of values made visible through practice: openness, connectedness, sharing, networking, Intercultural Education. The room in Madrid had nine participating countries plus Spain. That fact alone was part of the curriculum.
What the session established, before any methodology was introduced, was a commitment: the Erasmus+ Spirit is not incidental to the learning. It is the condition for it.

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/03-05-2026 - Sunday/Building a European Dimension.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Building a European Dimension
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The day closed with a quiz about Spain, which was also an icebreaker, which was also Game-Based Learning before it was named as such. The point was not the answers. It was the conversation around them, the laughter, the surprise, the first moments of a group becoming a group. Though one answer travelled further than the others: Goya appeared, framed not as court painter but as war reporter. It was 3 May. In Madrid. The coincidence required no commentary.

Quiz time! Team “Parque del Retiro”: Mara (IT), Claudia (IT), Julia (DE) and Filipe (PT) | Day 1 © English Matters
Structure creates connection. Connection creates community. Community creates the conditions for learning.

The day closed with a quiz about Spain.
It was also an icebreaker. It was also Game-Based Learning before anyone called it that. The point was never the answers. It was the conversation around them, the laughter, the surprise, the first moments of a group becoming a group. Twelve tables. Nine countries. Twenty-eight participants who had arrived as strangers and were leaving as something more than that.
The quiz did something else quietly. It extended the Images of Spain session into a participatory mode. Knowledge that had been received became knowledge negotiated, tested, debated across a table. That shift, from reception to interaction, from audience to participant, was itself a small demonstration of the active methodology the week would spend seven days exploring.
Structure creates connection. Connection creates community. Community creates the conditions for learning.

One answer from the quiz is worth carrying forward.
Among the questions about Spanish geography and culture, a painting appeared: El 3 de Mayo de 1808 en Madrid, Francisco de Goya y Lucientes. The facilitator offered a framing that did not come from an art history textbook: Goya not as court painter — which he also was — but as war reporter. Someone who stayed in the city during the French occupation. Who looked at close range. Who painted, six years later, what he had witnessed: an execution, at night, on the outskirts of Madrid, ordinary people in front of rifles they did not choose.
It was 3 May 2026. In Madrid.
The coincidence was not announced. It did not need to be. But the weight of that date, and that painting, in that city, carried something no slide could have planned for. History does not repeat itself. It rhymes.
That image would return. At the Prado, later in the week. In the conversations about art as testimony that kept surfacing in unexpected places. In Fiat Humanitas, the final note of the whole journey. Goya was the first of several artists the programme would use, deliberately or not, to anchor pedagogical arguments in specific human moments. Picasso would follow. So would Hieronymus Bosch. So would Diego Velázquez. Art as document. As moral witness. As the kind of evidence that stays long after the quiz answer is forgotten.

Kudos to Equipa Parque del Retiro.

References

Game-Based Learning
Active Methodologies
European Dimension in Education
Collaborative Learning
Erasmus+ Spirit
Goya
Fiat Humanitas


Part of European Dimension & Foundations, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes
