Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/03-05-2026 - Sunday/Images of Spain.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Images of Spain
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Notes on Images of Spain

How did the Spanish become who they are?

The session moved chronologically from Altamira to 2026. Cave paintings from 35,000 BCE. Phoenician gold from Seville. Roman Hispania with its aqueduct at Segovia and its theatre at Mérida, and the quiet detail that Seneca, Trajan, Hadrian and Marcus Aurelius all came from the peninsula. The Visigoths. Toledo as capital. Al-Andalus. The Mosque of Córdoba. The Alhambra. The Reconquista mapped in four stages across four centuries. The Catholic Monarchs. Felipe II and the empire that reached the Philippines. Carlos III, described as the best mayor Madrid ever had, who built primary schools and decreed all professions honourable. The Civil War. Franco. 1978.
The selection was panoramic and largely institutional. A national narrative, coherent and legible, oriented toward heritage, continuity and artistic legacy. What it did not hold much space for were the tensions inside that chronology: the colonial shadow of the empire, the violence of the Inquisition, the expulsion of Jews and Muslims in 1492, the complexity of what reconquista means depending on who is telling the story.
And yet the throughline that gave the session its pedagogical force was precisely coexistence. Toledo as Visigothic capital before it became the city of three cultures. Córdoba as the intellectual centre of medieval Europe under Moorish rule. The Alhambra not as interruption but as foundation. The session showed, almost despite itself, that Spain’s identity is hybrid, layered and built from contact as much as from conflict.
The session closed with Federico García Lorca’s Canción de Jinete, a poem that carries all of that weight in a few lines. Córdoba. Far away and lonely. It was a good choice. History as image, as sound, as felt experience. Heritage as Curriculum in practice, before the term had been introduced.
This is Context4Content working at the level of historical narrative. The monument as document. The poem as evidence. The past as a resource for making sense of the present.

References

Context4Content
Heritage as Curriculum
Cultural Immersion
Intercultural Education
Visual Learning
European Dimension in Education
Place-based Learning


Part of European Dimension & Foundations, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/03-05-2026 - Sunday/Tools for Reflection and Professional Development.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Tools for Reflection and Professional Development
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
Tools for Reflection and Professional Development grounded the day in a different register. Not history, but identity. Not Spain, but the educator in the room.
The questions were never “what do you know?” They were “who are you as an educator?” and “what do you want to transform?” Reflective Practice was introduced here as a professional competence, not an afterthought. The Learning Portfolios appeared for the first time, not as assessment, but as a living document: part reflection, part evidence, part dissemination tool. It would travel home and keep growing.
The question it kept asking was simple: what are you doing with what you are learning?


“We do not learn from experience. We learn from reflecting on experience.”
John Dewey

The quote was on the first slide. It functioned less as citation and more as manifesto.
The session introduced the Learning Portfolios not as an assessment instrument but as a living document. Part reflective journal, part evidence of learning, part dissemination tool. It would travel home and keep growing after the course ended.
The evaluation framework made the course’s priorities explicit. Instruments included the portfolio itself, a final survey, a participant report and a training organisation report. Criteria covered participation in group activities and debates, general understanding of content, use of field projects and cultural experiences for dissemination, and the Erasmus+ Spirit. That last criterion is worth holding: the spirit of the experience was formally assessed. Belonging, openness and engagement were not incidental. They were part of what the course was measuring.
The questions underpinning the portfolio were equally revealing. Not “what did you learn technically?” but rather: what cultural differences did you notice? What pedagogical challenges do you face? How are you using a second language? What connections will you maintain? What will you apply? What did you learn outside the classroom?
The focus was on transformation, not content acquisition. The portfolio was asking who you are becoming as an educator, not what you now know.
This is Reflective Practice understood as professional competence. Metacognition not as an add-on but as a core outcome. And the dissemination expectation embedded from day one: learning that does not travel back to your school, your students, your colleagues, remains incomplete.

References

Reflective Practice
Learning Portfolios
Professional Development
Metacognition
European Dimension in Education
Erasmus+ Spirit


Part of European Dimension & Foundations, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/03-05-2026 - Sunday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 1 - Sunday, 3 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
European Dimension & Foundations
Overview
Before the course began, there was a market.
On the morning before the official start, a small Portuguese group made their way to La Latina for pan con tomate and then down into El Rastro, Madrid’s Sunday street market. No agenda, no task card, no Seesaw. Just a city doing what cities do on Sunday mornings, and a group of educators paying attention. That walk became the first learning of the week, before learning had been officially scheduled.
El Rastro is the first of the Field Learning Projects.
The official day opened with European Dimension, moved through Images of Spain and Tools for Reflection and Professional Development, and closed with Building a European Dimension. The sequence was not accidental. First, build belonging. Then, locate that belonging in a place. Then, ask what you will do with it when you go home.

Programme
index#03052026---sunday

Notes on European Dimension & Foundations
Before anything else, there was a European Song Contest. Each country offered something. The Portuguese group opened with Barco Negro, a Fado by Amália. It didn’t need explaining.
The Erasmus+ Spirit is real. It is also constructed, deliberately, session by session, meal by meal, conversation by conversation. By the end of Sunday, twelve groups of strangers had a shared reference point. That is not accidental. It is design.
And underneath everything, quietly, the commitment that would only be named on the last day: Fiat Europa. Make Europe. Not as bureaucracy. As daily practice, in every classroom, in every opportunity, in every lesson that opens a window instead of closing one.
The week had begun.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/04-05-2026 - Monday/21st Century Skills.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on 21st Century Skills
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A framework for the competencies learners need to thrive in contemporary professional and civic life. Commonly grouped into four clusters: the 4Cs of Learning, Literacy Skills, Life Skills, and Career Skills.

The 4Cs of Learning
Critical Thinking
working together toward a shared goal; combining individual strengths through collaborative structures. The social dimension of knowledge construction.

Station 1, Critical Thinking activity: Priority Diamond | Day 2 © Filipe Gama

Priority Diamond | Day 2 © Filipe Gama

Priority Diamond quotes | Day 2 © Filipe Gama
Creativity
The ability to evaluate evidence, separate fact from opinion, and resist the pull of uncritical acceptance. Asking why, how do we know, and whose perspective is this.

Station 3, Creativity: Design a Learning Experience | Day 2 © Filipe Gama

Station 3, “Democracy”, a learning experience explained with chess pieces | Day 2 © Filipe Gama
Communication
Conveying ideas with clarity and purpose; reading the audience and adjusting register, format, and complexity accordingly.
Collaboration
Generating original ideas, approaches, and solutions; thinking outside established conventions; taking creative risks.
Note: the source materials for this section used inconsistent labelling across files. The above reflects the corrected mapping of content to competency.

Literacy Skills
Information Literacy — locating, evaluating, and applying information from multiple sources, including the capacity to distinguish reliable from unreliable content. Connects to Healthy scepticism and AI Literacy.
Media Literacy — understanding how media messages are constructed, what interests they serve, and how they shape perception. Connects to What is truth in 2026? and Las Meninas.
Technology Literacy — digital proficiency; the capacity to use tools independently and to conduct independent research. Connects to Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence and the five DigComp areas.

Life Skills
Flexibility — adapting to new contexts, navigating social situations with appropriate register, and maintaining productive working relationships across difference.
Initiative — intrinsic motivation, personal work ethic, and the capacity to act without waiting for permission or instruction.
Productivity — managing time and attention; completing work to standard within available constraints.
Leadership — setting goals for a group, guiding collaborative effort, and taking responsibility for shared outcomes.

Expression
The ability to demonstrate learning and communicate understanding in multiple formats — written, verbal, visual, spatial, and performative. Connects to Universal Design for Learning (multiple means of action and expression) and Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences.

Connections in the Course
The 21st Century Skills framework ran through the entire week: cooperative tasks built collaboration and communication; museum visits required critical and creative thinking; reflective writing and multimodal outputs developed expression. See Future of Jobs Report 2025 for the current labour market evidence for these skills.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/04-05-2026 - Monday/Being an Educator in 2026 and beyond.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Being an Educator in 2026 and beyond
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The morning divided cleanly into two parts. Pedagogically, both served the same purpose.
The course was not asking what teachers should teach. It was asking who teachers are, and who they are becoming. That shift, from content to identity, from transmission to relation, ran through everything that Monday brought.
The day opened with Being an Educator in 2026 and beyond and moved into 21st Century Skills, each session building on the one before. Not through accumulation, but through repetition of a single underlying argument: that learning happens through experience, and that the teacher’s role is to design that experience, not deliver information.

Megan (UK), the course tutor, during the “21st century skills” session | Day 2 © Team
What made this particularly coherent was that the sessions were not merely discussing active methodologies. They were enacting them. Participants were not told about collaboration. They collaborated. They were not explained critical thinking. They were asked to hierachise, negotiate, justify and reflect. The course kept practicing what it preached, and that consistency was itself a pedagogical statement.


Portuguese team lunch | Day 2 © Team

The session never announced its argument. It simply enacted it.
Rather than opening with a framework or a lecture on what teaching means today, the facilitator started with people in a room who did not yet know each other. The first task, Let’s Have a Date, sent participants moving through the space, sitting briefly with person after person, answering questions that had nothing to do with curriculum or content. What challenges do you face as an educator? What do you want to transform? What does innovation mean in your context?
By the time the activity ended, the group had already practised community-building, reduced the social anxiety of an unfamiliar setting, and experienced horizontal exchange with no central authority. Nobody explained what Active Methodologies look like. They did it first.
That sequence was itself the session’s core argument: the contemporary educator is not primarily a transmitter of information. The role is relational, reflective and adaptive. And the best way to make that case is to create conditions where participants feel it before they name it.
The Let’s Have a Date activity did something else that is easy to miss. The questions chosen for the speed-dating round were not about curriculum, exams or content. They were about identity, challenge, transformation and belief. That selection revealed the philosophical priorities of the course before any framework was introduced.
The freewrite that followed did something equally important. After the noise and movement of the dating activity, writing alone slowed everything down. It individualised the experience and turned interaction into Reflective Practice. The metacognitive habit the course would return to throughout the week, that awareness of one’s own process as a learner, began here quietly, on a blank page.
What the session did not do is worth noting as well. There was no heavy theoretical scaffold, no reading list, no passive content delivery. The vision of the teacher as facilitator, as someone who designs experiences rather than delivers knowledge, was present in the room’s architecture before it was ever stated aloud.

References:

Active Methodologies
Reflective Practice
Let’s Have a Date


Part of Educator Identity, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/04-05-2026 - Monday/Images of Madrid - a walking tour.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Field Learning project 1
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Meeting point:
Plaza Mayor: equestrian statue of Philip III,
in the centre of the square. Monday - 16:00



Go to your AppStore and download the Seesaw Learning App.
There are 2 Groups (Parque del Retiro & Jardines de Sabatini)

Follow your tutor’s instructions for the tour and the task provided.
Please stay with the group!
Share your place photos on Seesaw if you can.
The activity lasts 90 minutes but you are free to stop for a break.
The activity ends when you want it to end.
Starting point: Plaza Mayor




Madrid Quiz

1 point for a photo taken in each location (at least two members of your group must be in each photo). Bonus points available for original/creative poses!
1 point for each question answered correctly. 


1. Plaza Mayor
The Plaza Mayor was once the centre of old Madrid. It was completed in 1620 and in the past was used for many purposes, including bullfights, processions, fiestas and theatrical productions, trials of the Inquisition, and even executions.

Team gathered at Plaza Mayor | Day 2 © English Matters

2. Restaurante Botin
The restaurant Botin is the oldest restaurant in the world and is a common destination for tourists. Their speciality is suckling pig cooked in the same oven that was used when the restaurant first opened. 

Claudia (IT) and Julia (DE) at Restaurante Botín | Day 2 © Team

Restaurante Botín has a Guinness record for being the oldest restaurant in the world | Day 2 © Team

3. Plaza de la Villa
This square has some of the oldest buildings in the city and was one of the main medieval centres of Madrid.
The oldest building is thought to be the tower of the Lujanes. The legend of this tower is that King Francis I of France was kept captive here in 1525 after losing in battle to Carlos I. When Francis I refused to bow to Carlos I, the king of Spain had the entrance doorway lowered so that Francis was forced to bow every time he left or entered the building.

Julia (DE), Mara (IT), Filipe (PT) and Claudia (IT) at Plaza de la Villa | Day 2 © Team

Claudia and Julia bowing at the tower of Lujanes, just as King Francis I of France was forced to do before Carlos I of Spain | Day 2 © Team

4. Almudena cathedral
Building work on the Almudena cathedral started in 1893, but it was not completed until 1993. One of the reasons for this delay was the civil war in the 1930s.
If you walk north past the main cathedral entrance, you will see a square on your left. On your right you have the back of the palace, and to your left you have the cathedral de Almudena. 

In front of the Almudena Cathedral, Filipe (PT) welcomed his colleagues to his crib, the Palacio Real | Day 2 © Team

5. The Royal Palace
The Royal Palace of Madrid is the largest in Western Europe and one of the largest in the world. It was built by Carlos V, the first king in the Bourbon dynasty (the same as the current Spanish Royal family. With over 135,000 square metres and 3,418 rooms, it has witnessed centuries of Spanish history.
Outside the palace there is a plaque commemorating heroes from ‘2 de mayo de 1808’

Filipe (PT) and Claudia (IT) at “El 2 de Mayo de 1808” plaque | Day 2 © Team

6. Plaza de Oriente
From Plaza de Oriente you can see the palace and the Royal Opera House. The man on the statue is Philip IV. When he ordered the statue to be constructed, he wanted the horse to be rearing, which was very unusual for the time. The Italian sculptor Tacca couldn’t think of a way to achieve this (so that the statue wouldn’t fall over), so he wrote to Galileo Galilei for help. 

Mara (IT) sunbathing at Plaza de Oriente | Day 2 © Team

Julia (DE) and Claudia (IT) looking for Galileo’s secret at the Philip IV statue, Plaza de Oriente | Day 2 © Team

Julia (DE) and Claudia (IT) found Galileo’s secret for achieving Tacca’s sculpture stability | Day 2 © Team

7. San Gines chocolateria
A traditional snack in Madrid is a cup of hot chocolate with churros (a type of long doughnut). 
Cacao was brought back to Spain from South America in the 16th century. People then adapted it by adding sugar and cinnamon. By the 17th century thick, hot chocolate was very popular with the Spanish elite and then later with the general public. 
The San Gines chocolateria is one of the most famous places to try this. If you have time to spare you could try some!

Claudia (IT) was disappointed by the long queue for “churros con chocolate” at Chocolatería San Ginés | Day 2 © Team

8. Sol 
The Puerta del Sol (gate of the sun) is one of the most famous squares in Madrid and is a common meeting place for both tourists and residents. In the centre is a statue of Carlos III. The large white building was built in the 18th century and was built as Madrid’s central post office. 
Since the 1990s it has been the headquarters of Madrid’s regional government. 
The bear and strawberry tree statue represents the Madrid coat of arms. Medieval Madrid had lots of forests and bears were very common. The ‘strawberry’ tree is in fact an unedo tree. This is a tree which is common to Spain and Portugal and has small red fruits which can be used to make liqueur. 

Julia (DE) and Claudia (IT) touching the golden parts of the bear statue for good luck, Puerta del Sol | Day 2 © Team

Bonus challenges
Sol is the point from which all Spanish roads are measured.

Julia (DE), Mara (IT) and Claudia (IT) touching the Km0 for extra points | Day 2 © Team

The whole “Parque del Retiro” team touched the Km0 plaque | Day 2 © Team
Madrid is famous for its ‘bocadillo de calamares’ (squid sandwiches).

Mara (IT), Claudia (IT) and Julia (DE) found a restaurant with a “Bocadillos de Calamares” sandwich picture on its menu, and it gave the team 2 extra points | Day 2 © Team

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/04-05-2026 - Monday/Station Rotation.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Station Rotation
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Critical Thinking

At the critical thinking station, participants arranged nine quotes into a Priority Diamond, negotiating hierarchy and justifying their choices. The activity asked for structured thinking, not right answers. Perspective-taking and metacognition were built into the instructions, not added as extras.

Collaboration

At the collaboration station, a school budget-cut scenario assigned incompatible roles: teacher, parent, student representative, principal, business manager. The Consensus Challenge introduced real conflict and asked for a negotiated proposal. It was applied social learning, and it was uncomfortable in the right ways.

Creativity

At the creativity station, the instruction was explicit: no direct instruction, textbooks, or passive content delivery. Participants were asked to redesign a lesson from scratch using the Design a Learning Experience prompt. The constraint was the point. Removing familiar tools forces the question of what learning actually requires.

Communication

At the communication station, the task was to explain a complex concept to a fifth-grader and then to a peer. That gap, between social fluency and academic depth, maps directly onto the BICS and CALP distinction the course would return to on Wednesday. Communicating without losing meaning is harder than it sounds, and harder still when the audience keeps changing.

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/04-05-2026 - Monday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 2 - Monday, 4 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Educator Identity and City as Curriculum
Overview
What does it mean to teach in 2026? The day opened with Being an Educator in 2026 and beyond and moved into 21st Century Skills.
In the afternoon, Context4Content walked us into the streets looking for Images of Madrid. When the tour ended, both groups, Parque del Retiro and Jardines de Sabatini, found each other at a terrace nearby. Cañas, tapas, ten countries around the same table. The Erasmus+ Spirit does not need a session plan.
Later, the city offered something unplanned: Sala Equis.

Programme
index#04052026---monday

Notes on Educator Identity
Three questions stayed with me at the end of the day.
The first: if the sessions themselves model what good teaching looks like, what does that say about the gap between what teachers experience in training and what they reproduce in their own classrooms?
The second: the 4Cs (critical thinking, collaboration, creativity, communication) appear as consensus. But consensus in pedagogy deserves the same sceptical attention we ask students to apply everywhere else. Who defines these skills? For whom? Toward what ends?
The third, quieter: how much of what happened today was learning, and how much was belonging? And does the distinction matter?
Later, the city offered something unplanned: Sala Equis. On its wall, Metropolis — Fritz Lang’s 1927 film, silent, projected without sound. The central line of the film played on loop while we talked about teaching: The mediator between the head and the hands must be the heart. Nobody had planned it. Nobody could have.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/05-05-2026 - Tuesday/20260505 - Cooperative Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Cooperative Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
Cooperative Learning opened with a theoretical frame that was unusually explicit for this programme. The session named its sources: Vygotsky’s Zone of Proximal Development, Ausubel’s Meaningful Learning, Rogers’s Experiential Learning, Krashen’s Affective Filter Hypothesis, Gardner’s Multiple Intelligences. Each theory, viewed separately, offers a partial account of how people learn. Together, they converge on the same claim: learning is social, affective and constructed in relation to others.
What the session did particularly well was insist on a distinction that matters in practice. Cooperative Learning is not group work. Group work puts students in proximity. Cooperative Learning structures what happens between them, and holds both the group and each individual accountable for the outcome. The 5 Pillars of Cooperative Learning (Positive Interdependence, Individual and Group Accountability, Promotive Interaction, Social Skills, Group Processing) are the architecture that makes that accountability possible.

Julia (DE), Filipe (PT), Maria Grazia (IT) and Mara (IT) working cooperatively | Day 3 © English Matters
The practical component did what the course has consistently done well: it made the methodology available through experience. Participants moved through several techniques — Think-Pair-Share, Quiz-Quiz-Trade, Pens in the Middle, Let’s Have a Date, Pass the Page, Numbered Heads Together, Team Interview, Shared Reading. The question after each was not “what is this technique?” but “what did you notice about your own process?”

Claudia (IT) and Filipe (PT) during a “Think-Pair-Share” activity | Day 3 © English Matters
The session also addressed group formation honestly: self-selected groups tend toward homogeneity. Teacher-selected heterogeneous groups create more friction, and more learning. The tension between student autonomy and pedagogical intent was not fully resolved, but it was honestly named.

Notes on Cooperative Learning
The session opened by naming its theoretical roots before introducing any technique. That sequence was intentional: the frameworks came first, so that the practical tools that followed could be understood as applications of principles, not isolated tricks.

Theoretical Foundations
Five theorists grounded the session. Together they form a coherent argument: that learning is social, affective and constructed.
Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences. Students learn in different ways and bring different cognitive strengths to a task. Cooperative structures create conditions for those strengths to surface, circulate and become visible to others.
David Ausubel — Meaningful Learning. New information takes root when connected to prior knowledge. “Meaningful Learning occurs when the new information is related to prior knowledge.” The student is at the centre of the process; the teacher is a facilitator. Learning happens not because content was delivered, but because connections were made.
Lev Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development. “The zone of proximal development refers to the difference between what a learner can do without help and what he or she can achieve with guidance and encouragement from a skilled partner.” Proximal refers to skills the learner is close to mastering. Cooperative structures are, in effect, deliberate ZPD arrangements: the more capable peer is not the teacher alone. It is the group.
Carl Rogers — Experiential Learning. Learning is inseparable from emotion. “We feel, therefore we learn.” Education that includes personal change and self-knowledge is not a luxury. It is the condition for retention and transfer. Rogers insisted that learning connected to growth and self-awareness is qualitatively different from content absorbed and forgotten.
Stephen Krashen — Affective Filter Hypothesis. Three variables shape how open a learner is to input: motivation, self-confidence and anxiety. High anxiety and low self-esteem raise the filter and block acquisition. Cooperative structures, when well-designed, lower the filter. Particularly relevant for students with SEND, language learners and anyone operating under conditions of social vulnerability.

The Core Definition
“Cooperative learning is a form of active learning where students work together to perform specific tasks in a small group previously organised by the teacher.”
Two details matter here. The group is organised by the teacher, not self-selected. The task is specific, not open-ended. Without structure, what looks like cooperative learning is often just proximity.
“Students work together to maximise their own and each other’s learning.”
The goal is not task completion. It is learning, individual and collective, simultaneously.

The 5 Pillars
The architecture that distinguishes cooperative learning from group work.
1. Positive Interdependence — “We need each other to succeed.” No one succeeds alone. If one member fails, the group fails. This is structural, not motivational: the task has to be designed so that interdependence is real, not assumed.
2. Individual and Group Accountability — “We’re accountable to somebody for our thinking and contributions.” Each member contributes. Each member is visible. Accountability prevents passengers and makes the individual’s role legible to the group.
3. Promotive Interaction — “Everyone has the chance to participate in something valuable.” Sharing, supporting, encouraging, praising, helping one another. The group actively facilitates each member’s learning, not just the completion of the task.
4. Social Skills — “We’re always moving around and working with people that are different from us.” Leadership, decision-making, trust-building, communication, conflict management. These are not assumed. They are taught explicitly, because students do not automatically know how to work well together.
5. Group Processing — “We know what skills will help us communicate and succeed in school and life.” The group reflects on its own functioning. What worked? What did not? How do we maintain effective relationships? This pillar closes the loop and makes learning about collaboration itself, not just through it.

Techniques
The session moved through several cooperative structures, experienced rather than simply described.
Think-Pair-Share. Individual thinking, pair discussion, group sharing. Simple and high-transfer. The reflective question after practice: “What was the process from thinking to sharing? Did your expression change or improve?” That question is the technique’s real payload.
Quiz-Quiz-Trade. Participants quiz each other in pairs, then trade cards and find new partners. High-energy, good for vocabulary, factual content and review. The trading mechanism ensures everyone practices both asking and answering.
Pens in the Middle. Group discussion with pens placed in the centre as a visual commitment device. When you take your pen back, you are done contributing and others have the floor. A simple way to manage turn-taking and prevent dominance.
Date Time. The teacher prepares 12 questions. Students schedule appointments for each hour slot in advance. The teacher calls a time; students meet their booked partner and answer together. Good for discussion, review and mixing the room without social awkwardness.
Pass the Page. Collaborative task completion, one section per student. Each student reviews and extends the previous contribution before adding their own. The page records both the thinking and the progression.
Numbered Heads Together. Groups discuss a question; members are numbered; the teacher calls a number and that student reports for the group. Everyone prepares, because anyone might be called. Questions used in the session: How should you form groups? When has cooperative learning worked well in your experience? How do you ensure individual accountability? What challenges have you encountered?
Team Interview. Structured interviewer and interviewee roles within a group, then sharing out to the whole class. Questions are prepared in advance. The role rotation ensures everyone experiences both sides.
Shared Reading. Three roles rotating across the group: reader, summariser, corrector. Particularly useful for long or demanding texts. The rotation means no one holds a single cognitive position for too long.

Forming Groups
Three approaches, each with different implications.
Random selection (colours, cards, tickets): low social risk, high novelty. Works well for short tasks where relationship continuity is not the goal.
Teacher-selected heterogeneous groups: the most pedagogically sound option in most contexts. Distributes profiles strategically. Balances academic levels, language abilities, personality types. Requires the teacher to know their class, which is itself a reason to favour it.
Self-selected groups: the most common and the most problematic. High achievers cluster. Some students are consistently left out or self-exclude. The session noted this honestly: “The teacher knows his/her class best.” Which is precisely the argument for not delegating group formation to students by default.

One Distinction Worth Keeping
Cooperative Learning is not group work.
Group work places students in proximity. Cooperative Learning structures what happens between them, assigns accountability to individuals and to the collective, and makes social skills an explicit object of learning. The five pillars are the difference between a productive structure and an uncomfortable Friday afternoon.

References

5 Pillars of Cooperative Learning
Think-Pair-Share
Quiz-Quiz-Trade
Let’s Have a Date
Pass the Page
Numbered Heads Together
Team Interview
Shared Reading
Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development
Ausubel
Meaningful Learning
Rogers
Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis
Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences
Active Methodologies


Part of Cooperative & Language Integrated Learning, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/05-05-2026 - Tuesday/Content and Language Integrated Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Content and Language Integrated Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
Content and Language Integrated Learning opened with a question posed as a multiple-choice: is CLIL a method, a necessity, a mindset, or all of the above? The correct answer, the session implied, is that the frame matters less than the practitioner’s posture.
The plumber metaphor was the session’s most generative image. A plumber does not apply the same technique to every pipe. They diagnose. They adapt. They know that the leak is often a symptom of something structural, and they work with the tools they have and the context they are in. No household is the same. The question it left open was pointed: how much unlearning is necessary? Every new framework implies letting go of an older, less adequate one. This is uncomfortable in ways the session did not fully resolve, and perhaps should not. Some things need time.
The session covered the core architecture of a CLIL lesson: integrating language and content, using multimodal input, building in Scaffolding, attending to both HOTS and LOTS, correcting language errors without interrupting cognition. Scaffolding appeared as the central technical concept: providing temporary supportive structures gradually removed as independence develops. The point was equity. The goal is not to simplify content but to make access possible. Everyone reaches equivalent outcomes through different levels of support.
BICS and CALP surfaced briefly but usefully: social fluency and academic language proficiency are not the same thing, and students who seem fluent may still be operating without the cognitive-academic register the subject requires. Expressing Opinions using PREP was offered as a practical scaffolding tool for oral argumentation: Point, Reason, Example, Point. Simple enough to teach, flexible enough to adapt across subjects.

Notes on Content and Language Integrated Learning
The session opened with a question posed as a multiple-choice: is CLIL a method, a necessity, a mindset, or all of the above? The answer was never stated directly. It was implied by everything that followed.

The Plumber
The session’s most generative image came early.

Mihaela (IE), CLIL tutor, asking “What if teachers worked like plumbers?” | Day 3 © Team
”What if teachers worked like plumbers?”
A plumber arrives with the right tools, the right training, the right mindset. No household is the same. The leak is a symptom, not the whole problem. Things can be fixed, but only if the diagnosis is honest and the tool is chosen for the job, not applied by habit.
Translated into pedagogy: a CLIL teacher does not apply a fixed sequence to every class. They read the room. They adapt content and language demands simultaneously. They understand that a student struggling with academic language is not struggling with intelligence.
The metaphor holds because it resists the idea of CLIL as a method to be implemented correctly. It positions the teacher as a practitioner who diagnoses, adapts and works with what is in front of them.
The sharpest question of the day came here: “How much unlearning is necessary?”
Every new framework implies something left behind. Every shift in posture requires letting go of a prior certainty. This is uncomfortable. The session did not pretend otherwise, and did not rush past it.

What Is CLIL
CLIL — Content and Language Integrated Learning — integrates subject content teaching with explicit attention to the language through which that content is learned and expressed.
It is most associated with foreign language instruction but its logic applies anywhere language is the medium through which thinking becomes visible. Which is everywhere.
Two poles in practice:
Soft CLIL embeds language support into content teaching without restructuring the lesson around dual objectives. Language development happens through content, but is not foregrounded.
Hard CLIL organises the lesson around both content and language objectives from the start. Both are planned for, assessed and treated as equally real learning targets.
The question behind both: who is responsible for language? The session’s implicit answer: every teacher is, to some degree, a language teacher. Some do it deliberately. Some do not know they are doing it at all.

The CLIL Teacher
A CLIL teacher:

integrates language and content objectives in lesson design
activates both language and content knowledge at the start of the lesson
uses multimodal input (visual, oral, written, gestural)
adapts materials to language level without reducing cognitive demand
organises structured speaking and writing tasks
corrects language errors without interrupting cognitive flow
assesses both subject knowledge and language development
thinks explicitly about HOTS (Higher-Order Thinking Skills) vs LOTS (Lower-Order Thinking Skills)
monitors comprehension continuously
paraphrases and switches registers as needed
knows subject-specific terminology and teaches it explicitly
motivates students by making language visible as a tool, not a barrier
improves language proficiency as a by-product of subject teaching

The tension in practice: content teachers trained in a subject discipline are not always trained in language observation. Becoming a CLIL teacher requires adding a layer of attention that was never part of the original role. That is the unlearning the session was pointing at.

Scaffolding
The central technical concept of the session.
“Providing students with temporary supportive structures gradually removed as independence develops.” (Riddett, 2015; Gibbons, 2002)
The purpose: “Lowering the barriers to comprehension.”
The principle: everyone reaches equivalent outcomes. What varies is the level and type of scaffolding, and the pace at which it is withdrawn. This is not differentiation as separate tracks. It is differentiation as equitable access to the same destination.
Scaffolding is not simplification. Simplifying content reduces cognitive demand. Scaffolding maintains cognitive demand and changes the conditions of access.
This is a precise and important distinction. A scaffolded task is harder to design than a simplified one. It asks the teacher to hold two things simultaneously: the rigour of the learning objective and the specific barrier a particular learner faces.

BICS and CALP
BICS and CALP: A distinction worth carrying beyond language classrooms.
BICS — Basic Interpersonal Communication Skills: the conversational fluency students develop in informal social contexts. Fast to acquire (two to three years). Can mask underlying gaps in academic language.
CALP — Cognitive Academic Language Proficiency: the register required for academic tasks, argumentation, abstract thinking and discipline-specific vocabulary. Takes significantly longer to develop (five to seven years in a second language context).
A student who sounds fluent may still be operating without the cognitive-academic register the subject requires. This matters in every content classroom where fluency is assumed and academic struggle is misread as a comprehension failure.

PREP
A practical scaffolding structure for oral argumentation. Simple enough to teach in ten minutes. Flexible enough to adapt across subjects and levels.
Point — state your position

In my opinion / I believe / I reckon / I think / If you ask me…

Reason — explain why

…because…

Example — provide evidence or illustration
Point — restate your position as conclusion
The structure works because it externalises the logic of argument. Students who struggle with academic oral production often have the thinking but not the language to organise it. PREP provides the scaffolding without replacing the thought.

Transclude of Active-Reading

The Jigsaw
Jigsaw is a cooperative structure adapted for language tasks. Each student or group receives a different piece of the text or content. Understanding the whole requires exchange. Language is the tool of that exchange. The design guarantees that everyone speaks, because everyone holds information the others need.

Julia (DE) adding a piece to the CLIL jigsaw | Day 3 © English Matters

CLIL jigsaw complete | Day 3 © Filipe Gama

The team completed the CLIL jigsaw puzzle | Day 3 © English Matters

One Question Worth Keeping
“Who are your students’ other teachers?”
The question points to something systemic. A CLIL approach does not happen in one lesson or one teacher’s practice. Language development is cumulative, cross-disciplinary and social. What one teacher builds, another can dismantle. The question is whether those teachers know what each other is doing. Usually, they do not.

References

Scaffolding
BICS and CALP
Expressing Opinions using PREP
Jigsaw
HOTS and LOTS
Soft CLIL
Hard CLIL
Gibbons, P. (2002). Scaffolding Language, Scaffolding Learning
Riddett (2015)
Active Reading
Cooperative Learning
Reflective Practice


Part of Cooperative & Language Integrated Learning, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/05-05-2026 - Tuesday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 3 - Tuesday, 5 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Cooperative & Language Integrated Learning
Overview
Tuesday arrived with a small ceremony. Before the sessions began, the group voted on the best photographs from the walking tour of Madrid. The Parque del Retiro team won. A fitting start for a day that would, in many ways, be about how we see, frame and share what we encounter together.
The morning was the most theoretically grounded of the programme so far. Cooperative Learning brought together the theoretical roots of everything the group had been living since the beginning. CLIL followed, insisting that language is not separate from content, and that every teacher is, to some degree, a language teacher.
The afternoon programme listed Autonomous Portfolio Work. What it became was Casa de Campo, Madrid’s vast urban forest, where the programme continued without an agenda. Space. Movement. Observation. Deceleration.

Programme
index#05052026---tuesday

Notes on Cooperative & Language Integrated Learning
Tuesday felt like the moment the course paused to look at itself in the mirror. The techniques we had been experiencing since Sunday finally received their names and their theoretical roots. Cooperative Learning is not group work. CLIL is not a method. Scaffolding is not simplification. These distinctions matter. They are the difference between doing something and understanding why it works, and for whom, and under what conditions.
The question asked during the CLIL session stays with me: how much unlearning is necessary? Every new framework implies something left behind. Every tool in the plumber’s belt requires letting go of an older, less adequate one. This is uncomfortable in ways the day did not fully address, and perhaps should not. Some things need time before they can be named.
Casa de Campo offered exactly that time. No task, no output, no reflection prompt. Just a park. Just walking. And the strange, productive feeling that everything from the morning was continuing, quietly, at a different pace.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/06-05-2026 - Wednesday/20260506 — Universal Design for Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Universal Design for Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The morning opened with a question before it offered a definition. Not “what is UDL?” but “what are your barriers?” The question was split deliberately: barriers this week, so far, and barriers as a learner, in general. That move did something precise: it shifted the conversation about inclusion away from a category of students who need accommodation and toward every person in the room.
Universal Design for Learning was introduced as a mindset, not a checklist. Its three principles — Multiple Means of Engagement, Multiple Means of Representation, Multiple Means of Action and Expression — described a philosophy of design before they described a set of classroom techniques. The session modelled what it was teaching: the activities themselves offered multiple entry points, multiple forms of output, and multiple moments of reflection. Participants experienced learner variability rather than simply hearing about it.
The book pages activity was the session’s most precise pedagogical move. Pages from a text, given individually without context. The experience of fragmentation and uncertainty was not incidental. It was the point: that is what many learners feel when content arrives without scaffolding. Understanding followed from feeling it, not from being told about it.
Before the group left for the Prado, a Goya painting puzzle appeared on screen. What can you see? What do you think is happening? The visit had already begun.
Digital Competences and Artificial Intelligence followed with a question that sharpened everything that came before: As educators, should we just limit ourselves to adapting our tools to respond to technology, or can we begin to shape the technology itself? The session covered DigCompEdu, SELFIE for Teachers, the The SAMR Model, and the emerging frameworks for AI literacy from UNESCO and the OECD. It closed with a Station Rotation through eight tools. One of them, Padlet Arcade, ran on Guernica. The Picasso thread had entered the room.

Notes on Universal Design for Learning
The session opened not with a definition but with a question: “What are your barriers?”
Divided deliberately into two: barriers this week, so far, and barriers as a learner in general. The distinction mattered. The second question did not ask about students. It asked about the people in the room. That is where UDL begins: with the recognition that every learner is highly variable, and that the professional in front of a class is not exempt from that variability.

The Definition and the Distinction
“Universal Design for Learning is a set of principles for curriculum development that give all individuals equal opportunities to learn.”
The key distinction in the session: UDL is a mindset, not a technique.
At its core, it is not about retrofitting accessible versions of inaccessible lessons. It is about designing for variability from the start, so that the exception becomes the norm and the adaptation is built in rather than added on afterward. The difference between accommodating diversity and designing for it is not semantic. It changes what you make before the lesson exists.
“The UDL approach to teaching minimises barriers and maximises outcomes for all learners.”

Three Principles
Multiple Means of Engagement — the why of learning. Motivation, interest, belonging, participation, autonomy. Different learners engage differently, and engagement is not a fixed trait of the student. It is a function of the design.
Multiple Means of Representation — the what of learning. Text, image, audio, video, diagrams, manipulation, multimodal input. Information presented in one form excludes learners for whom that form is a barrier. Representing content in multiple ways is not redundancy. It is access.
Multiple Means of Action and Expression — the how of learning. Oral, written, visual, digital, practical, collaborative. How students demonstrate what they know is as much a design choice as what they are asked to learn.
The session modelled all three. The activities offered multiple entry points. The synthesis task asked participants to choose how to represent their learning: a written note, a voice note, a drawing or comic, a sequence of questions. UDL was enacted as it was being described.


“Group of 4: Numbered Heads Together” activity, after viewing “Hidden Potential” short film | Day 4 © Filipe Gama

Three Levels
UDL is not only a classroom framework. It operates across three scales simultaneously.
Macro level — directives, legislative acts, national and regional standards and policies. The framework requires systemic adoption, not individual goodwill.
Meso level — institutional governance, school policies, community and family links, the conditions for sustaining inclusive innovation over time.
Micro level — teaching practices, classroom design, assistive technologies, learning spaces, co-design with learners. This is where most UDL conversations begin, but the session made clear it cannot stay here.
The implication: a teacher who designs for UDL inside an institution that does not support it is working against the current. The question “How could your school improve on their current approach to UDL?” was not rhetorical.

UDL with Immigrant Students
A specific application that the session addressed directly.

Place students in mainstream classes and provide support rather than segregate.
Design inclusive classrooms physically: language labels, visual aids, scaffolded teaching materials.
Design them emotionally: value home cultures and languages, treat the first language as a resource.
Use breaking-the-ice activities, peer mentoring, and resources that work for everyone.

The underlying principle: the conditions that support immigrant learners are not separate from good teaching. They are good teaching.

The Book Pages Activity
The most precise pedagogical move of the session.
Each participant received pages from a text, individually, without context. The task: look at your page. What is happening in the images? How is the man feeling?
Then pairs: look at both pages together and discuss what you think is happening. How do you feel looking at the pages?
Then groups: work together to put the pages into the correct order.

The team trying to reorder Shaun Tan’s “The Arrival” pages | Day 4 © English Matters

Caster (DE) interpreting the graphic novel “The Arrival” | Day 4 © Filipe Gama
The discomfort of fragmented information without context was the content of the activity. Not an illustration of what learners experience. The actual experience. Understanding followed from feeling, and the metacognitive question that closed the session — “What different forms of representation were used in this series of activities?” — made that explicit: the session had been performing UDL on itself.

The Goya Puzzle
Before the group left for the afternoon, a Goya painting appeared on screen as a puzzle. Can you put this painting back together to see this moment in history? Then: What can you see? What do you think is happening?
The two questions that follow good looking: observation before interpretation. Inference before explanation. The UDL session had been building toward the Prado visit, and the visit had already begun.

References

UDL — Three Principles
Multiple Means of Engagement
Multiple Means of Representation
Multiple Means of Action or Expression
Learner Variability
Scaffolding
Inclusive Education
Goya
Highlights of Spanish Art
Context4Content
Active Methodologies


Part of UDL, AI & Museum Learning, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/06-05-2026 - Wednesday/CLIL Essentials — Mehisto & Lucietto.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: CLIL Essentials — Mehisto & Lucietto (2010/2011)
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
CLIL Essentials — Mehisto & Lucietto (2010/2011)
Reference framework for quality CLIL design. Distributed as supplementary reading in connection with the CLIL sessions.
The framework presents CLIL not as a method to be applied but as a set of conditions to be maintained. Twelve principles, each pointing at a different dimension of what makes CLIL work — and what makes it fail when absent.

What CLIL Is Not
Before the principles: the framework is explicit about what CLIL is not.
It is not simply delivering a subject in English. It is not an elitist approach for high achievers. It does not replace or devalue the first language. Linguistic acquisition happens through cognitively authentic tasks, not through exposure alone.

The Twelve Principles
1. Managing the Affective Side. Learning content in an additional language can generate anxiety, self-consciousness and fear of error. The classroom must be a safe space: high expectations for all, no ridicule, no sarcasm, the error treated as part of the process rather than evidence of failure. The affective filter (Krashen) applies here directly.
2. Making Intended Learning Explicit and Visible. Students need to know what they are learning, why it matters, and how they will demonstrate it. In CLIL, objectives must be dual: content and language. “We are studying volcanoes” is not a learning objective. “We will identify tectonic plates and explain their interactions using scientific analogy” is.
3. Making Academic Language Visible. Conversational fluency and academic language proficiency are not the same thing (BICS/CALP). The CLIL teacher explicitly teaches: discipline-specific vocabulary, connectors, argumentative structures, formal registers. Academic language is treated as a cognitive tool, not an ornament.
4. Reflecting on Learning. Metacognition is built into the lesson structure, not added at the end. Reflection covers content, language and learning strategies: What did I learn? What language did I use? What worked? What was difficult?
5. Fostering Critical Thinking. CLIL must not reduce to memorisation. Bloom’s taxonomy revised: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate, Create. The higher order operations require more complex language, and more complex language develops through higher order thinking. The two are inseparable.
6. Taking Time for Making Learning Meaningful. Less content, more depth. Connections to students’ lives, interests and prior knowledge. Meaningful learning (Ausubel) transfers and lasts. Surface coverage does not.
7. Scaffolding Content. Temporary support structures that reduce cognitive load without reducing cognitive demand. Graphic organisers, visual supports, analogies, chunked tasks, activated prior knowledge. The scaffolding is withdrawn gradually as independence develops.
8. Scaffolding Language. The language of the subject requires its own support. Sentence frames, writing frames, modelled phrases, vocabulary organised by function rather than topic. “I think that… because…” The structure externalises the logic of the argument until the learner has internalised it.
9. Cooperative Learning. Structured collaboration increases authentic language use, negotiation of meaning, and social safety. The principles of cooperative learning (positive interdependence, individual accountability, promotive interaction) apply fully in CLIL contexts, where the incentive to communicate is also the incentive to learn.
10. Fostering Learner Autonomy. Students who can monitor their own progress, choose strategies, and manage their learning are not dependent on a specific teacher or context. CLIL builds toward self-directed learners who can continue beyond the classroom.
11. Connecting with CLIL Language Speakers and Cultures. The language of instruction is also a window onto other cultures, communities and ways of knowing. eTwinning, exchanges, authentic contact with speakers: the language becomes a tool of genuine intercultural communication rather than a school subject.
12. Asymmetry in Classroom Talk in Favour of Students. The CLIL classroom should feature less presentational talk and more exploratory talk. Students negotiate meaning, build hypotheses, argue, question. The teacher facilitates, models and creates conditions for language to happen. Language is acquired through use.

The Eight Scaffolding Strategies for Multilingual Learners
(From practice-based research in ELL/multilingual contexts)
1. Visuals and Realia — images, objects, real-world items that contextualise vocabulary and concept before text.
2. Modelling and Gestures — demonstrating rather than explaining; physical enactment of processes; non-verbal communication as comprehension support.
3. Connecting to Background Knowledge — activating prior experience before introducing new content; analogies, questions, images that build a bridge.
4. Graphic Organisers — tables, timelines, Venn diagrams, mind maps; visual structures that reduce cognitive load by externalising organisation.
5. Sentence Structures and Starters — “The main cause was…”, “In comparison with…”, “I think… because…”; language frames that allow participation before full linguistic mastery.
6. Read Aloud — complex texts read expressively, displayed visually; gives access to grade-level content before independent reading is possible.
7. Intentional Small Group and Partner Work — structured collaboration where groupings are chosen for linguistic, cognitive and interpersonal reasons, not convenience.
8. Use of First Language — strategic, not full translation; first language as a bridge for key vocabulary and conceptual clarity; identity and comprehension both supported.


“Good instruction for second language learners is great instruction for all learners.”


References

Scaffolding
BICS and CALP
Cooperative Learning
Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis
Ausubel
Bloom’s Taxonomy
Content and Language Integrated Learning
Learner Autonomy


Resource note. Part of UDL, AI & Museum Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/06-05-2026 - Wednesday/Digital Competences and Artificial Intelligence.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Digital Competences and Artificial Intelligence
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Notes on Digital Competences and Artificial Intelligence

“If we teach today as we taught yesterday, we rob our children of tomorrow.”
John Dewey

The session opened on a slide that carried this quote under the heading From Digital Competences to AI Literacies. Dewey was not there for decoration. He was an argument: that the question of technology in education is not a technical question. It is a pedagogical one, and it has been the same question for longer than anyone in the room had been teaching.

The tutor Neda (BG), English Matter’s coordinator Eduardo (ES), and Sílvia (IT) during the Digital Competences session | Day 4 © Team

The Central Question
“As educators, should we just limit ourselves to adapting our tools to respond to technology — or can we begin to shape the technology itself?”
This was the session’s sharpest moment. It arrived early and stayed. Adapting to tools is the path of least resistance and the path most organisations take. Shaping tools requires a different kind of agency, a different relationship to the technology, and a different understanding of what the educator’s role actually is. The session did not resolve this. It planted it.
The door and key metaphor organised the rest: which door are you trying to open? Which key do you need? Which lock is stopping you? The metaphor worked because it directed attention toward the barrier before the tool. The question is not “which tool?” The question is “what am I trying to do, for whom, and what is currently making that impossible?”

DigCompEdu and SELFIE for Teachers
The European framework for educators’ digital competence.
DigCompEdu defines six areas of educator digital competence: Professional Engagement, Digital Resources, Teaching and Learning, Assessment, Empowering Learners, and Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence.
What the session emphasised: DigCompEdu does not assess whether an educator uses tools. It assesses whether the use has pedagogical intent, impact, and equity of access. The distinction between using technology and using technology purposefully runs through every area of the framework.
SELFIE for Teachers (Self-reflection on Effective Learning by Fostering the use of Innovative Educational Technologies) is the European Commission’s self-assessment instrument built on DigCompEdu. Not a test. A mirror.

The SAMR Model
A framework for thinking about levels of technology integration.

Substitution — technology as a direct replacement for an existing tool, with no functional change.
Augmentation — technology as replacement with some functional improvement.
Modification — technology allows significant task redesign.
Redefinition — technology creates new tasks previously inconceivable.

“We should think of the SAMR model as a spectrum.” On one end, a PDF replaces a printed page. On the other end, a student collaborates in real time with a school in another country.
The session was careful about this: “Tools can be used on different levels. It depends on how you use them.” A Padlet is not inherently at any level. The same tool becomes substitution or redefinition depending entirely on the pedagogical intention behind it. The tool is not the pedagogy.

AI Literacy
The session’s final turn, and its most open-ended.
“AI literacy represents the technical knowledge, durable skills, and future-ready attitudes required to thrive in a world influenced by AI. It enables learners to engage, create with, manage, and design AI, while critically evaluating its benefits, risks, and ethical implications.”
Three things the session insisted on: AI skills differ from ICT and digital skills. They require specialised frameworks. And they are not primarily about tools.

The tutor Neda (BG), English Matter’s coordinator Eduardo (ES), and Sílvia (IT) during the Digital Competences session | Day 4 © Team
The challenges flagged: fairness, transparency, privacy, accountability, and the question of human agency in a world where systems can mimic human behaviour. The UNESCO framework and the OECD draft both treat AI literacy as requiring critical thinking, ethical awareness, and the ability to evaluate rather than simply operate.
PISA 2029 will include media and AI literacy in its assessment. The implication for educators: these are not optional competences for the enthusiasts. They are forming part of the official literacy baseline.

Station Rotation
Eight tools explored in pairs, ten minutes each.
Mentimeter — real-time polling, word clouds, formative feedback.
NotebookLM — AI-assisted research and synthesis from uploaded sources.
Curipod — AI-generated interactive lesson slides.
Padlet Arcade — gamified collaboration. The theme used: Guernica. The Picasso thread arrived through the back door of a pedagogical tool exercise.
Webwise — Irish internet safety centre. Digital citizenship, media literacy, protection resources.
Genially — interactive visual content (already encountered in the walking tour). The session closed with a Genially Escape Room in groups of four.

Mónica (PT), Claudia (IT), Julia (DE) and Filipe (PT) exploring digital tools | Day 4 © Team
The Station Rotation was the same methodology used in the 21st Century Skills session on Monday. By Wednesday, participants were not just experiencing it. They were noticing they had experienced it before.

References

DigCompEdu
SELFIE for Teachers
The SAMR Model
AI Literacy
UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies
OECD AI Literacy Framework
DigComp
Station Rotation
Guernica
Genially
Padlet
21st Century Skills
Dewey


Part of UDL, AI & Museum Learning, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/06-05-2026 - Wednesday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 4 - Wednesday, 6 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Universal Design for Learning, Digital Competences & Museum Learning
Overview
Wednesday was the day the course made its deepest argument.
The morning brought Universal Design for Learning followed by Digital Competences and Artificial Intelligence. Two sessions that, on paper, could read as separate topics. In practice they were the same claim stated twice: that education without attention to access is not education at all, and that the tools we adopt carry ethical choices we are responsible for making consciously.
The afternoon brought the course’s most deliberate act of Context4Content: a guided visit to the Museo del Prado. The meeting point was the statue of Goya at the main entrance. Inside, fourteen works — Fra Angelico, El Bosco, Van der Weyden, El Greco, Tintoretto, Rubens, Velázquez, Goya — formed a curated argument about representation, perspective, power and the act of looking itself.
By the end of the day, the logic of the whole week had become visible. The sessions, the city, the art: all of it had been pointing at the same thing. Learning as the careful act of reading the world.

Programme
index#06052026---wednesday

Notes on UDL, AI & Museum Learning
Wednesday was the day the course stopped describing pedagogy and became it.
The UDL session did not explain what it feels like to encounter content without scaffolding. It made us feel it. The AI session did not lecture about critical digital literacy. It asked us whether we wanted to be shaped by technology or to shape it. The Prado did not summarise Spanish art. It put us in a room with it and asked us to look carefully.
The statue of Goya at the entrance. The guide’s voice in the Goya rooms. The painting that was already on a screen that morning. The painting that had appeared in a quiz on the third day of May, in the third week of this century, in the city where it was made.
There is a particular quality of experience when the week’s threads converge in one afternoon. That is what the Prado offered on Wednesday. Not a visit. A confirmation.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/07-05-2026 - Thursday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 5 - Thursday, 7 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
School-Based Case Study: IES Príncipe Felipe
Overview
Thursday moved the course off the page and into a building.
The morning was a guided visit to IES Príncipe Felipe, a vocational and secondary school in Fuencarral, north Madrid. What greeted the group was not an illustration of the week’s frameworks. It was the frameworks made architectural: UDL in the layout of the labs, CLIL in the negotiated multilingual encounter with the director, cooperative learning in the way students move through shared spaces, active methodologies in every room that was designed to make doing the dominant mode of learning.
Somewhere in the morning corridors, a conversation with the course coordinator confirmed what the week had been suggesting. The art, the Prado, the Guernica on a Padlet, the Goya puzzle before the Prado visit, Las Meninas on the last day of the quiz: none of it had been accidental. The programme was built on Context4Content at two registers — culture with a capital C and culture with a lowercase one. The galleries and the bocadillos. The paintings and the stickers. Both intentional.

After the visit, the team took a group picture in front of the paper plane statue, IES Príncipe Felipe | Day 5 © Team
The afternoon brought the week’s most collaborative act of synthesis. Two teams — two Portuguese, two German and four Italian participants,  — had spent the week alongside each other, divided by assignment but not by experience. At lunch they decided that presenting two separate portfolios made no sense. By the time they sat down to work, the question was no longer “what do we present?” but “what did we actually learn, together, and how do we show it?”

During the afternoon, the team gathered to work on their presentation. Julia (DE) was hungry | Day 5 © Team
The answer was a mind map. And when the question arose of what to use as the visual connectors, the artworks offered themselves. Las Meninas, The Third of May 1808, The Three Graces: paintings that had been accumulating meaning since Sunday evening, now carrying the weight of a shared week.

See IES Príncipe Felipe for the full school visit notes and Portfolio Development for the afternoon’s work.
The evening was El Matador. Cañas e tapas near Sol. The lowercase culture that the week had always included.

Programme
index#07052026

Notes on School-Based Case Study
Thursday was the day the course verified itself against a real school.
Every framework the week had introduced — UDL, CLIL, cooperative learning, SAMR, active methodologies — was either visible in the IES or visibly absent in ways that clarified what its presence would mean. The labs were not evidence of good practice. They were a prompt: what would it look like if your institution took seriously the idea that space teaches?
The conversation with the coordinator confirmed something the week had been building toward without stating directly. The art was not decoration. It was design. The choice to use Goya, Hieronymus Bosch, Diego Velázquez and Picasso as reference points across the week — in quizzes, in session activities, in the Prado visit, in the Padlet Arcade — reflected a deliberate reading of Context4Content: that culture operates at both registers, the canonical and the everyday, and that learning happens in the space between them.
The cañas at El Matador near Sol were the lowercase version of the same argument. Not an epilogue. Part of the text.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content
