Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/08-05-2026 - Friday/Place and Sensory Learning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Place and Sensory Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Day note synthesis
The last session before Toledo prepared the group for Toledo.
Place and Sensory Learning opened with a Think-Pair-Share on sensory memory: “Do you have a particular smell or sound that triggers a memory for you?” Before any framework, the argument was already located in the room. Memory is not primarily textual. Learning leaves traces in the body. This is not a poetic observation. It is what the cognitive science says.
The session introduced multisensory learning as the practical extension of Universal Design for Learning: different sensory channels are not aesthetic alternatives, they are different encoding pathways. Learning that engages more of them encodes more durably.
Then Toledo arrived in the room before anyone had left the building. A timeline. A word cloud. A predictive learning activity built from an image of nun figurines in a shop window — an image that would be recognised again, collectively, when the group passed the actual shop in the actual city hours later. A tableau exercise in which the text about Toledo’s history was turned into frozen scenes, bodies solving what the mind had processed.
And marzipan. Tasted in the room. Toledo’s confection, carried into the session as sensory anchor. By the time the group walked the city, the taste was already there.
The session closed with mindfulness: the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding exercise, beach ball breathing, box breathing. An invitation to carry conscious attention into the afternoon. The city would do the rest.

Place and Sensory Learning
Notes on the Morning Session — Friday, 8 May 2026
The last formal session of the week opened on the body before it opened on the city.
The theme was “The role of cognitive science in multisensory learning” — and the first move, characteristically, was not a lecture. It was a question.

Think–Pair–Share: Sensory Memory
“Do you have a particular smell or sound that triggers a memory for you?”
The prompt was an entry point to multisensory learning that arrived through personal experience rather than definition. Before any framework was introduced, the group had already located the argument in themselves: that memory is not primarily textual, that learning leaves traces in the body, that a particular smell or a particular sound can retrieve something the mind has apparently filed elsewhere.
The Think-Pair-Share structure held it:

Think: individual, silent.
Pair: in conversation, testing the experience against someone else’s.
Share: to the group, where the personal became the evidence.

The same cooperative technique had appeared on Tuesday, and again on Wednesday at the Prado. By Friday, participants were not just using it. They were noticing that they were using it again.

Multisensory Learning — The Framework
“Why multisensory approaches overlap with UDL.”
The connection is structural, not incidental. UDL’s Multiple Means of Representation argues that information presented in one sensory channel excludes learners for whom that channel is a barrier. Multisensory learning approaches the same reality from the cognitive side: the brain encodes more durably through multiple simultaneous channels. Hearing, sight, touch, smell, movement — each adds a strand to the memory trace. Learning designed for multiple senses is not more engaging because it is more entertaining. It is more durable because it is more encoded.
The cognitive science underpinning: sensory memory, emotional engagement, associative learning, autobiographical memory. The modalities that academic education most consistently underuses — smell, touch, kinesthetic, taste — turn out to be among the most emotionally weighted. The classroom that ignores them is not neutral. It is making a choice about which kinds of knowing count.

Introduction to Toledo — Toledo — The City of Three Cultures
Before the bus, the city was introduced inside the room.
The Historical Frame
Toledo’s timeline ran from the Roman era through the Visigothic Kingdom, the Moorish conquest, the Christian Reconquista, a Golden Age, and a subsequent decline. The point of the timeline was not chronology. It was convergence: for a period, three traditions — Jewish, Moorish and Christian — occupied the same city simultaneously. The architecture of Toledo is the architecture of that coexistence: a synagogue built in Mudéjar style, a mosque converted to a cathedral, a cathedral built above a mosque.
The Three Cultures framing matters because it refuses the narrative of collision as the default state of cultural difference. Toledo represents a different possibility — not without conflict, but with a record of shared space, shared craft, shared intellectual life.
Predictive Learning Activity
The session used an image as a predictive prompt: a close-up of figurines of nuns in a shop window. “Can you predict what the story will be about?”
The technique is classic pre-reading scaffolding: activate inference before exposure to content, use a visual cue to build anticipation, make the learner a hypothesis-generator rather than a passive receiver. The image of the nuns also had a later resonance — the shop appeared in Toledo itself, recognised collectively as the group passed it. The session had pre-loaded a moment of shared recognition.
Wordbuzz / Word Cloud
A word cloud activity followed, tied to the Toledo content. Reading the cloud as a set of associations rather than a sequential text: a different cognitive mode, lateral rather than linear, pattern-recognition rather than comprehension.
Tableau — Frozen Scene
Pairs read a text about Toledo’s history. Each pair defined a gesture for each paragraph. Then: two groups of four, three frozen moments to represent, half the script per group.
The tableau technique — also called “frozen image” or “living picture” — makes comprehension physical. The body has to solve what the mind has processed. Finding the gesture for a paragraph requires understanding it at a level that paraphrase alone does not demand. The collaborative element adds negotiation: the group must agree on what the frozen moment represents, which requires shared interpretation.
The result is learning that has passed through movement, through collective decision, through representation. It leaves a different residue than reading.

Marzipan Time — Context4Content, Multisensory and Mindful
Before leaving for Toledo, marzipan.
The Context4Content characteristics named in the session: immersive, outside the comfort zone, fun, engaging. Marzipan is Toledo’s traditional confection. The activity brought Toledo into the room through taste before the group had entered the city.
This is a precise methodological move. Multisensory learning argues that memory encodes through multiple channels; tasting Toledo’s marzipan before arriving in Toledo creates a sensory anchor that will activate when the same taste appears in a shop, a market stall, a gift. The city becomes associated with a physical memory laid down in advance.

Mindfulness — A Mindful Moment
The session closed with a grounding exercise before the journey.
Three techniques introduced:
5-4-3-2-1 (Grounding) — a sensory inventory: five things you can see, four you can feel, three you can hear, two you can smell, one you can taste. The sequence works by pulling attention into the immediate environment, interrupting mental loops by requiring specific sensory observation. The technique also happens to be a structured exercise in exactly the multisensory attention the session had been arguing for.
Beach Ball Breathing — a visualisation-guided breathing technique associating breath with the mental image of inflating and releasing a beach ball. Gentle, accessible, non-clinical.
Box Breathing — structured breathing in four equal phases: inhale, hold, exhale, hold. Used in stress regulation and focus. The structure is deliberate: it occupies the counting mind while the body regulates.
The invitation carried into Toledo: find time, space, and conscious attention during the afternoon. The city is the classroom. The practice continues.

The Methodology, Briefly
The morning session used, in sequence: Think-Pair-Share, predictive learning, word cloud, tableau, multisensory activation (marzipan), mindfulness grounding. Every technique served the same argument: that learning involves the body, that attention is a teachable skill, and that the most memorable experiences are the ones that engaged the most channels simultaneously.
Toledo was not the destination the session was building toward. It was the continuation of the session by other means.

References

Multisensory Learning
Universal Design for Learning
Place-based Learning
Context4Content
Cooperative Learning
Think-Pair-Share
Tableau
Mindfulness
Scaffolding
Toledo — The City of Three Cultures
Heritage as Curriculum
Embodied Learning


Part of Place-Based Learning & Cultural Heritage, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/08-05-2026 - Friday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 6 - Friday, 8 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Place-Based Learning & Cultural Heritage
Overview
The morning delivered the week’s final formal session before Toledo: Place and Sensory Learning, built around the cognitive science of multisensory learning and the pedagogy of place. A session about the body, about sensory memory, about what learning leaves behind when it passes through multiple channels at once. Methodologically, the fullest expression of Context4Content yet — marzipan as pedagogical material, Toledo tasted before it was walked.
In the afternoon, Toledo — The City of Three Cultures.

Toledo landscape | Day 6 © André Almeida
The city where Jewish, Moorish and Christian traditions shared the same streets, the same craftspeople, the same century. Where a synagogue is built in Mudéjar style and a mosque is built inside a cathedral. Where the architecture is the argument.


Julia (DE), Carsten (DE), Filipe (PT), Mara (IT), Claudia (IT) and Maria Grazia (IT) cheering during the last team’s dinner, this time at the typical Taberna Mariano | Day 6 © Team

Programme
index#08052026---friday

Notes on Place-Based Learning & Cultural Heritage
The week began on 3 May 2026 in Madrid, with Goya’s 3 de Mayo on a quiz screen. It ended in Toledo, where the same three traditions that Goya watched destroy each other in 1808 had spent centuries building a shared city. The architecture survived the communities. The marzipan survived the expulsions. El Greco arrived from elsewhere and made the image that defines the place.
History does not repeat itself. It rhymes. The course was designed so that the rhyme would become audible by Friday afternoon.
Context4Content was the framework. Toledo was the content. The week had always been pointing here.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/09-05-2026 - Saturday/European Dimension and Dissemination.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: European Dimension, Presentation and Dissemination
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
European Dimension, Presentation and Dissemination
Day note synthesis
The room was the same. The educators in it were not.
The session returned to the week’s opening question — what is the European Dimension, what does it ask of educators, what are we responsible for doing with what we have learned — but arrived at it now with the weight of what had come between. The Prado. Toledo. The IES. The presentations. The conversations over dinner, over cañas, over the course of a week in a city that had been making arguments all along.
The session opened on a quotation from John F. Kennedy: “The goal of education is the advancement of knowledge and the dissemination of truth.” Then the question that followed: “What is the truth in 2026? As teachers? As educators? As citizens?”
The week had been building toward exactly the conditions under which that question has urgency: generative AI, disinformation, algorithmic saturation, the collapse of shared evidential ground. The answer the session offered was not technical. It was vocational. The educator who teaches critical reading, who models the refusal of the convenient version, who plants the capacity for healthy scepticism in a student — that educator is doing something structurally necessary, not supplementary.
Three figures arrived at the end, each carrying a dimension of the week’s argument:
Cervantes: “La pluma es la lengua del alma.” Writing as the externalisation of interior life. The teacher who writes makes their practice visible. The student who writes makes their thinking visible.
Picasso: “Every child is born an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.” The same claim the week had been practising: unlearning the rigidity that formal education installs, recovering the openness that learning requires.
Salvador de Madariaga: “This Europe must be born. And it will be born when the Spaniards say ‘our Chartres’, when the English say ‘our Krakow’, when Italians say ‘our Copenhagen’.” The Europe that the course argued for is cultural, not administrative. It exists when patrimony stops being national and becomes shared. When a Portuguese educator looks at Las Meninas and carries it home.
“Fiat Europa.” Let there be Europe. Not given. Made. By educators willing to say “ours” about things built elsewhere.
See European Dimension and Dissemination for the full session notes.

European Dimension and Dissemination
Notes on the Closing Session — Saturday, 9 May 2026
The last session returned to the themes that opened the week.
European Dimension and Dissemination had been announced as the programme’s closing arc. Arriving there after six days was different from arriving cold on Sunday: the frameworks were familiar now, tested against a real school, a real city, real artworks. The question the session asked — “what is truth in 2026?” — was not abstract. It was the week’s final exam, returned to the room as a question rather than an answer.

The Central Question
The session opened on a slide carrying a quotation:

“The goal of education is the advancement of knowledge and the dissemination of truth.”
— John F. Kennedy

Then the question that turned the whole room:
“What is the truth in 2026? As teachers? As educators? As citizens?”

Eduardo (ES), the English Matters’ coordinator, during the final European Dimension session | Day 7 © André Almeida
The question was not rhetorical. The session had spent the week building toward exactly the conditions under which that question becomes urgent: generative AI, disinformation, algorithmic bubbles, post-truth, media saturation, the collapse of shared evidential ground. Media literacy, AI literacy, critical thinking, healthy scepticism — all of it had been building toward this. The dissemination of truth is not a bureaucratic task. It is a responsibility that grows harder precisely as the tools for its corruption multiply.

Dissemination — The Distinction That Matters
The session drew a clear line between two things that are often conflated:
Dissemination — making work available to the professional and scientific community. The circulation of practice, evidence, methodology.
Communication — making work visible to society. Public engagement, media, the broader democratic conversation about education.
Most teachers, the coordinator suggested, do neither. They work inside closed classrooms, produce extraordinary things, and allow that work to remain invisible. The institutional logics of education — timetables, assessment pressures, bureaucratic cultures — conspire to keep teaching private.
The session pushed against this directly. “Teachers make grandiose things but we’re not aware.” The sentence was noted in the room. It could be read as observation or as diagnosis.

Disseminare — Scattering Seeds
The Latin root of the word made an appearance: “disseminare” — to scatter seeds.
The image reframes what dissemination is. Not publication in the institutional sense, not the delivery of outputs to a funding body, not a checkbox in a project management system. Scattering seeds. The act does not control where they land, or whether they germinate, or what grows. It requires the willingness to release something into circulation without guarantee of return.
This connects to the course’s implicit model of how learning spreads. An educator in Portugal or Germany or Italy, returned from a week in Madrid, carrying a set of frameworks and experiences and artworks and conversations: seeds scattered into whatever contexts they inhabit. The rhizomatic metaphor was apt — Erasmus+ as a network of small practices connecting, mutating, surprising the people who carry them forward.


Eduardo (ES) during the Dissemination session | Day 7 © Team

”Write! You are the Intellectual!”
One of the session’s strongest moments: the direct address to teachers as knowledge producers.
Many educators do not recognise themselves as intellectuals. The dominant self-image is executor: curriculum delivered, assessment administered, results logged. The session challenged this directly. A teacher who has developed a practice, tested it, reflected on it, adapted it — that teacher has produced knowledge. The question is whether they consider themselves authorised to make it public.
“Write! You are the intellectual!” is a claim about professional identity, not just about communication strategy. It says: what you know is worth saying. What you have built has value beyond your classroom. The professional isolation that characterises teaching in most European systems is not inevitable. It is a choice — and it can be made differently.
The adjacent observation: “Do you have five minutes? I have this idea.” Micro-dissemination. The corridor conversation. The staffroom suggestion. Change in educational culture does not require conferences and publications. It requires people willing to say, informally and persistently, what they have learned.

Truth, Scepticism, and the Teacher as Barrier
One of the session’s most serious claims came from Gordon Pope:

“One of the most useful attributes we can develop in young people is the ability to think critically and maintain a healthy scepticism.”

The session connected this to the full arc of the week: every session had been practising interpretation, inference, and the refusal of easy reading. The Goya puzzle before the Prado. Las Meninas and the question of who is watching. The AI literacy session’s central challenge — “should we adapt to technology or shape it?” Every one of these was an exercise in healthy scepticism: attending carefully, deferring judgement, refusing the first available interpretation.
And then the note that stayed:
“Sometimes teachers are the only barrier between students and disaster.”
In 2026, with AI-generated content indistinguishable from documentary evidence, with algorithmic systems designed to capture and hold attention regardless of informational quality, with political systems under pressure from narratives that exploit the absence of shared factual ground — the educator who teaches critical reading, who insists on evidence, who models the refusal to accept the convenient version: that educator is not performing a supplementary role. They are doing something structurally necessary.

The Quotes That Framed the Closing
Three figures arrived at the end of the week, each adding a dimension:
Cervantes — “La pluma es la lengua del alma.” The quill is the tongue of the soul. Writing not as output but as the externalisation of interior life. The teacher who writes makes their practice visible; the student who writes makes their thinking visible. Cervantes appears in this context not as literary heritage but as argument: expression matters, and the forms it takes carry the soul of the person making it.
The session connected Cervantes to Don Quixote’s modernity: characters that evolve, that are not flat. True learning implies transformation, ambiguity, interior complexity. Not the delivery of information to a static receiver.
Picasso — “Every child is born an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.” The connection to unlearning is direct. The week had argued, in different registers across seven days, that what stands between learners and genuine learning is often what they have already been taught: about what counts as a right answer, about what the classroom is for, about who holds knowledge and who receives it. To remain an artist is to hold onto the openness that formal education systematically compresses.
Salvador de Madariaga — “This Europe must be born. And it will be born when the Spaniards say ‘our Chartres’, when the English say ‘our Krakow’, when Italians say ‘our Copenhagen’.”
This was the philosophical key to the entire week. The Europe that the course argued for — through the Erasmus Spirit on Sunday, through the Walking Tour on Monday, through Toledo on Friday — is not an administrative entity. It is a cultural one. It exists when patrimony stops being national and becomes shared. When a Portuguese educator looks at Las Meninas and says “our painting.” When a German participant stands in front of Goya’s Third of May and carries it home.
“Fiat Europa.” The formulation was almost liturgical. Let there be Europe. Not given. Willed. Made by educators who teach students to say “ours” about things that were made elsewhere.

”From Teachers to Teachers”
From teachers to teachers, the session’s summary of the course’s philosophy: peer learning, horizontal exchange, the refusal of the expert-to-novice transmission model. The course was not a training event delivering competences to passive recipients. It was a community of practice, temporary and European, that treated every participant as both teacher and learner.
The closing observation: what the week had been was not primarily methodological. It was cultural, ethical, civic, epistemological, and European. The frameworks — UDL, CLIL, cooperative learning, SAMR, AI literacy, place-based learning — were tools. The project was larger: educators who interpret the world carefully, disseminate what they find, and build with their students the capacity to do the same.

What to Disseminate
The session offered a practical grid:
What: “Our experience of Europe and Europeans… and the world.” Not an activity report. A lived experience of difference and connection.
To whom: Students, staff, school leadership, parents’ associations, educational authorities, professional journals, social media, local radio. The list was deliberately wide: the argument for visibility does not stop at the school gate.
How: Lessons, eTwinning, online communities, conferences, seminars, e-newsletters, reports, articles, handbooks. And the aspiration: “Making a handbook of good education in Europe.” One page describing how you teach in Europe. Documenting practice as the production of professional culture.
The nine plus one countries represented in the week: Bulgaria, Croatia, Portugal, France, Czech Republic, Germany, Italy, Slovakia, Greece. The seeds, already scattered.

References


European Dimension in Education


Erasmus+ Spirit


Critical Thinking


Media Literacy


AI Literacy


Visual Learning


Context4Content


Cervantes


Picasso


Goya


Las Meninas


Community of Practice


Place-based Learning



Part of Dissemination & Reflection, under Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/09-05-2026 - Saturday/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Day 7 - Saturday, 9 May 2026
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Dissemination & Reflection
Overview
The course ended in the morning. The day continued.
Saturday’s formal programme brought the week to its close with European Dimension and Dissemination — the same themes that had opened it on Sunday, now heard from the other side of seven days. The Portfolio Development followed: the eight-person collaborative presentation brainstormed on Tuesday built on Thursday afternoon, refined through Friday, finished in the early hours of Saturday morning. “I love a metaphor,” Megan replied.
Evaluations, certificates, the formal dispersal of a temporary community of practice. 9+1 countries. Educators who had spent a week learning to see, together, what they had been doing separately for years.

Innovative and Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning team holding their diplomas | Day 7 © Teresa Vieira Silva
The afternoon was personal and unscheduled. An audioguide rented at the Reina Sofia. Four to five hours across all the floors: Avant-Garde Territories, Guernica, the African Guernica, Contemporary Art post-1975. The course ended at midday. The learning continued until the building closed.

And, at the end, more calamares y cañas before departing | Day 7 © Filipe Gama

See European Dimension and Dissemination for the closing session notes and Perspective & Reflection for the presentation and reflection.

Programme
index#european-dimension-dissemination-and-portfolio

Notes on Dissemination & Reflection
The week began with a question about Europe and ended with the same question, heard differently.
On Sunday, European Dimension was a framework. By Saturday, it was a lived memory: shared meals, shared sessions, shared artworks, shared cities. The Madariaga quotation — “our Chartres, our Krakow” — had been the invisible logic of the whole week. The Prado’s paintings became shared property by Wednesday. Toledo’s architecture became shared by Friday. Guernica had been shared since the Padlet Arcade on Wednesday morning.
The Reina Sofia on Saturday afternoon was the week continued by other means. History does not repeat itself. But it rhymes. And when you have spent a week learning to listen for the rhyme, you hear it everywhere.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes Context4Content

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Programme/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Programme
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
40 hours across 7 days.  One through-line: learn by doing.
The week didn’t follow a traditional course structure. Each day built on the previous one, layering frameworks, places and reflection into something cumulative. You can follow it chronologically, or jump to what matters most to you.
03/05/2026 - Sunday
European Dimension & Foundations

El Rastro: the first learning of the week, before learning was scheduled
European Dimension in Education: principles, structure, framework
Introduction to Field Learning Projects: Context4Content
Images of Spain: Spain as learning context
Tools for Reflection and Professional Development: portfolios, reflective practice, professional identity
Building a European Dimension: quiz and icebreaker — the group becomes a group

04/05/2026 - Monday
Educator Identity and City as Curriculum

Being an Educator in 2026 and beyond: who teachers are, not just what they teach
21st Century Skills: the 4Cs — Critical Thinking, Creativity, Communication, Collaboration
Field Learning Projects: Images of Madrid: the city as curriculum
Sala Equis: unplanned — Metropolis on the wall, the mediator between head and hands

05/05/2026 - Tuesday
Cooperative & Language Integrated Learning

Cooperative Learning: Learning as social and affective
CLIL: the plumber metaphor, scaffolding, unlearning
Portfolio Development — First Draft: fragments, keywords, questions without answers
Casa de Campo: deceleration — the programme continued without an agenda

06/05/2026 - Wednesday
UDL, AI & Museum Learning

Universal Design for Learning: designing for variability, not accommodation
Digital Competences and Artificial Intelligence: DigCompEdu, SAMR, AI Literacy — who shapes the tools?
Field Learning Projects: Highlights of Spanish Art — fourteen works, one curated argument about looking

07/05/2026 - Thursday
School-Based Case Study

IES Príncipe Felipe: the frameworks made architectural — UDL, CLIL and Cooperative Learning in a real school
Portfolio Development — Mind Map: the two teams merged, the artworks offered themselves
El Matador: Context4Content, Lowercase

08/05/2026 - Friday
Place-Based Learning & Cultural Heritage

Place and Sensory Learning: marzipan before Toledo — the body as a learning instrument
Field Learning Project: Toledo — The City of Three Cultures: coexistence, architecture and what remains

09/05/2026 - Saturday
European Dimension, Dissemination and Portfolio

European Dimension and Dissemination: Fiat Europa — making Europe as daily practice
Portfolio Development — Perspective & Reflection: Las Meninas, Goya, vanishing points and perspective lines — “I love a metaphor”
Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia: Guernica, the African Guernica, four hours alone

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Cultural Understanding.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Cultural Understanding
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Cultural understanding is not a destination. It is a practice of sustained attention.
Madrid offered this in layers: the architecture of daily life, the pace of the streets, the rhythm of meals, the weight of history in museums. Spain is not simply a country with a different language. It is a country with a different relationship to time, to public space, to the past.
The Spanish language itself was part of the learning environment — not as a subject, but as an ambient presence. Navigating even partially in another language produces a specific kind of humility. You stop performing fluency. You start listening more carefully.
Cultural differences between Spain and Portugal are smaller than expected and larger than they appear. Proximity does not guarantee familiarity. The differences that matter most are the ones that surface in daily behaviour: how people occupy a terrace, when they eat, what silence means in conversation.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Curation.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Curation
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The intentional organisation of places, works, activities, emotions, encounters, and contexts to produce meaningful learning.

What Curation Is
A museum curator does not simply collect objects. They decide which objects to place in proximity, in what order, with what lighting, what wall text, what silences between them. The meaning emerges from the arrangement, not from any single piece.
Teaching works the same way.
The teacher who curates a learning experience is not deciding what to tell learners. They are deciding how to arrange the conditions under which learners will encounter something worth understanding.

The Hidden Methodology
The week’s deepest methodology was curation. The explicit frameworks — Active Methodologies, Cooperative Learning, CLIL, Universal Design for Learning — were the tools. Curation was the sensibility that held them together.
Which activities in which order. Which museum first. Which conversation before which artwork. Which silence after which film. These are curatorial decisions, and they shape whether the experience coheres or merely accumulates.

The Teacher as Curator
Curation as a pedagogical stance implies several commitments:
The learning environment is designed with intention, not assembled by default. Learner experience — not content coverage — is the primary design criterion. The sequence and rhythm of activities matter as much as the activities themselves. The gaps between things are as important as the things.

Connections in the Course
See Active Methodologies, Place-based Learning, Context4Content, Highlights of Spanish Art, and the Fiat Humanitas essay, which was itself an act of pedagogical curation: six works, a particular order, a specific argument emerging from the arrangement.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Fiat Europa.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Fiat Europa
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Fiat-se Europa. Faça-se Europa.
Let Europe be made. Not declared — made. Not as a bureaucratic structure, but as a daily practice of shared values, civic commitment, and deliberate encounter.

The Proposition
The training’s deepest objective was not methodological. It was ethical and civic.
The 9+1 countries in the room — Bulgaria, Croatia, Portugal, France, Czech Republic, Germany, Italy, Slovakia, Greece, Spain — were not simply a list of origins. They were Europe as a relational experience: people from different educational cultures, different languages, different histories, choosing to learn together.
That choice — repeated daily, in shared tasks, meals, museum visits, and late conversations — is what Europe is made of. Not treaties. Not institutions. This.

The Connection to Fiat Humanitas
Fiat Europa was the course’s implicit manifesto. Fiat Humanitas extends the argument: the commitment does not stop at the European border. It extends to the grammar of violence across all geographies, to the voices excluded from any canon, to the responsibility of teachers everywhere to make the world more navigable for those who arrive with less.

Connections in the Course
See Fiat Humanitas, , European Dimension in Education, and Dissemination Strategy.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/From teachers to teachers.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: From teachers to teachers
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
From teachers to teachers implies horizontality. It implies that the person in the room with you has something to offer that no curriculum document captures. It implies that Professional Development is not something done to teachers but something built between them. It positions English Matters less as a training provider and more as a mediator of encounters.

Part of Footscapes. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Healthy scepticism.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Healthy Scepticism
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
“The first step in the acquisition of wisdom is silence, the second listening, the third memory, the fourth practice, the fifth teaching others.” — Solomon Ibn Gabirol

Healthy scepticism is not cynicism. It is the disciplined habit of asking for evidence before accepting claims — including claims that feel comfortable, that confirm existing beliefs, or that arrive from apparently authoritative sources.

What It Means in Practice
Healthy scepticism means pausing before sharing. It means asking: where does this come from? Who benefits from it being believed? What would I need to see to change my mind?
It means being willing to say “I don’t know yet” — and treating that as an intellectually honest position rather than a failure.

Why It Matters in 2026
The information environment has changed faster than the habits required to navigate it. Generative AI, algorithmic curation, and viral circulation have made plausible misinformation easier to produce and harder to identify than at any previous point in history.
Healthy scepticism is not a personality trait. It is a teachable, learnable skill — and one of the most important things teachers can model and develop. See What is truth in 2026?.

Connections in the Course
The habit connects to AI Literacy, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, The Learning Pyramid (which is itself a case study in widely-cited, weakly-evidenced claims), Las Meninas (who controls the frame?), Goya, and What is truth in 2026?.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Learning Landscapes.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Learning Landscapes
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A landscape is not scenery. It is what remains after the learning has moved on.
This garden is called Footscapes because the metaphor that kept recurring across the week was never the classroom, it was the footprint: the trace a place leaves on a learner, and the trace a learner leaves on a place by paying it real attention. Context4Content gave this a name — the context does not illustrate the content, the context is the content — but the feeling came first, in Madrid, in the gap between a scheduled visit and an unscheduled one.
A learning landscape is what forms when a course stops being a sequence of sessions and becomes a terrain: overlapping, revisited, walked more than once. The same painting seen twice does not repeat, it rhymes, because the person looking at it has changed in between. The same square holds a peddypaper question and, an hour later, a completely different conversation. Nothing about the landscape moved. Everything about how it was read did.
To design for learning landscapes is to accept that the most durable lessons are rarely the ones delivered on schedule. They are the ones a place kept in reserve, waiting for a learner ready to notice them — and to trust that noticing, done properly, is itself a competence worth teaching.

Part of Reflections. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Art as a Technology for Interpretation.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Art as a Technology for Interpretation
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Art is not the subject of a museum visit. It is the instrument.
At the Prado, the mediator did not walk the group past paintings, she built an argument out of fourteen of them, curated in sequence like a text with chapters. At the Reina Sofía, a single room reframed Guernica through African Guernica and through History Doesn’t Repeat Itself, but It Does Rhyme, and the meaning of the first painting changed retroactively because of what came after it. Neither museum was teaching art history. Both were teaching how to read.
The audioguide made this explicit as a technique: interpretation offered in layers, available on demand, never replacing the direct encounter with the work but scaffolding the vocabulary needed to hold onto it. That is the technology part. A painting does not explain itself. It requires a method, and the museum’s job is to hand visitors that method without pretending it handed them the truth.
Fiat Humanitas is where this idea was pushed furthest: six works, four artists, one thesis, an argument built entirely out of images because some conclusions cannot be reached any other way. Art, treated as a technology, stops being content to admire and becomes a lens to look through — at history, at power, at what a frame includes and excludes. The frame is never neutral. Neither is the person choosing it.

Part of Reflections. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on European Values.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on European Values
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The European dimension was never listed on the syllabus. It was the room itself.
Nine countries, one room, teachers speaking to teachers with no hierarchy between them. The European dimension in education is often written down as a requirement to satisfy, a box a project must tick to qualify for funding. It felt like nothing of the sort in Madrid. It felt like the reason the funding exists in the first place.
Jean Monnet’s line keeps returning in this garden because it will not stop being true: we do not unite states, we unite people. Nobody in that room arrived carrying a flag. Everybody arrived carrying a suitcase of cultural baggage, methods, assumptions, classroom habits shaped by a different education system, and the week’s real curriculum was watching those differences meet without needing to resolve into sameness. Fiat Europa is where that feeling was given its full weight — Europe made not by treaty but by ruin, and remade daily, in ordinary classrooms, by ordinary practice.
That is the version of European values worth teaching forward: not a slogan, not a set of institutions, but a room where difference is the working material, and unity is something built, one shared table at a time, not something inherited by right.

Part of Reflections. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Language Development.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Language Development
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Spending a week as a learner in a foreign-language environment produced an unexpected shift: the experience of being a non-fluent speaker became a professional reference point.
When you cannot fully express yourself in the language of the room, you develop compensatory skills: you listen more attentively, you read body language more carefully, you ask for help more honestly. These are exactly the skills we want our students to develop — and we rarely think about what it costs them to use them.
Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis became concrete: anxiety genuinely interferes with language acquisition. A low-stakes, supportive environment is not a courtesy. It is a pedagogical requirement.
The shift between teacher and learner roles in the course — from Portuguese-speaker to English-language participant — was a productive disorientation. Self-expression in a foreign language requires a kind of courage that rarely gets acknowledged in language classrooms.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Meaningful Moments.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Meaningful Moments
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Not everything that matters can be planned. Some of the most significant learning moments of the week were incidental.
The encounter at the statue of Goya outside the Prado — before entering, before context was given — established a different relationship with the visit. Meeting the artist as a figure in the city, not just as a name on a wall.
The Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise. Attention to texture, sweetness, temperature. A lesson in what it means to actually experience something rather than process it.
The frozen Tableau technique produced a specific kind of collective embarrassment and then surprise: the body knows things the mind has not yet articulated.
The Audio Guide as Scaffolding exercise at the Reina Sofía made interpretation active. Choosing what to say about a work, and how, forces a kind of accountability to the work itself.
Walking alone in Madrid on a free afternoon — unscheduled, without group obligation — was its own pedagogy. Solitary movement through a city is a different kind of attention.
The connection during the film Metropolis, the late evening that emerged unexpectedly around shared listening, belongs to the part of professional development that no report ever captures.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Museums as Learning Spaces.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Museums as Learning Spaces
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A museum is not a repository. Treated properly, it is a classroom that happens to be full of evidence.
The difference between a visit and a lesson is preparation. At the Centro de Arte Oliva, the route was walked in advance, the questions were designed before the students arrived, and a guided tour became something closer to an inquiry: what do you think this means, asked once at the entrance and again at the exit, an hour apart, with the answers changing completely in between. That single repeated question did more pedagogical work than any worksheet could have. Context4Content names the principle; the museum supplied the proof.
This is place-based learning applied to an institution built for exactly this purpose. Museums already curate, sequence and juxtapose — the raw material of a lesson is done before a teacher arrives. What a teacher adds is the question that turns a room of objects into an argument, and the follow-up that turns a good answer into a better one.
Visual learning runs underneath all of it. A painting, a photograph, an installation: each demands a kind of attention that text does not, slower, more circular, willing to sit with ambiguity before resolving it. Museums as learning spaces work because they insist on that slowness, in an educational culture that increasingly does not.

Part of Reflections. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Professional Development.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Professional Development
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Professional Development that works is not about receiving information. It is about being placed in conditions where your existing assumptions are gently but persistently tested.
This course did that. Not through confrontation, but through design: every session modelled the methodology it described. You experienced Cooperative Learning while learning about Cooperative Learning. You practised Scaffolding while discussing its theory. The course was its own argument.
The most lasting professional shifts from this week are likely to be the ones that are hardest to name: a slightly different instinct for how to open a session, a more conscious attention to the emotional temperature of a room, a new suspicion toward transmissive teaching.
See Reflective Practice, dissemination and Notes on Unlearning.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Reflective Practice.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Reflective Practice
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The deliberate habit of using experience as a source of professional learning — not simply accumulating experience, but interrogating it.

The Distinction
A teacher with twenty years of experience has twenty years of accumulated practice. That is not the same as twenty years of professional learning. Experience without reflection tends to confirm existing habits rather than develop them.
Reflective practice is the professional discipline of asking: what happened, why did it happen, what does it mean, and what would I do differently? Honestly. Repeatedly. Over time.

The Frameworks
Donald Schön’s The Reflective Practitioner (1983) distinguished between reflection-in-action (adjusting in real time, mid-lesson) and reflection-on-action (deliberate review after the fact). Both are necessary; they develop different things. See Reflective Practice for the framework in full.
John Dewey’s earlier argument is foundational: experience alone does not produce learning. Experience plus reflection does.

Reflective Writing
The Learning Portfolios used during the course was a structured instrument for reflective practice: not a record of what happened, but an attempt to understand what it meant.
Writing is not the transcription of reflection. It is one of the mechanisms through which reflection happens. The act of putting experience into language forces a level of clarity that thinking alone does not require.

“La pluma es la lengua del alma.” — Cervantes


The Professional Stakes
In 2026, reflective practice is not optional. The rate of change in pedagogy, technology, and the social contexts of education means that practitioners who do not reflect systematically fall behind — not in information, but in professional judgement.
Reflective practice is also a form of dissemination: what you understand about your own practice, documented and shared, contributes to professional culture. See Micro-dissemination.

Connections in the Course
See Learning Portfolios, Fiat Humanitas, Curation, Professional Engagement, and Notes on Professional Development.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Notes on Unlearning.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Notes on Unlearning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
One of the questions posed on Day 3 was: How much unlearning is necessary?
The question is stronger than it first appears. It assumes that learning new approaches is not simply additive — that some things need to be set aside before new things can take root.
The practices most in need of unlearning are often the ones most deeply habituated: the instinct to fill silence, the assumption that explanation equals understanding, the reflex to simplify rather than scaffold, the habit of designing for the average learner rather than for actual learners.
Unlearning is uncomfortable in a specific way. It is not the discomfort of not knowing. It is the discomfort of suspecting that what you already know may be getting in the way.
The week in Madrid did not resolve this. It sharpened it.
See Reflective Practice, Active Methodologies and Notes on Professional Development.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Reflective Practice.md

See Learning Portfolios, Fiat Humanitas, Curation, Professional Engagement, and Notes on Professional Development.
