Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/Sensory overload.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Sensory Overload
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A condition in which the volume or intensity of sensory input exceeds a person’s capacity to process it comfortably. Common in learners with sensory processing differences, autism spectrum conditions, ADHD, anxiety disorders, and PTSD — but experienced in milder forms by most people in certain environments.

What It Looks Like
A learner experiencing sensory overload may: withdraw or become unresponsive, appear distracted or agitated, cover ears or eyes, leave the space without explanation, struggle to follow instructions, or appear to refuse participation.
These responses are not defiance. They are the nervous system’s attempt to manage input it cannot process.

Common Triggers in Learning Environments
Fluorescent lighting with flicker. Background noise — HVAC systems, corridor noise, multiple conversations. Strong or unfamiliar smells. Crowded or visually cluttered spaces. Unpredictable physical contact. Unexpected loud sounds.
Museum environments — with their hard floors, echoing rooms, crowds, and unfamiliar sensory conditions — can be challenging spaces for learners with sensory sensitivity.

Practical Design Responses
Reducing visual clutter in classroom and display design. Providing advance notice of environmental changes. Offering a quiet or lower-stimulus space as an opt-out option. Using noise-reducing earphones or earplugs as a standard resource. Allowing movement and regulation breaks. Dimming or adjusting lighting where possible.
These adjustments cost little and benefit the learner population broadly.

Connections in the Course
Sensory overload connects to Universal Design for Learning (reducing barriers by design), Why use a multi-sensory approach? (the distinction between rich multisensory design and overwhelming input), Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis (overload raises the filter), and the week’s use of mindfulness techniques as regulatory tools.
See 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding Exercise and Marzipan Time — A Mindfulness Exercise.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/The City as Classroom.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The City as Classroom
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Madrid was never just the backdrop to the course. It was the syllabus.
A peddypaper through the streets of the centre turned a public holiday into a question: what happened here, on this square, on this balcony, on the 2nd of May 1808? The city answered before any teacher did. A plaque became a primary source. A statue became an introduction. Place-based learning does not mean taking the classroom outside; it means admitting that the street was already teaching, whether or not a lesson plan said so.
Toledo pushed the same idea further. Judaism, Islam and Christianity did not illustrate coexistence there — the stone did. Synagogue, mosque and cathedral built on top of each other across centuries, without erasing what came before, made the abstract idea of layered history something you could put a hand on.
Casa de Campo and El Rastro offered a quieter version of the same lesson: a park and a market are not neutral spaces. They carry use, memory and negotiation, and a walk through either can be designed with the same intention as a classroom activity, if the teacher is willing to treat the city as a text.
None of this replaces the classroom. It relocates the question. Instead of asking what a text says, the city asks what a place has witnessed — and whether the people walking through it are still willing to notice.

Part of Reflections. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/What is truth in 2026?.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: What is truth in 2026?
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A question posed during the week. Not rhetorical. Genuinely open.

The Starting Point

“The great enemy of the truth is very often not the lie — deliberate, contrived and dishonest — but the myth — persistent, persuasive and unrealistic.” — John F. Kennedy, 1962

Kennedy’s distinction is more useful now than when it was made. The problem of 2026 is not primarily deliberate deception — it is the ecosystem of plausible myth: content that feels true, circulates as true, and is almost impossible to verify or refute at the speed it moves.

The New Conditions
Generative AI produces text, image, audio, and video that is indistinguishable from human-made content, at scale, in seconds. Algorithmic curation shows each person a version of the world calibrated to their existing beliefs. Viral circulation rewards emotional resonance over evidential quality.
The result: information literacy — always important — has become a survival skill.

Two More Voices

“One child, one teacher, one book and one pen can change the world.” — Malala Yousafzai

The access problem remains. Who gets to be information-literate? Whose communities have the tools, the time, and the education to navigate this landscape? Digital literacy is not equally distributed.

“In a world of propaganda, the answer is not counter-propaganda. The answer is education.” — Eduardo Galeano

The response to misinformation is not better misinformation. It is the long, unglamorous work of building critical capacity in learners — one classroom at a time.

The Teacher’s Position
Teachers in 2026 operate in an information environment that did not exist when most were trained. Navigating it requires not only digital skills but epistemological honesty: the willingness to say “we don’t know yet,” “this requires verification,” and “here is how we check.”
That is not weakness. That is the intellectual integrity the situation demands.

Connections in the Course
See AI Literacy, Healthy scepticism, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies, Las Meninas, and Future of Jobs Report 2025.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Reflections/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Reflections
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Reflections
The sessions ended. The thinking continued.
These notes are not summaries of what was learned. They are the places where the week kept working — in corridors and parks and paintings, in questions that arrived after the fact, in the gap between what was said and what it meant. Some are short. Some are essays. Fiat Humanitas is the longest, written after the Reina Sofía, after everything.
The register here is different from the programme notes or the framework references. Less explanatory. More willing to stay with difficulty.

On the Educator

Notes on Professional Development: what professional growth looks like when it is honest about its costs
Notes on Unlearning: every new framework implies letting go of an older, less adequate one — this is uncomfortable in ways the sessions did not fully resolve
Reflective Practice: reflection as professional competence, not afterthought — the habit the week kept returning to
Notes on Reflective Practice: the personal register of the same habit — writing as one of the mechanisms through which reflection actually happens
From teachers to teachers: the horizontal dimension — no hierarchy, no stage, a community of practice that moves across borders


On Culture and Place

Cultural Understanding: a practice of sustained attention, not a destination
Notes on Meaningful Moments: the moments the programme did not plan and could not have
Sensory overload: what happens when the week is too full, and why that fullness is itself a pedagogical argument
Notes on Language Development: what BICS and CALP look like from the inside — being a learner in a language you are also teaching
The City as Classroom: Madrid, Toledo, Casa de Campo and El Rastro as syllabus, not backdrop
Learning Landscapes: what a landscape keeps after the learning has moved on, and why this garden is named for it
Notes on Art as a Technology for Interpretation: art as an instrument for reading, not a subject to admire
Notes on Museums as Learning Spaces: the Prado, the Reina Sofía and Centro de Arte Oliva as classrooms built out of curated evidence


On Europe and Civic Responsibility

Fiat Europa: make Europe — not as declaration but as daily practice, in every classroom, in every opportunity
Notes on European Values: nine countries, one room, and what unites people when no flag does
What is truth in 2026?: the question posed on the last day. Not rhetorical. Genuinely open.
Healthy scepticism: the educator who teaches students to question evidence without becoming cynical — structurally necessary, not supplementary


Extended Essays

Fiat Humanitas: the week’s final reflection — Goya, Picasso, Dumile Feni, Mohammed Al-Hawajri, and what art asks of us when coexistence breaks down. The benchmark for this garden’s tone and register.
Curation: the educator as curator — what it means to select, sequence, contextualise and let go


Part of Footscapes. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/AI for Teaching.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: AI for Teaching
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A station rotation session exploring AI tools for educational use. Participants moved through digital stations, each focused on a different tool and application.

The Stations
Mentimeter — real-time audience engagement and polling. Live word clouds, Q&A, and quiz formats. Used for formative assessment and activating prior knowledge.
NotebookLM — Google’s AI-powered research assistant. Ingests documents and generates summaries, questions, and study guides from uploaded materials.
Curipod — AI-assisted slide and lesson generator. Creates interactive lessons from a prompt, including discussion questions and activities.
Padlet Arcade — collaborative digital wall with gamified elements. Used for brainstorming, sharing resources, and structured group responses.
WebWise — digital literacy and online safety resource, particularly focused on younger learners and vulnerable populations.
Genially — interactive content creation: presentations, infographics, escape rooms, and multimedia learning objects.
Canva — graphic design platform with strong education features including AI image generation and presentation tools.
Book Creator — multimodal digital book creation. Learners produce books combining text, image, audio, and video.

The Station Rotation Model
The session used a station rotation structure: groups rotated through each tool in timed intervals, with a facilitator at each station to support exploration and provoke reflection.
The model itself is a demonstration of Active Methodologies and The SAMR Model: the technology is not supplementary — it restructures the learning environment.

Connections in the Course
See AI Literacy, The SAMR Model, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies, and Digital Resources.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/Assessment.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Assessment
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
From the DigCompEdu framework, Area 4: Assessment. Three competency areas for using digital tools to support and improve assessment practice.

Assessment Strategies
Using digital technologies to diversify and enrich assessment approaches — moving beyond written tests toward multimodal, collaborative, and formative assessment designs.
Digital tools make it possible to assess process, not only product: to capture drafts, iterations, reflections, and collaborative contributions that a final test cannot reach.

Analysing Evidence
Using digital tools to collect, analyse, and interpret evidence of learning — from learning analytics platforms to simple response-gathering tools like Mentimeter or Padlet.
The key shift: assessment as an ongoing, data-informed practice rather than a periodic event. Formative assessment in real time.

Feedback and Planning
Using digital tools to provide timely, personalised, and actionable feedback — and to use assessment data to inform future planning.
The move from evaluation (judgement at the end) to feedback (guidance during) is one of the most consequential shifts in contemporary pedagogy. Digital tools make scalable personalised feedback more achievable.

Connections in the Course
See Empowering Learners, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, Active Methodologies, and AI for Teaching.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/Digital Resources.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Digital Resources
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
From the DigCompEdu framework, Area 2: Digital Resources. Three competency areas for the selection, creation, and management of digital educational materials.

Selecting Digital Resources
Identifying, evaluating, and selecting appropriate digital tools, resources, and content for educational purposes.
The key skill here is critical evaluation: not all digital resources are pedagogically sound, accurately evidenced, or contextually appropriate. Selecting well requires knowing what to look for — and what to reject.

Creating and Modifying Digital Resources
Adapting existing digital content or creating new materials suited to the specific learning context, learner group, and pedagogical goals.
This shifts the teacher from consumer to author — a significant professional and creative move. The resource is no longer generic; it is designed for this group, this moment, this purpose.

Managing, Protecting, and Sharing Digital Resources
Organising digital materials effectively, ensuring compliance with copyright and data protection regulations, and sharing resources within appropriate communities of practice.
The sharing dimension connects to : what teachers make, they can give. Professional culture is built from what practitioners make available to each other.

Connections in the Course
See AI for Teaching, The SAMR Model, Facilitating Learners’ Digital Competence, and Empowering Learners.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/English Matters — KA1 Application Guide.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: English Matters — KA1 Application Guide
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Strategic notes on the Erasmus+ KA1 application process, based on the English Matters programme documentation and the experience of applying for this course.

The Institutional Logic
A KA1 application is not a travel request. It is a professional development proposal that must demonstrate institutional need, pedagogical rationale, and planned impact.
The language of the application is significant: Erasmus+ frames mobility not as individual enrichment but as organisational improvement. The institution applies; the individual participates.

The Core Formula
A strong KA1 application follows a coherent argument across six elements:
Problem — what is the specific gap or need in the institution’s current practice? Not a general aspiration. A concrete, evidenced challenge.
Objective — what specific change in knowledge, practice, or attitude will the mobility produce?
Mobility — why this course, with this provider, in this location? What makes this the right intervention for this problem?
Implementation — how will learning be embedded in institutional practice after the mobility? Who will be involved? What will change?
Dissemination — how will the learning reach beyond the individual participant? What channels, what audiences, what timeline?
Impact — how will the institution know that the mobility produced the intended change? What indicators? What timeframe?

Mobility Is Not Travel
The distinction the programme emphasises consistently: mobility is a professional development intervention with institutional consequences. It is not a cultural visit with a learning component attached.
This distinction shapes every element of the application — and every decision about what to disseminate and how.

Connections in the Course
See Dissemination Strategy, , Disseminare, and European Dimension in Education.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/English Matters.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: English Matters
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
The organisation has been running since 1992, established formally in 1999, and has built partnerships with universities across Spain, Ireland, Iceland, Finland, Sweden, Norway, Portugal and Estonia. Its programmes span CLIL, active methodologies, AI and digital competences, wellbeing and mindfulness, outdoor and context-based learning, and several levels of English for teachers. The network is wide. But the identity it chooses is not institutional. It is relational.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/Future of Jobs Report 2025.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Future of Jobs Report 2025
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
World Economic Forum (WEF), 2025. Annual report projecting labour market transformations over a five-year horizon.

Key Findings Relevant to Education
Top rising skills (2025–2030): AI and big data, technological literacy, creative thinking, resilience and flexibility, systems thinking.
Top declining roles: data entry clerks, bank tellers, administrative assistants, postal workers — positions involving routine, rule-based cognitive tasks.
Top growing roles: technology specialists, sustainability officers, care economy professionals, educators and trainers.
The pattern is clear: skills that AI performs well (pattern recognition, information retrieval, rule-based processing) are declining in market value. Skills that AI performs poorly (relational intelligence, ethical judgement, contextual creativity, physical care) are rising.

Manual Dexterity
The report notes that manual dexterity and precision remain high-value skills in contexts where machines cannot reliably substitute: skilled trades, care work, craft, and embodied professional practice.
Vocational education is, in this light, not a second-tier pathway. It develops skills with increasing long-term relevance.

Connections in the Course
The report provides the labour market evidence for 21st Century Skills and grounds the week’s focus on AI Literacy in a concrete professional context. The question is not whether AI will change work — it is what teachers need to develop in learners so that change is navigable.
See What is truth in 2026?, UNESCO Framework for AI Literacies, and OECD AI Literacy Framework.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/Hidden Potential — Irish Short Film.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Hidden Potential — Irish Short Film
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Directed by Eamonn MacMahon, 2016. Ireland. Short drama film.

What It Shows
A dramatisation of a child’s experience of undiagnosed dyslexia in a school environment not designed to accommodate it. The film follows the gap between the child’s actual capacity and what the school is able to see — and the consequences of that gap for the child’s sense of self.

The Pedagogical Argument
The film’s title carries the argument: the potential is present. The problem is in the system’s ability to perceive and respond to it.
This connects directly to Universal Design for Learning — the principle that barriers to learning are most often environmental and structural, not inherent to the learner. When the environment changes, so does what the learner can do.

In the Course
Used as a case study in the discussion of inclusive design and Sensory overload — the ways in which standard classroom environments can be systematically hostile to certain learners, not through malice but through the accumulated assumptions of a design that was never built for them.

Connections in the Course
See Universal Design for Learning, Sensory overload, Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis, The Arrival, and the broader discussion of equity vs. equality in the design of learning environments.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/Scaffolding (Seamus Heaney).md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Scaffolding
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Seamus Heaney, from Door into the Dark, 1969.

Masons, when they start upon a building,
Are careful to test out the scaffolding;
Make sure that planks won’t slip at busy points,
Secure all ladders, tighten bolted joints.
And yet all this comes down when the job’s done
Flung on a truck, jobbed off to be used again.
So if, my dear, there’s something at a loss
Between our two loves, the loss is gain.
Build it all up again, and when the structure’s raised,
Take it down and start again.

The Pedagogical Reading
Heaney’s poem carries a double metaphor. The explicit subject is a love relationship — built up, taken down, rebuilt. But the poem is equally a description of good pedagogical practice.
Scaffolding is temporary by design. It is not the building — it is what makes the building possible. The goal of every scaffold is its own removal: when the learner no longer needs the support, the scaffold has succeeded.
The poem reminds us that this removal is not a loss. It is the moment the structure stands on its own.

Connections in the Course
See Scaffolding, Vygotsky — Zone of Proximal Development, Audio Guide as Scaffolding, Estratégias de Scaffolding para CLIL e Alunos Multilingues, and Seamus Heaney.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/Seamus Heaney.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Seamus Heaney
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
1939–2013. Irish poet. Born in County Derry, Northern Ireland, into a Catholic farming family. Educated at Queen’s University Belfast. Later taught at Harvard and Oxford. Nobel Prize in Literature, 1995.

Heaney’s poetry is grounded in the physical world — soil, tools, weather, labour — and attentive to the weight of history in ordinary places. His work carries the landscape of rural Ireland, the divisions of the North, and the inheritance of a literary tradition he engaged with as both heir and critic.
He received the Nobel Prize “for works of lyrical beauty and ethical depth, which exalt everyday miracles and the living past.”

The poem Scaffolding (Seamus Heaney) uses the image of a builder’s scaffold as a metaphor for the provisional structures that make lasting things possible.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/The Arrival.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Arrival
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Shaun Tan, 2006. Wordless graphic novel.

What It Is
A wordless graphic novel following a man who emigrates to an imagined foreign country — a place with an invented script, unfamiliar fauna, surreal architecture, and social conventions he cannot read. The experience of arrival is rendered without text: the reader inhabits the same disorientation the protagonist feels.
Because the language is invented, no reader is a native speaker. Everyone is equally at a loss.

The Pedagogical Argument
The Arrival is one of the most effective tools available for developing empathy toward the experience of linguistic and cultural displacement.
A learner who has never been a migrant, never felt the specific humiliation of not understanding a sign, a gesture, or a social norm — that learner experiences, in twenty minutes of careful reading, a version of what millions of students carry into classrooms every day.

Multimodal and Wordless
The absence of text is not a simplification — it is the point. Meaning is carried entirely through image, composition, facial expression, and visual narrative. This makes the book accessible across language levels and simultaneously demonstrates the expressive power of visual literacy.
It also makes it ideal for CLIL contexts: the content is rich; the language barrier is removed.

Connections in the Course
See Universal Design for Learning, CLIL, Stephen Krashen — The Affective Filter Hypothesis, Hidden Potential — Irish Short Film, and the broader discussion of who our classrooms are designed for.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/Resources/The Multiple Intelligence Quiz.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: The Multiple Intelligence Quiz
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
A self-assessment tool based on Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences’s framework. Eight categories, each assessed through a set of preference and behaviour statements.

The Eight Categories
Verbal-Linguistic — I enjoy reading, writing, storytelling, and word games. Language is my primary medium.
Logical-Mathematical — I think in patterns and systems. I enjoy puzzles, reasoning, and working with numbers or abstract structures.
Visual-Spatial — I think in images. I am good with maps, diagrams, design, and remembering where things are.
Interpersonal — I understand people easily. I work well in groups, read emotions accurately, and learn from social interaction.
Musical-Rhythmic — I am sensitive to pitch, rhythm, and melody. Music helps me think or focus.
Naturalistic — I notice patterns in the natural world. I am drawn to animals, plants, ecology, and outdoor environments.
Bodily-Kinesthetic — I learn by doing. I process information through movement, touch, and physical experience.
Intrapersonal — I understand my own thinking and feelings. I prefer working independently and need time for reflection.

How to Use It
Participants complete the statements, tally their scores per category, and identify their strongest profiles. The debrief question is more important than the results: What does this tell you about how you learn — and how you teach?
The quiz is an entry point for discussion, not a diagnostic tool. It opens reflection on learning preferences, teaching style, and the assumptions embedded in classroom design.

Connections in the Course
See Howard Gardner — Multiple Intelligences, Universal Design for Learning, 21st Century Skills, and Active Methodologies.

Part of Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning. LearningLandscapes

Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.filePath: Learning/Active Methodologies - Teaching and Learning/index.md
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.title: Innovative and Active Methodologies for Teaching and Learning
Learning/Active-Methodologies---Teaching.content:
Erasmus+ KA1 mobility. English Matters, Madrid and Toledo, May 2026.

“Every child is an artist. The problem is how to remain an artist once we grow up.” — Picasso


Programme — the week, day by day
Frameworks — the methodologies, techniques and theorists
Museums & Visual Culture — art as methodology, not illustration
Reflections — where the week kept thinking after the sessions ended
Field Learning Projects — the city as curriculum
Dissemination — what to do with what you learned

This course didn’t teach active methodologies. It enacted them.
Seven days. A city. Fourteen paintings. One question that kept returning: what does learning look like when the room is designed well?



                  
                  By the numbers 
                  
                

Porto to Madrid and back, by bus. 155,859 steps. 103 km walked. 7 days. 40 training hours.

index.content:
A digital garden is not a website. It is a practice.

Preface
These notes grew from movement. From classrooms that became museums and streets. From frameworks tested against real contexts. From the gap between what we teach and what we learn.
Footscapes is a record of that movement. Not a report. A garden: some notes fully formed, others still seeds. The gardener is not the authority. The gardener is the curator. See notes on Process.

If you are drawn to art and culture
Start with Guernica or Fiat Humanitas.
From there: Goya, Las Meninas, Prado, Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofia.
If you are drawn to pedagogy and learning design
Start with Scaffolding or The Four Zones of Teaching and Learning.
From there: Cooperative Learning, CLIL, Universal Design for Learning.
If you arrived here through Erasmus+
Start with European Dimension in Education or Disseminare.
From there: Dissemination Strategy, From teachers to teachers.
If you work in learning and development
Start with Transforma-te Transforma or On Language Traps.
From there: Frameworks, Learning Experience Design, Fiat Humanitas.
